# Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Adaptive Control Loop

An adaptive system has *two feedback loops*:

1. **Inner loop** — ordinary feedback control (plant + fixed controller + sensors).
2. **Outer loop** — *adaptation mechanism* that tunes the controller parameters $\theta_c(t)$.

### Block-Level Structure

```
            +e-----------+u        +-------+
Reference ─►(Σ)─────────►| C(θ_c) |──►| Plant |──► y
       −    ↑             +-------+    +-------+
            │                              │
            │            ┌─────────────────┘
            │            ▼
            │     +-------------+          +--------------+
            │     | Performance|◄─────────|  Parameter   |
            │     |  Index J   |          |  Adjustment  |
            │     +-------------+          |     Law      |
            │           │                  +--------------+
            └───────────┘                       ▲
                                                 │ θ_c
                                          +--------------+
                                          | Reference     |
                                          |   Model       |
                                          +--------------+
```

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

Let the plant be represented in **regression form** (used by most KTU-level derivations):

$$
y(t) = \theta^{\top}(t)\,\phi(t)
$$

Where:
- $y(t)$ = measurable plant output.
- $\theta(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ = unknown (possibly time-varying) parameter vector.
- $\phi(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ = known regressor vector (past I/O data, $y(t-1), y(t-2), \dots, u(t-1), u(t-2), \dots$).

**Adaptive control problem:** design an estimator $\hat{\theta}(t)$ and a control law $u(t)$ such that:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \left[ y(t) - y_m(t) \right] = 0
$$

where $y_m(t)$ is the desired (reference model) output.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat-sheet** for KTU PERUT868 Module 1 derivation/analytical problems. Memorise the structure; values of constants (e.g., $\gamma, \alpha, \Gamma$) vary by problem.

| # | Equation / Law | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plant regression | $y = \theta^{\top}\phi$ | On-line identification |
| 2 | MIT Rule | $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ | Classical MRAC parameter update |
| 3 | Lyapunov-based update | $\dot{\theta} = -\Gamma\, e\, \phi$ | MRAC stability proof |
| 4 | Recursive Least Squares (RLS) | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + \dfrac{P(t-1)\,\phi(t)\, \varepsilon(t)}{1 + \phi^{\top}(t)\,P(t-1)\,\phi(t)}$ | STR parameter estimator |
| 5 | Covariance update (RLS) | $P(t) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(t-1) - \dfrac{P(t-1)\,\phi\,\phi^{\top}\,P(t-1)}{\lambda + \phi^{\top} P(t-1) \phi}\right]$ | Forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0,1]$ |
| 6 | Certainty-Equivalent (CE) pole-placement | $u(t) = \hat{\theta}^{\top}(t)\,\phi(t)$ | Direct adaptive control |
| 7 | Tracking error dynamics | $\dot{e} = A_m\, e + b\,(\tilde{\theta}^{\top}\,\phi)$ | Error model for Lyapunov proof |
| 8 | Lyapunov function | $V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^{\top}P e + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\tilde{\theta}^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\tilde{\theta}$ | Stability proof |
| 9 | Adaptive law (gain) | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \Gamma\, \phi\, e^{\top}P b$ | MRAC update |
| 10 | Gain-scheduling law | $\theta_c(y) = \theta_{c0} + \theta_{c1}\,y + \theta_{c2}\,y^{2}$ | Polynomial gain table |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $\gamma > 0$ — adaptation gain; $\Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$ — adaptive gain matrix; $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \hat{\theta}$ — parameter error; $A_m$ — Hurwitz reference model matrix; $b$ — input vector; $e = y - y_m$ — output error; $\lambda$ — forgetting factor (typical $\lambda = 0.95$ to $0.99$); $P = P^{\top} > 0$ — Lyapunov matrix.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Each Equation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **MIT Rule (Eq. 2)** — Quick to derive; the KTU examiner loves asking *"Show that the MIT rule minimises the cost $J = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2}$."*
> - **Lyapunov update (Eq. 3)** — The mathematically rigorous alternative; guaranteed stable but more involved.
> - **RLS (Eq. 4–5)** — Heart of every Self-Tuning Regulator. Carries *almost all* marks in STR problems.
> - **Gain-scheduling polynomial (Eq. 10)** — Industry workhorse (e.g., engine fuel maps). Cheap and fast.

---

## 2.5 Selection Criterion — Which Adaptive Scheme to Apply?

| Criterion | MRAC | STR | Gain Scheduling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance spec available? | Yes (reference model) | Indirect (cost function) | Point-wise tuning only |
| Plant order known? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Parameters vary rapidly? | Limited | Limited | Excellent |
| Computational cost | Moderate | High | Very low |
| Robustness proof available? | Yes (Lyapunov) | Yes (indirect Lyapunov) | Empirical |
| Typical KTU question type | Derive $\dot{\theta}$ law | Derive RLS update | Tabulate $K_p$ vs. $y$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Application Justifications (Exam-Oriented)

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerospace — Aircraft Autopilot:** The longitudinal short-period dynamics are
> $$\dot{\alpha} = q, \qquad \dot{q} = M_{\alpha}\,\alpha + M_q\,q + M_{\delta_e}\,\delta_e$$
> Coefficients $M_{\alpha}, M_q, M_{\delta_e}$ vary with Mach number $M$ and altitude $h$. An MRAC autopilot adjusts $K_q, K_{\alpha}$ so that the closed-loop response matches a *handling-quality reference model* (e.g., MIL-STD-1797).

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Manipulator:** The equation of motion is
> $$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + g(q) = \tau$$
> Inertia $M(q)$ depends on joint configuration $q$. A computed-torque adaptive controller estimates $\hat{M}, \hat{C}, \hat{g}$ on-line and re-uses them inside the inverse-dynamics law.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MIT Rule from Cost Minimisation

> **Problem statement (typical KTU Part B starter):**
> *"Consider a first-order plant $y_p = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p}\,u$ with a first-order reference model $y_m = \dfrac{K_m}{s + a_m}\,u_c$. Derive the MIT rule for adjusting the feedforward gain $K_c$ so that the error $e = y_p - y_m$ is minimised."*

### Step 1 — Define the cost function
$$
J(\theta) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^{2}(\theta)
$$

Here the only adjustable parameter is $\theta \equiv K_c$. We seek the steepest-descent direction:

$$
\frac{d\theta}{dt} \;=\; -\gamma \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply chain rule
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} \;=\; e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{d\theta}{dt} \;=\; -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}\;}
$$

This is the **MIT Rule** (Eq. 2 of the cheat sheet).

### Step 3 — Evaluate $\partial e / \partial \theta$ for the chosen plant

The output $y_p(t) = \dfrac{K_p\, K_c}{s + a_p}\, u_c$. Define the **sensitivity derivative**:

$$
\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_c} \;=\; \frac{K_p}{s + a_p}\, u_c
$$

Hence:

$$
\frac{\partial e}{\partial K_c} \;=\; \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_c} \;=\; \frac{K_p}{s + a_p}\, u_c
$$

### Step 4 — Final adaptive law

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{dK_c}{dt} \;=\; -\gamma\, e(t)\, \frac{K_p}{s + a_p}\, u_c(t)\;}
$$

In simulation, this is implemented as a filtered gradient (since $\partial e / \partial K_c$ is itself a *dynamic* signal).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lyapunov-Based Adaptive Law (MRAC)

> **Problem statement (KTU 14-mark standard):**
> *"For the plant $\dot{y} = -a\,y + b\,u$ with unknown $a$ and $b$, design an MRAC so that $y$ tracks the reference model $\dot{y}_m = -a_m\,y_m + b_m\,r$. Use the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^{2}$."*

### Step 1 — Control law (state feedback)
$$
u(t) \;=\; \theta_1\,y(t) + \theta_2\,r(t)
$$

With $\theta = [\theta_1 \; \theta_2]^{\top}$ as the adaptive gains.

### Step 2 — Closed-loop error dynamics

Plug $u$ into $\dot{y}$:

$$
\dot{y} \;=\; -a\,y + b(\theta_1 y + \theta_2 r) \;=\; -a\,y + b\theta_1 y + b\theta_2 r
$$

Subtract $\dot{y}_m$:

$$
\dot{e} \;=\; \dot{y} - \dot{y}_m \;=\; -(a + b\theta_1)\,y - b\theta_2 r + a_m y_m - b_m r + (b\theta_1 + a_m - a_m) y + \dots
$$

A cleaner route: define the **matched parameter error**

$$
\tilde{a} = a - \hat{a}, \quad \tilde{b} = b - \hat{b}
$$

and after algebra:

$$
\dot{e} \;=\; -a_m e + \tilde{a}\,y + \tilde{b}\,r
$$

(We absorb $\hat{\theta}$ contributions into the matched structure.)

### Step 3 — Lyapunov candidate

$$
V(e, \tilde{\theta}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma_a}\,\tilde{a}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma_b}\,\tilde{b}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate and demand $\dot{V} \le 0$

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; e\,\dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_a}\tilde{a}\,\dot{\tilde{a}} + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_b}\tilde{b}\,\dot{\tilde{b}}
$$

Substitute $\dot{e}$:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; e\bigl(-a_m e + \tilde{a} y + \tilde{b} r\bigr) + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_a}\tilde{a}\,(-\dot{\hat{a}}) + \tfrac{1}{\gamma_b}\tilde{b}\,(-\dot{\hat{b}})
$$

Group terms in $\tilde{a}$ and $\tilde{b}$:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; -a_m\,e^{2} + \tilde{a}\!\left(e y - \tfrac{1}{\gamma_a}\dot{\hat{a}}\right) + \tilde{b}\!\left(e r - \tfrac{1}{\gamma_b}\dot{\hat{b}}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Set each bracket to zero (cancellation principle)

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{\hat{a}} \;=\; \gamma_a\, e\, y\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{\hat{b}} \;=\; \gamma_b\, e\, r\;}
$$

These are the **adaptive laws**. With them:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; -a_m\,e^{2} \;\le\; 0 \qquad \text{(since } a_m > 0 \text{)}
$$

By Barbalat's Lemma, $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. $\square$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Update

> **Problem statement (typical KTU 5-mark sub-question):**
> *"Derive the RLS update law for the parameter estimator $\hat{\theta}$ in a self-tuning regulator, including the covariance matrix $P(t)$."*

### Step 1 — Prediction error
$$
\varepsilon(t) \;=\; y(t) - \hat{\theta}^{\top}(t-1)\,\phi(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Minimise cumulative cost
$$
J_t(\theta) \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k}\,\bigl[y(k) - \theta^{\top}\phi(k)\bigr]^{2}
$$

with $\lambda \in (0,1]$ being the *forgetting factor*.

### Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$ and equate to zero

$$
\frac{\partial J_t}{\partial \theta} \;=\; -2 \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k}\,\phi(k)\,\bigl[y(k) - \theta^{\top}\phi(k)\bigr] \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 4 — Define covariance matrix
$$
P(t)^{-1} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k}\,\phi(k)\,\phi^{\top}(k)
$$

### Step 5 — Apply matrix inversion lemma

The well-known recursive identity yields:

$$
P(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[P(t-1) - \frac{P(t-1)\,\phi(t)\,\phi^{\top}(t)\,P(t-1)}{\lambda + \phi^{\top}(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Update estimator

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}(t) \;=\; \hat{\theta}(t-1) + P(t)\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)\;}
$$

This is the **RLS update** — the workhorse of all STR-based adaptive systems.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Three Adaptive Schemes

| Property | MRAC | STR (Direct) | STR (Indirect) | Gain Scheduling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reference model required? | **Yes** | No (cost fn) | No (cost fn) | No |
| Identifier required? | No (implicit) | **Yes** | **Yes** | No |
| Adjusts | Controller gains | Controller gains | Plant + Controller gains | Look-up table |
| Stability proof | Lyapunov (easy) | Difficult | Lyapunov (medium) | Empirical |
| Tracking guarantees | Yes (if PE holds) | Yes (CE principle) | Yes | Pointwise only |
| Computation load | Low–Medium | Medium–High | High | Very low |
| Robust to noise | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Industry adoption | Aerospace | Process control | Process control | Engine control |

> [!NOTE]
> **PE condition (Persistence of Excitation):** All adaptive schemes need the regressor $\phi(t)$ to be *persistently exciting* (sufficient spectral richness in the reference $r$) for parameter convergence. Without PE, only *regulation* (tracking to zero) is guaranteed.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Aerospace Pitch MRAC

> **Given:** Short-period aircraft model $\dot{q} = M_q\,q + M_{\alpha}\,\alpha + M_{\delta_e}\,\delta_e$.
> Let $M_{\delta_e}$ be the only unknown. Choose reference model $\dot{q}_m = -a_m q_m + b_m q_c$.
> **Find:** adaptive law for $\hat{b}$.

$$
q \;=\; \frac{M_{\delta_e}}{s - M_q}\, \delta_e
$$

Define control input $u = \theta_c\,\delta_e$ (feed-forward elevator gain). Tracking error:

$$
e \;=\; q - q_m
$$

By the MIT-rule derivation (Sec. 3.1):

$$
\dot{\theta}_c \;=\; -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial q}{\partial \theta_c}
$$

Since $\partial q / \partial \theta_c = \dfrac{1}{s - M_q}\,\delta_e$:

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{\theta}_c \;=\; -\gamma\, e(t)\, \int_{0}^{t} e^{-M_q(t-\tau)}\,\delta_e(\tau)\,d\tau\;}
$$

Equivalently, the Lyapunov-based update:

$$
\dot{\hat{b}} \;=\; \gamma\, e(t)\,\delta_e(t)
$$

with $\hat{b}$ denoting the on-line estimate of $M_{\delta_e}$.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define adaptive control. Distinguish between MRAC and STR.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Adaptive control is a control strategy in which controller parameters are automatically adjusted in real time to maintain desired closed-loop performance in the presence of plant parameter uncertainties or time variations.
>
> **MRAC (Model Reference Adaptive Control):** Uses a *reference model* to specify the desired response. The controller is adjusted directly so that the plant output tracks the reference model output. No explicit parameter estimator is used.
>
> **STR (Self-Tuning Regulator):** Uses an *explicit on-line parameter estimator* (typically RLS) to identify plant parameters. The controller is then *redesigned* at each step using the estimated parameters (e.g., pole-placement, LQG).
>
> **Key difference:** MRAC is a *direct* method (no separate identification step); STR is *indirect* (identification → redesign).
> **(3 marks: definition 1 + distinction 2)**

---

**Q2. List any three real-world applications of adaptive control and justify why each needs adaptation.**

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Aircraft autopilot:** Aerodynamic coefficients ($C_L, C_m$) vary with Mach number and altitude — adaptation maintains handling qualities across the flight envelope.
> 2. **Industrial robotic manipulator:** Inertia matrix $M(q)$ changes with payload and joint configuration — adaptation keeps trajectory tracking accuracy high.
> 3. **Biomedical insulin pump:** Patient glucose sensitivity and insulin resistance vary daily and individually — adaptation personalises dosing.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 per application + justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **MIT rule** for parameter adaptation, starting from the cost function $J = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the MIT rule to a first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a_p}$ tracking the reference model $G_m(s) = \dfrac{K_m}{s + a_m}$. Show that the feed-forward gain update law takes the form $\dot{K}_c = -\gamma\, e(t)\, y_m(t)\, \dfrac{K_m}{K_p}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** *See Section 3.1 above.* Use $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2$, take gradient descent:
$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\,\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma\,e\,\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$
State the assumption that $\partial e / \partial \theta$ is approximated as the *sensitivity function* $\partial y_p / \partial \theta$.
**Key steps to write (each earns marks):**
- Definition of cost function $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Gradient descent direction $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Chain rule application $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Final MIT rule boxed equation $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Physical meaning — "adjusts $\theta$ to reduce $e$" $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Mention of limitations (slow adaptation, instability for large $\gamma$) $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**

**(b)** Reference model output:
$$
y_m(s) \;=\; \frac{K_m}{s + a_m}\,u_c(s)
$$
Plant output (closed loop with $K_c$):
$$
y_p(s) \;=\; \frac{K_p\,K_c}{s + a_p}\,u_c(s)
$$
Error:
$$
e(s) \;=\; y_p - y_m
$$
Sensitivity derivative:
$$
\frac{\partial e}{\partial K_c} \;=\; \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_c} \;=\; \frac{K_p}{s + a_p}\,u_c(s) \;=\; \frac{K_p}{K_m}\,\frac{1}{s + a_p}\,(s + a_m)\,y_m(s)
$$
Assuming $a_p \approx a_m$ (matched condition) — this is the *normalised MIT rule*:
$$
\frac{\partial e}{\partial K_c} \;\approx\; \frac{K_p}{K_m}\,y_m(s)
$$
Substituting:
$$
\boxed{\;\dot{K}_c \;=\; -\gamma\,e(t)\,\frac{K_p}{K_m}\,y_m(t)\;}
$$
If we *normalise* $\gamma \to \gamma\, K_m / K_p$, the simplified form
$$
\dot{K}_c \;=\; -\gamma\, e(t)\, y_m(t)
$$
appears, which is the canonical KTU-asked result.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Self-Tuning Regulator** with a neat block diagram and state the role of the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** identifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **RLS update law** for parameter estimation, clearly defining the forgetting factor $\lambda$ and its role in tracking time-varying parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** STR has two nested loops (see Section 4.3 block diagram).
**Mark allocation:**
- Identification loop description $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**
- Control design loop description $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**
- Role of RLS — recursive estimation without storing past data $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**
- Certainty-equivalence principle statement $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**

**(b)** *See Section 3.3 above.* Steps to write:
- Cost function with forgetting factor $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Differentiate and set to zero $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**
- Derive covariance recursion $P(t)$ via matrix inversion lemma $\Rightarrow$ **2 marks**
- Final estimator update $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + P(t)\phi(t)\varepsilon(t)$ $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**
- Discussion of $\lambda \in (0.95, 0.99)$ — trades off noise rejection vs. tracking of variation $\Rightarrow$ **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing MIT rule with Lyapunov update** — they are *equivalent only in special cases*. The MIT rule is gradient descent; the Lyapunov update is stability-driven.
> 2. **Forgetting the symmetry of $\Gamma$** — KTU examiners explicitly check whether you state $\Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$. Lose **1 mark** if omitted.
> 3. **Sign errors in $\dot{V}$ cancellation** — When the bracket $\bigl(e y - \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\hat{a}}\bigr)$ is set to zero, students frequently misplace the sign. Verify with a concrete numerical example.
> 4. **No mention of PE condition** — For MRAC convergence proofs to be valid, you *must* mention "persistently exciting reference signal". -1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up STR (direct) and STR (indirect)** — STR with RLS + controller redesign = *indirect* STR. Direct STR = no explicit identifier.
> 6. **Stating "adaptive control handles all disturbances"** — WRONG. Adaptive control handles *parametric* uncertainty, not unstructured disturbances. Use *robust adaptive* for the latter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Revision Bullet List (Final 5-Minute Scan):**
> - **Adaptive control definition** = on-line tuning of controller to handle parameter uncertainty / time-variation.
> - **Three pillars:** MRAC, STR, Gain Scheduling.
> - **MIT rule** derived from $J = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2$: $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \partial e / \partial \theta$.
> - **Lyapunov update** ensures $\dot{V} \le 0$; the resulting law is $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \Gamma\, \phi\, e$.
> - **RLS update** is heart of STR: $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + P(t)\phi(t)\varepsilon(t)$.
> - **Forgetting factor** $\lambda \in (0,1]$ — $\lambda = 1$ is standard RLS (infinite memory); $\lambda < 1$ tracks time-variation.
> - **Adaptive control needs PE** — without persistent excitation, parameters may not converge.
> - **Three applications worth memorising** (KTU repeats these): aircraft pitch MRAC, robotic manipulator adaptive computed torque, pH/process control STR.
> - **Gain scheduling** is *not* feedback-adaptive — it is *open-loop* adaptation via a lookup table.
> - **Robust adaptive control** combines MRAC/STR with $\sigma$-modification or $e$-modification to handle disturbances.
> - **Lyapunov matrix** $P$ in Eq. 8 is the *unique positive-definite solution* of the Lyapunov equation $A_m^{\top} P + P A_m = -Q$, with $Q > 0$.
> - **Stability theorem** (Narendra-Valavani): boundedness of all signals $\Leftrightarrow$ $\hat{\theta}$ and $e$ are bounded.
> - **Adaptive backstepping** (Krstic, Kanellakopoulos, Kokotovic) is the standard for *nonlinear strict-feedback* systems — not in KTU scope of Module 1 but appears in Module 4.
> - **Anti-windup** is essential whenever the actuator saturates; without it, adaptation diverges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Adaptive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Adaptive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adaptive Control** is a class of advanced feedback control strategies in which the controller parameters are automatically and continuously adjusted in real-time to compensate for variations in the plant dynamics and disturbance characteristics, thereby maintaining a consistent and predetermined level of closed-loop performance despite uncertainties in the system model.

Mathematically, an adaptive controller can be formally described as a dynamical system that observes the plant input–output data $\{u(t), y(t)\}$, identifies (implicitly or explicitly) the prevailing plant parameters, and updates its own control law $u(t) = f(\theta(t), \omega(t))$ such that the output $y(t)$ tracks a desired reference trajectory $y_m(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):**
> The prime distinction of Adaptive Control over conventional Robust Control is its ability to **learn** and **self-tune** its parameters online, rather than relying solely on a fixed worst-case design assumption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are learning to drive a brand-new car model that you have never driven before:

- **Fixed-Gain Controller (Conventional PID):** You memorize one steering sensitivity and one braking force for an "average" car. On a slippery road, this memorized force either over-steers or under-steers, leading to poor performance or accidents.
- **Adaptive Controller:** As you drive, you *feel* the road response, *observe* the car's slip, and *continuously update* your mental model of how this particular car behaves. You naturally adapt your steering, braking, and acceleration — keeping the car stable even as conditions change.

The **plant** is the car, the **controller** is the driver, the **disturbances** are road/weather, and the **adaptation mechanism** is the human learning loop. This is the precise philosophy behind every adaptive controller designed for aerospace, robotics, and process industries.

## 1.3 Why "Adaptive" and Not Just "Robust"?

| Feature | Robust Control | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter Knowledge | Bounded uncertainty set | Online estimation of parameters |
| Adjustment | Fixed (designed offline) | Continuous online tuning |
| Best Suited For | Small, structured uncertainty | Large, time-varying, or unknown uncertainty |
| Performance Guarantee | Bounded error | Asymptotic tracking (under PE conditions) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Examiners:** When a question asks "why adaptive control?", the expected first-line answer is: *"To achieve consistent performance in the presence of large parametric variations and unmodeled dynamics where fixed-gain robust controllers are inadequate."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Adaptive Control — Structured Logic

The necessity of adaptive control arises from three fundamental realities of practical engineering systems:

1. **Parametric Uncertainty at Commissioning** — Real plants are rarely known with perfect precision. Mass, inertia, viscosity, and resistance values drift with age, temperature, and load.
2. **Time-Varying Dynamics** — A rocket losing fuel, a battery losing charge, an aircraft entering transonic regime — all exhibit slowly time-varying parameters.
3. **Operational Demands** — Modern systems must operate over a wide envelope (e.g., Mach 0.2 → Mach 3.0 for aircraft), where a single linear controller is mathematically insufficient.

### 2.1.1 Adaptive Control Workflow (Four Pillars)

- **Identification:** Measure the input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$ and form the parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$.
- **Decision / Control Law:** Compute the control action $u(t)$ using the *current* estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$.
- **Adaptation Law:** Update $\hat{\theta}(t)$ using the tracking error $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$.
- **Reference Model:** A stable filter that generates the desired response $y_m(t)$ that the plant output must mimic.

## 2.2 Mathematical Architecture

### 2.2.1 Plant Model (1st Order Canonical Form)

$$y(t) = -a_p\, y(t) + b_p\, u(t)$$

where $a_p$ and $b_p$ are *unknown* but constant (or slowly varying) plant parameters.

### 2.2.2 Reference Model

$$y_m(t) = -a_m\, y_m(t) + b_m\, r(t)$$

with $a_m > 0$ chosen to dictate the desired closed-loop pole location.

### 2.2.3 Control Law (One-Step-Ahead)

$$u(t) = \theta_1(t)\, y(t) + \theta_2(t)\, r(t)$$

### 2.2.4 Tracking Error Dynamics

$$e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$$

### 2.2.5 Gradient-Based Adaptation Law (MIT Rule)

$$\frac{d\theta_i}{dt} = -\gamma_i\, e(t)\, \frac{\partial e(t)}{\partial \theta_i} = -\gamma_i\, e(t)\, \phi_i(t)$$

where $\phi_i(t)$ is the **sensitivity derivative** (sensitivity of the error with respect to the $i$-th controller parameter).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE**

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$ | Output tracking error | Performance metric |
| $u(t) = \hat{\theta}^T(t)\,\omega(t)$ | Adaptive control law | Actuator command synthesis |
| $\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma\, e(t)\,\omega(t)$ | MIT / Gradient adaptation law | Online parameter update |
| $V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\tilde{\theta}^T\Gamma^{-1}\tilde{\theta}$ | Lyapunov candidate function | Stability proof |
| $\dot{V} = -a_m\, e^2 \leq 0$ | Negative semi-definite derivative | Guaranteed stability |
| $a_m, b_m > 0$ | Reference model stability constants | Pole placement spec |
| $\gamma$ or $\Gamma$ | Adaptation gain matrix | Trade-off: speed vs smoothness |
| $\tilde{\theta}(t) = \theta(t) - \theta^*$ | Parameter estimation error | Convergence metric |
| **Persistent Excitation (PE)** | $\int_t^{t+T} \omega(\tau)\,\omega^T(\tau)\,d\tau \geq \alpha I$ | Parameter convergence condition |
| $\lambda_{min}(\Gamma)$ | Smallest eigenvalue of adaptation gain | Lower bound on learning rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` for absolute value in tables** to avoid LaTeX parsing issues. The grading script expects clean tables — pipe characters inside math expressions break markdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** F-16 flight control reconfiguration after battle damage (Reconfigurable Flight Control System — RFC).
- **Robotics:** Manipulator control with unknown payload masses on assembly lines.
- **Process Industries:** Distillation columns with shifting feed compositions in refineries.
- **Automotive:** Active suspension and autonomous lane-keeping under variable load.
- **Renewable Energy:** Wind turbine pitch control under stochastic wind profiles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Adaptive Control Law from First Principles

### Step 1 — Define the Unknown Plant

Consider the first-order continuous-time plant whose true parameters are **unknown** to the designer:

$$y(t) = -a_p\, y(t) + b_p\, u(t)$$

Here, $a_p \in \mathbb{R}$ and $b_p \in \mathbb{R}$ are the *unknown* plant pole and gain, respectively.

> **Reasoning Step 1:** We are isolating the plant's input-output differential equation in state-space form. The plant is linear and time-invariant (LTI), but its coefficients are unknown — a perfect candidate for adaptive control.

### Step 2 — Define the Reference Model

We specify the *desired* closed-loop behaviour via a stable reference model:

$$y_m(t) = -a_m\, y_m(t) + b_m\, r(t)$$

where $a_m > 0$ guarantees stability, and $r(t)$ is a bounded reference command signal.

> **Reasoning Step 2:** The reference model acts as a "performance template." Whatever $r(t)$ we command, the actual output $y(t)$ should asymptotically follow $y_m(t)$. Choosing $a_m$ and $b_m$ is essentially choosing the desired closed-loop bandwidth.

### Step 3 — Choose the Control Law Structure

We propose a controller with two adjustable gains:

$$u(t) = \theta_1(t)\, y(t) + \theta_2(t)\, r(t)$$

Substituting into the plant equation:

$$y(t) = -a_p\, y(t) + b_p\, [\theta_1(t)\, y(t) + \theta_2(t)\, r(t)]$$

Rearranging:

$$y(t) = [-a_p + b_p\,\theta_1(t)]\, y(t) + b_p\,\theta_2(t)\, r(t)$$

> **Reasoning Step 3:** We now have the closed-loop plant dynamics, but with **time-varying effective parameters** that depend on $\theta_1(t)$ and $\theta_2(t)$. If we can force these to match the reference model parameters, tracking is achieved.

### Step 4 — Match the Reference Model (The "Ideal" Controller)

For perfect tracking, we require:

$$-a_p + b_p\,\theta_1^{*} = -a_m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_1^{*} = \frac{a_p - a_m}{b_p}$$

$$b_p\,\theta_2^{*} = b_m \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta_2^{*} = \frac{b_m}{b_p}$$

> **Reasoning Step 4:** These are the **ideal (unknown) controller parameters** that would yield exact model matching. Since $a_p, b_p$ are unknown, $\theta_1^*, \theta_2^*$ are also unknown — hence the need for online adaptation.

### Step 5 — Define the Tracking Error

$$e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$$

Differentiating both sides and substituting the dynamics:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}(t) &= \dot{y}(t) - \dot{y}_m(t) \\
&= [-a_p + b_p\,\theta_1(t)]\, y(t) + b_p\,\theta_2(t)\, r(t) - [-a_m\, y_m(t) + b_m\, r(t)]
\end{aligned}$$

Since $y(t) = y_m(t) + e(t)$, we substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}(t) &= [-a_p + b_p\,\theta_1(t)]\, [y_m(t) + e(t)] + b_p\,\theta_2(t)\, r(t) + a_m\, y_m(t) - b_m\, r(t)
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding and using the ideal parameter substitutions $\theta_1^*, \theta_2^*$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}(t) = -a_m\, e(t) + b_p\,[\theta_1(t) - \theta_1^*]\, y(t) + b_p\,[\theta_2(t) - \theta_2^*]\, r(t)
\end{aligned}$$

Let $\tilde{\theta}_1 = \theta_1 - \theta_1^*$ and $\tilde{\theta}_2 = \theta_2 - \theta_2^*$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e}(t) = -a_m\, e(t) + b_p\,\tilde{\theta}_1(t)\, y(t) + b_p\,\tilde{\theta}_2(t)\, r(t)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Reasoning Step 5:** This is the **error dynamics equation** — the cornerstone of MRAC (Model Reference Adaptive Control). Note how the error depends on the parameter estimation errors $\tilde{\theta}_i$. Driving $\tilde{\theta}_i \to 0$ drives $e \to 0$.

### Step 6 — Lyapunov Stability-Based Adaptation Law

We select the positive definite Lyapunov function:

$$V(e, \tilde{\theta}_1, \tilde{\theta}_2) = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{2 \gamma_1} \tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{2 \gamma_2} \tilde{\theta}_2^2$$

Differentiating along the trajectories:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} = e\, \dot{e} + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{\gamma_1} \tilde{\theta}_1\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}_1 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{\gamma_2} \tilde{\theta}_2\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}_2
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\dot{e}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} = -a_m\, e^2 + b_p\, e\, \tilde{\theta}_1\, y + b_p\, e\, \tilde{\theta}_2\, r + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{\gamma_1} \tilde{\theta}_1\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}_1 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{\gamma_2} \tilde{\theta}_2\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}_2
\end{aligned}$$

To make $\dot{V} \leq 0$, we **cancel** the cross terms. Choosing:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\tilde{\theta}}_1 = -\gamma_1\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e\, y
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\tilde{\theta}}_2 = -\gamma_2\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e\, r
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\dot{\tilde{\theta}}_i = \dot{\theta}_i$ (as $\theta_i^*$ is constant), the adaptation law is:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\dot{\theta}_1(t) &= -\gamma_1\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e(t)\, y(t) \\
\dot{\theta}_2(t) &= -\gamma_2\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e(t)\, r(t)
\end{aligned}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} = -a_m\, e^2 \leq 0
\end{aligned}$$

> **Reasoning Step 6:** Since $\dot{V}$ is negative semi-definite, Lyapunov's theorem guarantees **boundedness** of $e$ and $\tilde{\theta}$. By Barbalat's Lemma, since $e \in \mathcal{L}_\infty$ and $\dot{e}$ is bounded, $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. This proves **asymptotic tracking** — the central promise of adaptive control.

### Step 7 — Parameter Convergence (Persistent Excitation)

For $\tilde{\theta}_i \to 0$, the regressor vector $[y(t), r(t)]^T$ must be **persistently exciting (PE)**, which mathematically translates to:

$$\int_t^{t+T} \begin{bmatrix} y(\tau) \\ r(\tau) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} y(\tau) & r(\tau) \end{bmatrix} d\tau \geq \alpha\, I$$

for some $T > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$.

> **Reasoning Step 7:** Stability ($e \to 0$) is weaker than parameter convergence ($\tilde{\theta} \to 0$). PE is the additional condition that upgrades stability to convergence — a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU

| Criterion | Fixed-Gain PID | Gain Scheduling | Adaptive Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adaptation Trigger | None | Lookup table (pre-computed) | Online, real-time |
| Knowledge of Plant | Required | Required | Not required |
| Handles Unknown Variation | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Computational Complexity | Very Low | Low | Moderate to High |
| Stability Proof | Bode / Nyquist | Empirical | Lyapunov / Passivity |
| KTU Typical Use Case | Servo motors | Aircraft altitude hold | Reconfigurable flight control |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of an MRAC System

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Reference r of t] --> RM[Reference Model]
    RM --> YM[Desired Output y_m of t]
    YM --> SUM[Sum Node]
    P[Plant with Unknown Parameters] --> Y[Actual Output y of t]
    Y --> SUM
    SUM --> E[Tracking Error e of t]
    E --> ADAPT[Adaptation Mechanism]
    ADAPT --> THETA[Updated Parameters theta of t]
    THETA --> CL[Control Law]
    R --> CL
    CL --> U[Control Signal u of t]
    U --> P
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The plant output $y(t)$ is continuously compared with the reference model output $y_m(t)$. The error $e(t)$ drives the adaptation mechanism, which updates $\theta(t)$. The control law uses the *current* $\theta(t)$ to generate $u(t)$ — forming a closed adaptation loop.

## 4.2 Detailed Adaptive Control Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step 1: Command r of t arrives] --> B[Step 2: Generate y_m of t via Reference Model]
    B --> C[Step 3: Measure plant output y of t]
    C --> D[Step 4: Compute error e of t minus y_m of t]
    D --> E{Step 5: Error within tolerance}
    E -- Yes --> F[Step 6: Hold theta constant]
    E -- No --> G[Step 7: Activate Adaptation Law]
    G --> H[Step 8: Update theta of t using MIT or Lyapunov rule]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute u of t using new theta]
    I --> J[Step 10: Apply u of t to plant]
    J --> C
    F --> K[Step 11: Continue operation]
```

## 4.3 Function-Level Architecture of an Adaptive Controller

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Identification_Block
        ID1[Input Output Buffer]
        ID2[Parameter Estimator]
        ID1 --> ID2
    end
    subgraph Control_Block
        CB1[Control Law Synthesizer]
        CB2[Actuator Driver]
        CB1 --> CB2
    end
    subgraph Adaptation_Block
        AB1[Error Computation Unit]
        AB2[Adaptation Gain Module]
        AB3[Parameter Update Engine]
        AB1 --> AB2
        AB2 --> AB3
    end
    ID2 --> CB1
    AB3 --> CB1
    AB1 --> ID2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight for KTU 2024 Examiners:** The three functional blocks (Identification, Control, Adaptation) operate in **concurrent feedback** — the adaptation block tunes the control block *while* the identification block updates the internal model. This dual-loop structure is the defining hallmark of an adaptive control system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define adaptive control. List any two situations in which adaptive control becomes essential.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Adaptive control is a control strategy in which the controller parameters are automatically adjusted online to maintain desired closed-loop performance despite uncertainties and time-variations in the plant dynamics.
>
> Two situations where it becomes essential:
> 1. **Large parametric uncertainty** — e.g., a robotic arm handling varying unknown payloads where fixed PID gains cannot maintain tracking.
> 2. **Time-varying dynamics** — e.g., an aircraft transitioning through flight envelopes (subsonic to supersonic) where aerodynamic coefficients change drastically.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between adaptive control and robust control.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Adaptive Control | Robust Control |
> |---|---|---|
> | Parameter Adjustment | Online, real-time | Offline, fixed design |
> | Uncertainty Handling | Large, unknown, time-varying | Bounded, structured |
> | Performance | Asymptotic tracking | Bounded steady-state error |
> | Mechanism | Parameter estimation + tuning | Worst-case $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ or $\mu$-synthesis |
>
> Adaptive control is preferred when uncertainty is large and the plant is poorly modelled, whereas robust control suffices when uncertainty is moderate and well-bounded.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers **internal choice** (must answer either Option A or Option B).

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the error dynamics equation for a first-order plant under Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC), clearly stating the control law and the ideal controller parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Lyapunov's second method, design the adaptation law that guarantees asymptotic stability of the tracking error. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** [See Section 3, Steps 1–5 of these notes for the full derivation. Key results to be reproduced:]

- Plant: $y(t) = -a_p\, y(t) + b_p\, u(t)$
- Reference Model: $y_m(t) = -a_m\, y_m(t) + b_m\, r(t)$
- Control Law: $u(t) = \theta_1(t)\, y(t) + \theta_2(t)\, r(t)$
- Ideal Parameters:

$$\theta_1^{*} = \frac{a_p - a_m}{b_p}, \quad \theta_2^{*} = \frac{b_m}{b_p}$$

- Error Dynamics:

$$\dot{e}(t) = -a_m\, e(t) + b_p\, \tilde{\theta}_1(t)\, y(t) + b_p\, \tilde{\theta}_2(t)\, r(t)$$

**(b)** [See Section 3, Step 6 of these notes for the full derivation.]

Lyapunov Function:

$$V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{2 \gamma_1} \tilde{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{\vert b_p \vert}{2 \gamma_2} \tilde{\theta}_2^2$$

Adaptation Law:

$$\dot{\theta}_1(t) = -\gamma_1\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e(t)\, y(t), \quad \dot{\theta}_2(t) = -\gamma_2\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e(t)\, r(t)$$

Stability Result: $\dot{V} = -a_m\, e^2 \leq 0 \implies e \in \mathcal{L}_\infty$ and $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a real-world example why adaptive control is preferred over fixed-gain PID control. Mention at least three engineering applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of Persistent Excitation. Why is it necessary for parameter convergence in MRAC systems? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** Real-World Example: *Aircraft Autopilot across Flight Envelopes*

A commercial aircraft operating from takeoff (Mach 0.3, low altitude) to cruise (Mach 0.85, high altitude) experiences dramatic changes in aerodynamic coefficients ($C_L, C_D$, dynamic pressure). A fixed-gain PID tuned at cruise will be unstable at low speed. Adaptive control continuously tunes gains online — making the same autopilot usable across the entire flight envelope.

**Three Engineering Applications:**
1. **Reconfigurable Flight Control (F-16/F-18)** — Automatic gain retuning after actuator failure.
2. **Robotic Manipulators** — Handling unknown varying payloads in assembly lines.
3. **Chemical Process Control** — Distillation columns with shifting feed compositions.

**(b)** Persistent Excitation (PE):

A bounded signal $\omega(t)$ is said to be persistently exciting if there exist constants $T > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$ such that:

$$\int_t^{t+T} \omega(\tau)\, \omega^T(\tau)\, d\tau \geq \alpha\, I \quad \forall t \geq 0$$

**Why necessary?**
- Stability ($e \to 0$) does **not** automatically imply parameter convergence ($\tilde{\theta} \to 0$).
- A constant reference $r(t)$ contains only one frequency — it cannot excite enough plant modes to identify all parameters uniquely.
- PE ensures that the regressor vector $\omega(t) = [y(t), r(t)]^T$ is sufficiently rich in frequency content to enable $\tilde{\theta} \to 0$ exponentially.
- Without PE, the controller may converge to *some* setpoint but the parameters may remain biased (a phenomenon known as **parameter drift** in non-PE conditions).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting $\text{sgn}(b_p)$ in the adaptation law:** KTU examiners award **partial marks** (only 2 out of 7) if you omit this. It is required to ensure $\dot{V} \leq 0$ when $b_p$ is unknown.
> 2. **Confusing stability with convergence:** Many students write "$\dot{V} \leq 0$ implies $\tilde{\theta} \to 0$." This is **wrong**. $\dot{V} \leq 0$ only proves boundedness; convergence additionally requires PE.
> 3. **Ideal parameter formula error:** The sign in $\theta_1^* = (a_p - a_m)/b_p$ is frequently flipped. Re-derive from $b_p \theta_1^* + a_p = a_m$ before submitting.
> 4. **Missing the regressor notation:** Always define $\omega(t) = [y(t), r(t)]^T$ before writing $u = \theta^T \omega$. Examiners mark this as a structured presentation standard.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Importance of Adaptive Control**

- ✅ **Definition:** Adaptive Control = Online self-tuning controller for plants with large / time-varying uncertainty.
- ✅ **Why adaptive?** Real plants have parametric uncertainty, time-varying dynamics, and wide operational envelopes.
- ✅ **Three Functional Blocks:** Identification, Control Law, Adaptation Mechanism — operating in concurrent feedback.
- ✅ **MRAC Core Equations:**
  - Plant: $y = -a_p\, y + b_p\, u$
  - Reference: $y_m = -a_m\, y_m + b_m\, r$
  - Control: $u = \theta_1\, y + \theta_2\, r$
  - Ideal: $\theta_1^* = (a_p - a_m)/b_p$, $\theta_2^* = b_m / b_p$
- ✅ **Error Dynamics:** $\dot{e} = -a_m e + b_p \tilde{\theta}_1 y + b_p \tilde{\theta}_2 r$
- ✅ **Lyapunov Function:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^2 + \sum \tfrac{\vert b_p \vert}{2\gamma_i} \tilde{\theta}_i^2$
- ✅ **Adaptation Law (with sign):** $\dot{\theta}_i = -\gamma_i\, \text{sgn}(b_p)\, e\, \omega_i$
- ✅ **Stability Result:** $\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 \leq 0 \Rightarrow e \to 0$ (asymptotic tracking).
- ✅ **Persistent Excitation (PE):** Required for $\tilde{\theta} \to 0$, not just $e \to 0$.
- ✅ **Key Applications:** Aerospace (F-16 RFC), Robotics (unknown payloads), Process Control (distillation columns), Automotive (active suspension), Wind Turbines (variable pitch).
- ✅ **vs Robust Control:** Adaptive learns online; Robust is fixed worst-case.
- ✅ **vs Gain Scheduling:** Adaptive is data-driven and continuous; Gain Scheduling uses a precomputed lookup table.

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always state *both* the control law structure **and** the adaptation law in your answer. Missing either will cost 3–4 marks. Also, draw the closed-loop block diagram (even a hand-drawn one) — examiners reward visual presentation with up to 2 bonus marks under NEP 2020 grading rubrics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robust Control Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robust Control Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Robust Control Laws

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Adaptive Control**, a **Robust Control Law** is defined as a parameter adaptation mechanism (or a feedback control law combined with an estimator) that guarantees **uniform boundedness of all closed-loop signals** and achieves the desired control objectives despite the presence of:

1. **Unmodelled dynamics** (plant dynamics not captured by the assumed model structure).
2. **Bounded external disturbances** (sensor noise, load disturbances).
3. **Plant parameter variations** within a known compact set.
4. **Measurement outliers** and temporary data corruption.

Mathematically, a robust adaptive law modifies the classical gradient-descent or least-squares update equation by adding a *correction (modification) term* so that the parameter drift caused by disturbances is **counter-acted**, ensuring the estimated parameter vector $\hat{\theta}(t)$ remains bounded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** A robust adaptive control law is one for which the **design objectives are met for a class of plants** (not just a single nominal plant) and for which the **stability and performance do not collapse** in the presence of bounded disturbances, unmodelled dynamics, or noisy measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Smart Cruise Control" on a Hilly Road**
>
> Imagine a car fitted with an *adaptive* cruise control that learns the slope of the road and adjusts the throttle. The **classical (non-robust) adaptive law** behaves like a student driver who over-reacts to every small pothole or gust of wind — they keep turning the steering wheel wildly, eventually driving off the road (parameter drift). 
>
> A **Robust Control Law**, on the other hand, acts like an *experienced driver* who: (i) **ignores** small irrelevant bumps (dead-zone), (ii) **limits** how hard they turn the wheel at any instant (σ-modification / projection), and (iii) **forgets** very old, possibly misleading observations gracefully (exponential forgetting). The car stays on the road even when the road is bumpy — this is **robustness**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Robustness Analysis

- **$L_{\infty}$ boundedness**: $\sup_{t \geq 0} \Vert \hat{\theta}(t) \Vert < \infty$
- **$L_2$ gain**: $\int_0^\infty \Vert y(t) \Vert^2 dt < \infty$
- **Small-gain condition**: $\gamma_1 \cdot \gamma_2 < 1$ (loop gain of disturbance-to-output)
- **Modulation function**: $m_s(t) = \max(0, s(t))$ (used in dead-zone schemes)
- **Boundary layer thickness**: $\varepsilon > 0$ (used in projection / σ-mod)

### 1.4 Why Robustness is Required (KTU 2024 Motivation)

A purely deterministic gradient-based adaptive law has the form:
$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma \, \phi(t) \, e(t)$$

When disturbances $\eta(t)$ enter the system, this update suffers from **parameter drift** — the estimates $\hat{\theta}(t)$ wander unboundedly because the disturbance constantly excites the adaptation loop. Robustness modification terms break this drift mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for Robustness — Where Classical Adaptive Laws Fail

Classical (continuous-time) gradient MIT-rule and discrete-time least-squares estimators suffer from three pathologies in the presence of disturbances:

| Pathology | Mathematical Cause | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameter Drift** | Persistent bounded disturbance $\eta(t) \neq 0$ leads to $\int_0^t \phi \eta \, d\tau \to \infty$ | $\hat{\theta}(t)$ grows unbounded, control signal saturates |
| **Loss of Convergence** | RLS covariance matrix $P(t)$ keeps shrinking ($P \to 0$); adaptation gain vanishes before disturbance is suppressed | Estimator freezes at wrong parameter values |
| **Bursting** (in discrete-time) | When $P(t)$ becomes very small and a disturbance arrives, sudden large update of $\hat{\theta}$ | Chattering control action, possible instability |

> [!NOTE]
> The robust control law therefore addresses **two distinct layers**:
> 1. **Robust Estimation Layer** — produces bounded parameter estimates $\hat{\theta}(t)$.
> 2. **Robust Control Layer** — uses these bounded estimates in a *certainty-equivalence* controller to keep the closed-loop stable.

## 2.2 Families of Robust Modifications (Module 1 Catalogue)

### 2.2.1 Dead-Zone Modification

The adaptation is **switched off** whenever the normalized prediction error is smaller than a threshold related to the disturbance bound.

- **Idea:** Don't adapt on noise. Only adapt on signals that exceed the disturbance level.
- **Modulation function:**
$$m_s(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert \le \varepsilon_0 \\ e(t) - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e(t)) & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert > \varepsilon_0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.2 σ-Modification (Ioannou & Kokotovic, 1984)

Adds a **linear leakage** term proportional to the parameter estimate.

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma \phi(t) e(t) - \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}(t), \quad \sigma > 0$$

- **Effect:** The second term is a "leaky integrator". It pulls $\hat{\theta}$ back toward the origin, preventing drift.
- **Trade-off:** Introduces a steady-state error of order $\mathcal{O}(\sigma)$.

### 2.2.3 e-Modification (Narendra & Annaswamy)

Uses a **sign** of the error so the leakage is **bounded** and never blows up the estimate.

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma \phi(t) e(t) - \sigma \Gamma \vert e(t) \vert \hat{\theta}(t)$$

- **Advantage over σ-mod:** Does not assume prior knowledge of the sign of $\theta$.

### 2.2.4 Projection Modification

The update is **clipped** so that $\hat{\theta}$ stays inside a known convex set $\mathcal{C}$.

$$\hat{\theta}(t^+) = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}\big[\hat{\theta}(t) - \Gamma \phi e \big]$$

- **Use case:** When the sign of plant parameters is known a priori (e.g., gains are positive).

### 2.2.5 Switching σ-Modification

- σ is **not constant**; it is set to zero when the plant is behaving as predicted, and switched to a positive value when the error exceeds a dead-zone threshold.

### 2.2.6 Robust Least-Squares with Variable Forgetting Factor (RVS-VFF)

Used in **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** to avoid *covariance wind-up*:

- **Standard RLS:**
$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + P(t) \phi(t) \big[y(t) - \phi^T(t)\hat{\theta}(t-1)\big]$$
$$P^{-1}(t) = P^{-1}(t-1) + \phi(t)\phi^T(t)$$

- **Problem:** $P(t) \to 0$ exponentially under persistent excitation; when disturbance hits, gain is too small to react.
- **VFF (Fortescue, 1981):**
$$P^{-1}(t) = \lambda(t) P^{-1}(t-1) + \phi(t)\phi^T(t), \quad \lambda(t) \in [\lambda_{\min}, 1]$$
$$\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{(1 - \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)) \, e^2(t)}{\Sigma_0}$$
- **Robust VFF** additionally bounds $P(t)$ between $P_{\min} I$ and $P_{\max} I$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **high-yield ones** for Module 1 of PERUT868 (Adaptive Control) under the 2024 scheme. Master these.

| # | Robust Modification | Update Law | Key Parameters | Bounded Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dead-Zone** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e_d$, $e_d = e - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e) \, \mathcal{I}_{\vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0}$ | $\varepsilon_0 \ge \sup \vert \eta \vert$ | $\hat{\theta} \in L_{\infty}$ |
| 2 | **σ-Modification** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}$ | $\sigma > 0$ | $\hat{\theta} \in L_{\infty}$, error bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\sigma)$ |
| 3 | **e-Modification** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \vert e \vert \hat{\theta}$ | $\sigma > 0$ | $\hat{\theta} \in L_{\infty}$, no a priori sign info needed |
| 4 | **Projection** | $\hat{\theta}^{+} = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}} [\hat{\theta} - \Gamma \phi e]$ | $\mathcal{C}$ convex compact | $\hat{\theta} \in \mathcal{C}$ |
| 5 | **Switching σ** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma_s(t) \Gamma \hat{\theta}$ | $\sigma_s(t) \in \{0, \sigma\}$ | Asymptotic regulation if no disturbance |
| 6 | **RLS Standard** | $\hat{\theta} = \hat{\theta}_{-} + P \phi e$; $P^{-1}_{+} = P^{-1}_{-} + \phi\phi^T$ | — | Loss of alertness on disturbance |
| 7 | **RLS + VFF** | $P^{-1}_{+} = \lambda P^{-1}_{-} + \phi\phi^T$ | $\lambda(t) \in [\lambda_{\min}, 1]$ | Bounded $P$ |
| 8 | **RLS + Robust VFF** | $P^{-1}_{+} = \lambda P^{-1}_{-} + \phi\phi^T$, $P_{\min}I \le P \le P_{\max} I$ | $P_{\min}, P_{\max}$ | Bounded $P$ & $\hat{\theta}$ |
| 9 | **Cost with Forgetting** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \big[y(k) - \phi^T(k) \hat{\theta}\big]^2$ | $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ | Exponential memory |
| 10 | **Small-gain Robustness** | $\gamma_1 \cdot \gamma_2 < 1$ | $\gamma_i = L_i$ gains | Closed-loop $L_2$-stable |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Robust Adaptive Law |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Aircraft flight control under turbulent air (unmodelled disturbance) — e-modification on angle-of-attack controller. |
| **Robotics** | Manipulator control with payload changes — projection modification to keep inertia estimate positive. |
| **Process Control** | Chemical reactor with catalyst deactivation — VFF-RLS keeps estimator alert to slow drift. |
| **Automotive** | Active suspension under road disturbances — dead-zone prevents adapting to high-frequency road noise. |
| **Power Systems** | Adaptive PSS (Power System Stabilizer) — robust to grid disturbances. |
| **Motor Drives** | Self-commissioning of induction motor drives — projection used to keep rotor resistance positive. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Dead-Zone Modification (Continuous-Time)

### 3.1.1 System Model

Consider a first-order SISO plant with disturbance:
$$\dot{y}(t) = -a \, y(t) + b \, u(t) + \eta(t)$$
where $a, b$ are unknown constants and $\eta(t)$ is a bounded disturbance with $\vert \eta(t) \vert \le \eta_0$.

Re-write in regression form:
$$y(t) = \theta^{*T} \phi(t) + \eta_{eq}(t)$$
where $\theta^{*T} = [a, b]$, $\phi(t) = [-y(t), u(t)]$, and $\vert \eta_{eq} \vert \le \eta_0$.

### 3.1.2 Standard Gradient Adaptive Law

Define the prediction error:
$$e(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t)$$

Subtracting the model:
$$e(t) = \tilde{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t) + \eta_{eq}(t)$$
where $\tilde{\theta} = \theta^* - \hat{\theta}$.

Standard MIT-rule / gradient law:
$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = \Gamma \phi(t) e(t), \quad \Gamma = \Gamma^T > 0$$

### 3.1.3 Lyapunov Analysis — Why Drift Occurs

Choose $V(\tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}$. Then
$$\dot{V} = -\tilde{\theta}^T \phi e = -(\tilde{\theta}^T \phi)(\tilde{\theta}^T \phi + \eta_{eq}) = -(e - \eta_{eq})^2$$

This expands to:
$$\dot{V} = -e^2 + 2 e \eta_{eq} - \eta_{eq}^2$$

Using $2e \eta_{eq} \le e^2 + \eta_{eq}^2$ (Young's inequality), we get:
$$\dot{V} \le \eta_{eq}^2$$

Since $\eta_{eq}$ is **persistently non-zero**, $V$ is non-decreasing in the mean → **drift**.

### 3.1.4 Dead-Zone Insertion — Derivation

Define the dead-zone function:
$$\mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \vert e \vert \le \varepsilon_0 \\ e - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e) & \text{if } \vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0 \end{cases}$$

The modified law:
$$\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \Gamma \phi \, \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0]$$

Now form the augmented error $\bar{e} = e - \eta_{eq} = \tilde{\theta}^T \phi$.

Case 1: $\vert e \vert \le \varepsilon_0$. Then $\mathcal{D} = 0$, so $\dot{V} = 0$. No drift.

Case 2: $\vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0$. Then $\dot{V} = -(\tilde{\theta}^T \phi) \, \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0]$.

In this region, since $\vert \eta_{eq} \vert \le \varepsilon_0$, we have:
$$\vert \tilde{\theta}^T \phi \vert = \vert e - \eta_{eq} \vert \ge \vert e \vert - \varepsilon_0 = \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] \cdot \mathrm{sign}(e)$$

Therefore, $\dot{V} \le 0$. This proves $\tilde{\theta}(t) \in L_{\infty}$ and $\mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] \in L_2$, which together give $e(t) \to \varepsilon_0$ asymptotically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** With dead-zone, the prediction error converges to the **disturbance band** $[\!-\varepsilon_0, \varepsilon_0]$; this is the *best you can do* under persistent bounded disturbance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: σ-Modification Stability

### 3.2.1 Update Law

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = \Gamma \phi(t) e(t) - \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}(t), \quad \sigma > 0$$

Hence:
$$\dot{\tilde{\theta}}(t) = -\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma \phi(t) e(t) + \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}(t) = -\Gamma \phi e + \sigma \Gamma (\theta^* - \tilde{\theta})$$

### 3.2.2 Lyapunov Function

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2} \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}$$

$$\dot{V} = \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \tilde{\theta}^T \phi(t) e(t) - \sigma \tilde{\theta}^T \hat{\theta}(t)$$

Since $\tilde{\theta}^T \hat{\theta} = \tilde{\theta}^T(\theta^* - \tilde{\theta}) = \tilde{\theta}^T \theta^* - \Vert \tilde{\theta} \Vert^2$:

$$\dot{V} = \tilde{\theta}^T \phi e - \sigma \tilde{\theta}^T \theta^* + \sigma \Vert \tilde{\theta} \Vert^2$$

Using $\tilde{\theta}^T \phi e = (e - \eta_{eq})e = e^2 - e \eta_{eq} \ge \tfrac{1}{2} e^2 - \eta_{eq}^2$ (Young), and $- \sigma \tilde{\theta}^T \theta^* \ge -\tfrac{\sigma}{2} \Vert \tilde{\theta} \Vert^2 - \tfrac{\sigma}{2} \Vert \theta^* \Vert^2$:

$$\dot{V} \le -\tfrac{\sigma}{2} \Vert \tilde{\theta} \Vert^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \Vert \theta^* \Vert^2 \sigma + \eta_{eq}^2$$

By the **Comparison Lemma** (Khaliil), $V(t)$ is bounded by:
$$V(t) \le V(0) e^{-\sigma t} + \frac{\tfrac{\sigma}{2} \Vert \theta^* \Vert^2 + \eta_{eq}^2}{\sigma}$$

Hence:
$$\Vert \tilde{\theta}(t) \Vert \le \sqrt{ \frac{2}{\lambda_{\min}(\Gamma^{-1})} \left[V(0)e^{-\sigma t} + \Vert \theta^* \Vert^2 + \frac{2 \eta_{eq}^2}{\sigma} \right] }$$

So $\tilde{\theta}$ is **uniformly ultimately bounded** with a residual set proportional to $\sigma$ and $\eta_{eq}^2 / \sigma$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Recursive Least Squares with Variable Forgetting Factor

### 3.3.1 The Exponential-Weighted LS Cost

$$J_t(\hat{\theta}) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \big[y(k) - \phi^T(k) \hat{\theta}\big]^2$$

Setting $\partial J_t / \partial \hat{\theta} = 0$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \phi(k) \phi^T(k) \, \hat{\theta} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \phi(k) y(k)$$

Define recursively:
$$P^{-1}(t) = \lambda P^{-1}(t-1) + \phi(t) \phi^T(t)$$

$$L(t) = P(t) \phi(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}$$

$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + L(t) \big[ y(t) - \phi^T(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1) \big]$$

### 3.3.2 Variable Forgetting Factor — Fortescue's Update

To keep trace of $P(t)$ constant, set $\lambda(t)$ such that:

$$\mathrm{tr}\, P^{-1}(t) = \lambda(t) \, \mathrm{tr}\, P^{-1}(t-1) + \mathrm{tr}\big[\phi(t) \phi^T(t)\big] = \mathrm{const} = \Sigma_0$$

Solving:

$$\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{\phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\Sigma_0}$$

But the **actual VFF update** that maintains bounded $P$ is:
$$\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{\big[1 - \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)\big] \, e^2(t)}{\Sigma_0}$$

**Robust VFF** further bounds $\lambda$ and $P$:

$$\lambda_{\min} \le \lambda(t) \le 1, \quad P_{\min} I \le P(t) \le P_{\max} I$$

The lower bound $P_{\min} > 0$ guarantees **persistent alertness** to disturbance, and $P_{\max}$ prevents **covariance wind-up** when the input is uninformative.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Dead-Zone | σ-Mod | e-Mod | Projection | VFF-RLS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Drift prevention** | ✔ (with $\varepsilon_0$ known) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Prior knowledge of $\theta^*$ bound** | Not required | Not required | Not required | Sign of $\theta$ required | Not required |
| **Asymptotic zero error (no disturbance)** | ✘ (residual $\varepsilon_0$) | ✘ (residual $\sigma$) | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Boundedness proof** | Lyapunov + case analysis | Lyapunov | Lyapunov + B-G lemma | Lyapunov on $\mathcal{C}$ | Lyapunov + Persistency |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **KTU board favorite** | ✔✔ | ✔✔✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔✔ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robust Adaptive Control — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **complete robust adaptive control loop**, showing the interplay between the robust estimator (RLS-VFF), the certainty-equivalence controller, and the robustness modifications.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ref[R* Reference Signal] --> sumRef[Summing Junction]
    sumRef --> ctrl[Certainty Equivalence Controller]
    ctrl --> |u t| plant[Plant with Unmodelled Dynamics]
    plant --> |y t| sumOut[Summing Junction]
    dist[Bounded Disturbance nu t] --> sumOut
    sumOut --> |y_meas| meas[Sensor with Noise]
    meas --> |y_t| errCalc[Prediction Error Calculator]
    regressor[Regressor Generator phi t] --> errCalc
    errCalc --> |e t| dead[Dead Zone Modulator]
    errCalc --> |e t| robustMod[Robust Modification Term]
    regressor --> |phi t| estimator[Robust Estimator RLS with VFF]
    estimator --> |theta_hat t| ctrl
    dead --> estimator
    robustMod --> estimator
    plant -.-> |unmodelled| robustnessCheck{Small Gain Condition gamma1 gamma2 less than 1}
    dist -.-> robustnessCheck
    robustnessCheck --> |satisfied| stable[Closed Loop L2 Stable]
    robustnessCheck --> |violated| warn[Robustness Margin Lost]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **disturbance** $\eta(t)$ and the **unmodelled dynamics** enter the loop. They excite the prediction error $e(t)$. The estimator must *resist* this excitation. The **Dead-Zone** blocks small errors (noises), the **Robust Modification Term** (σ / e / projection) prevents drift, and the **VFF-RLS** keeps the estimator's gain $P(t)$ bounded. The **Small-Gain Condition** at the bottom is the *closed-loop robustness certificate*: if $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 < 1$, the system is provably $L_2$-stable.

## 4.2 Robustness Modification Family — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Choose Robust Modification] --> q1{Know the sign of theta star?}
    q1 -- Yes --> proj[Use Projection on convex set C]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Know disturbance bound eta_0?}
    q2 -- Yes --> dead[Use Dead Zone with threshold epsilon_0 greater than eta_0]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Need asymptotic zero error?}
    q3 -- Yes --> sw[Use Switching sigma Modification]
    q3 -- No --> sig[Use sigma Modification sigma greater than 0]
    sig --> choice{Discretetime or Continuoustime?}
    choice -- Continuous --> emod[Use e Modification for unknown sign]
    choice -- Discrete --> rls[Use RLS with Variable Forgetting Factor]
    dead --> rls
    rls --> bound[Bound P between P_min and P_max]
```

## 4.3 Recursive Least Squares with VFF — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[New Measurement phi t, y t] --> calce[Compute e t]
    calce --> gate{Dead Zone active?}
    gate -- No --> skip[Skip Update]
    gate -- Yes --> calcp[Compute P t via VFF]
    calcp --> boundCheck{P between P_min and P_max?}
    boundCheck -- No --> clip[Clip P to bounds]
    boundCheck -- Yes --> accept[Accept P]
    clip --> update[Update theta_hat t]
    accept --> update
    skip --> out
    update --> out[theta_hat t, P t for Controller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (KTU 2024 Emphasis):** Notice the **three nested safety nets** in the diagram: (1) *dead-zone* decides whether to update at all; (2) *bound check* on $P$ decides whether the gain is sensible; (3) *clipping* enforces the bounds. This **defensive programming** philosophy is what makes the estimator *robust* in real industrial deployment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adaptive Control]`

> **Q1.** What is **parameter drift** in adaptive control? State **two** robust modifications that prevent it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** *Parameter drift* is the phenomenon in which the parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}(t)$ grows unbounded over time even though the true parameters $\theta^*$ are constant. It occurs because under a persistently non-zero bounded disturbance $\eta(t)$, the standard gradient update $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \Gamma \phi e$ keeps accumulating the error $e \approx \eta$, leading to $\hat{\theta} \to \infty$.
- **(1 mark)** *Robust Modifications*:
  - (a) **Dead-zone modification:** switches off adaptation when $\vert e \vert \le \varepsilon_0$.
  - (b) **σ-modification (Ioannou & Kokotovic):** adds a leakage term $-\sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}$ to the update.
- **(1 mark)** Either of (a) e-modification, (b) projection modification, (c) switching-σ modification may also be cited as the second.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adaptive Control]`

> **Q2.** Distinguish between **σ-modification** and **e-modification** in robust adaptive control.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | σ-Modification | e-Modification |
|---|---|---|
| **Update law** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}$ | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \vert e \vert \hat{\theta}$ |
| **Prior knowledge needed** | Magnitude (or sign) of $\theta^*$ is implicitly used; if $\theta^* = 0$ the estimate is forced to zero — incorrect for non-zero bias | Does **not** assume knowledge of $\theta^*$ sign or magnitude; the leakage is automatically scaled by $\vert e \vert$ |
| **Stability condition** | $\sigma > 0$ (constant) | $\sigma > 0$ (constant); guarantees $L_{\infty}$ boundedness without bias |
| **Residual error** | $\mathcal{O}(\sigma)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sigma \vert e \vert)$ — vanishes when $e \to 0$ |
| **Year (Original)** | 1984, Ioannou & Kokotovic | 1987, Narendra & Annaswamy |

(1 mark for the laws, 1 mark for the prior-knowledge distinction, 1 mark for residual-error distinction.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question 3A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adaptive Control]`

> **Q3A (a)** [7 marks] Consider the plant $y(t) = \theta^{*T} \phi(t) + \eta(t)$, with $\vert \eta(t) \vert \le \eta_0$. Design a **dead-zone robust adaptive law** and prove that all signals remain bounded.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Regression model.** $\phi(t) = [-y(t), u(t)]^T$, $\theta^* = [a, b]^T$, prediction error $e(t) = y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t)$. Then $e(t) = \tilde{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t) + \eta(t)$ where $\tilde{\theta} = \theta^* - \hat{\theta}$.

**Step 2 — Dead-zone function.**
$$\mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert e \vert \le \varepsilon_0 \\ e - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e), & \vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Modified adaptive law.**
$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = \Gamma \, \phi(t) \, \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0], \quad \Gamma = \Gamma^T > 0, \quad \varepsilon_0 \ge \eta_0$$

**Step 4 — Lyapunov function.** $V = \tfrac{1}{2} \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}$.

**Step 5 — Derivative of $V$.**
$$\dot{V} = -\tilde{\theta}^T \phi \, \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0]$$

**Step 6 — Case analysis.**
- *Case 1*: $\vert e \vert \le \varepsilon_0$. Then $\mathcal{D} = 0$, so $\dot{V} = 0$ (no drift, $V$ non-increasing).
- *Case 2*: $\vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0$. Then $\mathcal{D} = e - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e)$. Since $\vert \eta \vert \le \varepsilon_0 \le \vert e \vert$, we have
$$\vert \tilde{\theta}^T \phi \vert = \vert e - \eta \vert \ge \vert e \vert - \varepsilon_0 = \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] \cdot \mathrm{sign}(e)$$

Hence $\tilde{\theta}^T \phi \cdot \mathcal{D}$ has the sign opposite to $\mathcal{D}^2$ (or zero), so:
$$\dot{V} = -(\tilde{\theta}^T \phi) \mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] \le 0$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** $V(t) \le V(0)$, so $\tilde{\theta}(t) \in L_{\infty}$, hence $\hat{\theta}(t) \in L_{\infty}$ and $u(t) \in L_{\infty}$. Also $\mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] \in L_2$, implying $e(t) \to \varepsilon_0$ asymptotically. $\blacksquare$

> **Q3A (b)** [7 marks] Compare the **dead-zone**, **σ-modification** and **projection** methods. What is the key **trade-off** in each?

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Dead-Zone (2 marks):** *Trade-off:* Requires knowledge of the disturbance bound $\eta_0$ to set $\varepsilon_0$. If $\varepsilon_0$ is set too high, the algorithm stops adapting even when the model is wrong; if set too low, drift reappears. Gives bounded error but **no asymptotic convergence to zero error**.

**σ-Modification (2 marks):** *Trade-off:* Introduces a steady-state bias of order $\mathcal{O}(\sigma)$ between $\hat{\theta}$ and $\theta^*$. Increasing $\sigma$ improves robustness margin but worsens tracking accuracy. No prior knowledge of $\eta_0$ required. The estimate is *always* pulled toward zero, which is correct only if $\theta^* = 0$ is the a priori best guess.

**Projection (2 marks):** *Trade-off:* Requires *a priori* knowledge of a convex set $\mathcal{C}$ containing $\theta^*$. If such a set is available (e.g., positive gains), projection is the cleanest method — preserves asymptotic convergence in the disturbance-free case. If the set is wrongly chosen (does not contain $\theta^*$), the algorithm may lock onto a boundary point, never reaching the true parameter.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The unifying trade-off across all robust modifications is **robustness margin vs. nominal performance**: each modification sacrifices asymptotic accuracy in exchange for boundedness under disturbance. The σ-modification is the most commonly used in KTU-board problems because it requires the least prior knowledge.

---

### **Question 3B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adaptive Control]`

> **Q3B (a)** [7 marks] Derive the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS) algorithm** with **Variable Forgetting Factor (VFF)**. Explain why VFF is needed in robust adaptive control.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost function with exponential forgetting.**
$$J_t(\hat{\theta}) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \big[y(k) - \phi^T(k) \hat{\theta}\big]^2, \quad \lambda \in (0, 1]$$

**Step 2 — Optimality condition.** $\partial J_t / \partial \hat{\theta} = 0$ gives:
$$\left[\sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \phi(k) \phi^T(k)\right] \hat{\theta} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \phi(k) y(k)$$

**Step 3 — Define covariance-like matrix.**
$$P^{-1}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \lambda^{t-k} \phi(k) \phi^T(k)$$

This satisfies the **Riccati recursion:**
$$P^{-1}(t) = \lambda P^{-1}(t-1) + \phi(t) \phi^T(t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply matrix inversion lemma** $\big[A + u v^T\big]^{-1} = A^{-1} - \frac{A^{-1} u v^T A^{-1}}{1 + v^T A^{-1} u}$:

With $A = \lambda P^{-1}(t-1)$, $u = v = \phi(t)$:
$$P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[ P(t-1) - \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t) \phi^T(t) P(t-1)}{\lambda + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Update equation.**
$$L(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)}$$
$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + L(t) \, e(t), \quad e(t) = y(t) - \phi^T(t) \hat{\theta}(t-1)$$

**Step 6 — VFF choice (Fortescue, 1981).** To maintain constant trace:
$$\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{\big[1 - \phi^T(t) P(t-1) \phi(t)\big] e^2(t)}{\Sigma_0}$$

with $\Sigma_0$ a design constant.

**Step 7 — Why VFF is needed in robust control (2 marks).** In standard RLS, $P(t) \to 0$ exponentially under persistent excitation, so the algorithm loses "alertness". A sudden disturbance cannot be tracked. VFF prevents $P$ from collapsing; the **robust VFF** further bounds $P_{\min} I \le P(t) \le P_{\max} I$ so that the gain is always sufficient to react to disturbances. $\blacksquare$

> **Q3B (b)** [7 marks] In a robust adaptive law, what is the **small-gain condition**? Show that for a robust modification with loop gain $\gamma$ the closed-loop error satisfies $\Vert e \Vert_2 \le \frac{\gamma}{1 - \gamma^2} \Vert \eta \Vert_2$ (or similar $L_2$-gain bound).

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark).** The small-gain theorem states that the feedback interconnection of two $L_2$-stable systems with $L_2$-gains $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ is internally $L_2$-stable if and only if $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 < 1$.

**Step 2 — Set-up (2 marks).** Partition the robust adaptive system into:
- **Subsystem 1** (adaptive/plant block): input $\eta$, output $e$, with $\Vert e \Vert_T \le \gamma_1 \Vert \eta \Vert_T + \beta_1$
- **Subsystem 2** (robust modification/observer): input $e$, output $\hat{\eta}$, with $\Vert \hat{\eta} \Vert_T \le \gamma_2 \Vert e \Vert_T + \beta_2$

**Step 3 — Bound (2 marks).** Using the triangle inequality on the loop:
$$\Vert e \Vert_T \le \gamma_1 \big(\gamma_2 \Vert e \Vert_T + \beta_2 + \Vert \eta \Vert_T \big) + \beta_1$$

Rearranging:
$$\Vert e \Vert_T (1 - \gamma_1 \gamma_2) \le \gamma_1 \Vert \eta \Vert_T + \gamma_1 \beta_2 + \beta_1$$

If $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 < 1$:
$$\Vert e \Vert_T \le \frac{\gamma_1}{1 - \gamma_1 \gamma_2} \Vert \eta \Vert_T + \frac{\gamma_1 \beta_2 + \beta_1}{1 - \gamma_1 \gamma_2}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (2 marks).** Hence the closed-loop is finite-gain $L_2$-stable, with **$L_2$-gain** $\gamma_{cl} = \frac{\gamma_1}{1 - \gamma_1 \gamma_2}$. This bound is the KTU-board version of the **robust stability certificate**: as $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 \to 1^-$, the gain blows up — the small-gain condition gives a *quantitative* safety margin. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Robust Control Law Questions**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the gradient update.** The KTU convention writes $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = \Gamma \phi e$ (descending *toward* the correct $y$). Many students write the update with the wrong sign and lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the disturbance bound.** In the dead-zone proof, the case-2 analysis requires $\vert \eta \vert \le \varepsilon_0$. Writing only $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ without stating the relationship to $\eta_0$ is the most common half-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing σ and e modifications.** Examiners specifically test if the student can articulate *why* e-modification is needed when the sign of $\theta^*$ is unknown. A blind statement of "e-mod is bounded" without the sign-independence argument loses full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the matrix-inversion lemma step in RLS derivation.** Board examiners expect the *name* of the lemma and the *form* of the resulting update. Skipping this step is worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the VFF bound on $P(t)$.** A RLS answer without the bound $P_{\min} I \le P \le P_{\max} I$ is **not** a robust law — it's a plain RLS. The "robust" qualifier is the question's central theme; missing it costs up to 3 marks.
> 6. **Omitting the small-gain condition.** A complete robust analysis *must* end with the small-gain condition $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 < 1$. Stopping at Lyapunov boundedness is a common 1-mark loss.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Robust Control Laws)**
>
> - **Definition:** A robust control law guarantees **boundedness of all closed-loop signals** in the presence of bounded disturbances, unmodelled dynamics, and plant variations.
> - **Three pathological issues** the robust law must solve: **(i)** parameter drift, **(ii)** loss of estimator alertness, **(iii)** bursting.
> - **Dead-zone modification:** $\mathcal{D}[e, \varepsilon_0] = e - \varepsilon_0 \, \mathrm{sign}(e) \cdot \mathcal{I}_{\vert e \vert > \varepsilon_0}$; choose $\varepsilon_0 \ge \eta_0$.
> - **σ-modification:** $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \hat{\theta}$, $\sigma > 0$; introduces steady-state error of order $\sigma$.
> - **e-modification:** $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e - \sigma \Gamma \vert e \vert \hat{\theta}$; preferred when sign of $\theta^*$ is unknown.
> - **Projection:** $\hat{\theta}^+ = \Pi_{\mathcal{C}}[\hat{\theta} - \Gamma \phi e]$; requires convex compact set $\mathcal{C}$ containing $\theta^*$.
> - **Switching-σ:** $\sigma(t) \in \{0, \sigma\}$; activated by a supervisory logic that monitors error.
> - **RLS update:** $\hat{\theta} = \hat{\theta}_- + P \phi e$; $P^{-1}_+ = P^{-1}_- + \phi \phi^T$; uses matrix inversion lemma.
> - **Exponential forgetting:** $P^{-1}_+ = \lambda P^{-1}_- + \phi \phi^T$, $\lambda \in (0, 1]$; gives finite memory.
> - **Variable Forgetting Factor:** $\lambda(t) = 1 - \frac{[1 - \phi^T P_- \phi] e^2}{\Sigma_0}$; keeps $\mathrm{tr}\, P^{-1}$ constant.
> - **Robust VFF:** $P_{\min} I \le P(t) \le P_{\max} I$; prevents both wind-up and loss of alertness.
> - **Lyapunov function:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2} \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}$ — workhorse of all continuous-time proofs.
> - **Young's inequality:** $2ab \le a^2 + b^2$ — used repeatedly in disturbance-cancellation steps.
> - **Small-gain theorem:** $\gamma_1 \gamma_2 < 1$ — *the* fundamental robustness condition for interconnected systems.
> - **$L_2$-gain bound:** $\Vert e \Vert_T \le \frac{\gamma_1}{1 - \gamma_1 \gamma_2} \Vert \eta \Vert_T + \text{const}$.
> - **Trade-off:** robustness margin $\leftrightarrow$ nominal accuracy; no free lunch in robust adaptive control.
> - **KTU 2024 emphasis:** expect at least **one 7-mark sub-question** on either (i) the Lyapunov proof for σ-modification or (ii) the RLS-VFF derivation with the inversion lemma; both are board favorites.
> - **Industrial deployment ranking** (most → least common): **σ-mod ≈ projection > dead-zone > e-mod > switching-σ** for fixed-structure adaptive controllers; **VFF-RLS with $P$-bounds** is dominant in self-tuning regulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pole Placement Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pole Placement Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pole Placement Design** is a classical control synthesis technique in which the closed-loop characteristic polynomial of a controlled system is explicitly specified by the designer, and the controller gains are computed so that the actual closed-loop poles coincide exactly with these desired pole locations.
