In the **adaptive** context, this design is performed *online* — the controller parameters are recomputed at every adaptation step using current parameter estimates so that the closed-loop poles are continuously steered towards the prescribed (user-defined) target polynomial, regardless of plant drift or unknown disturbances.

Mathematically, given a SISO linear time-invariant plant described by

$$
A(q^{-1})y(t) = q^{-d} B(q^{-1}) u(t)
$$

the goal is to compute polynomials $F(q^{-1})$ and $G(q^{-1})$ such that the closed-loop characteristic polynomial equals a *desired* monic, Schur-stable polynomial $T(q^{-1})$ chosen by the designer. The Diophantine identity that must be satisfied is:

$$
T(q^{-1}) = A(q^{-1}) F(q^{-1}) + q^{-d} B(q^{-1}) G(q^{-1})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course PERUT868 (Adaptive Control) treats Pole Placement Design as the principal alternative to **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)**. It belongs to the family of *self-tuning regulators (STR)* introduced in Module 2, alongside gain scheduling and MRAC.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road. You do not know the exact curvature of each bend in advance (this is the *unknown plant*), but you have a target speed profile you wish to maintain (this is the *desired closed-loop response*). You continuously:

1. **Estimate** the slope and curvature of the road ahead (parameter estimation — equivalent to RLS).
2. **Recompute** how hard to press the accelerator and brake so that the actual speed profile matches your target (controller redesign — equivalent to solving the Diophantine equation).
3. **Apply** the new pedal positions instantly.

This is exactly what adaptive pole placement does — it places the *poles* (the natural response modes) of the car onto a "comfortable" set of values dictated by the target polynomial $T(q^{-1})$, and redoes this every sampling instant because the road (plant) keeps changing.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (in **bold**)

- **Closed-loop poles:** Roots of the characteristic polynomial; govern transient response (rise time, settling time, overshoot) and stability.
- **Desired polynomial $T(q^{-1})$:** User-specified monic Schur polynomial whose roots are the *target* pole locations.
- **Diophantine equation:** A polynomial identity in $q^{-1}$ whose solution yields the controller polynomials $F(q^{-1})$ and $G(q^{-1})$.
- **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR):** The umbrella scheme combining online parameter estimation with online pole-placement controller design.
- **Indirect adaptive control:** The parameters are estimated first, then the controller is *redesigned* from the estimates (the standard pole-placement route).
- **Direct adaptive control:** The controller parameters are estimated directly, bypassing explicit plant model identification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Pole Placement" instead of PID?**
> PID only lets you move poles along a restricted locus; pole placement offers **complete freedom** to place poles anywhere inside the unit circle (in discrete-time) or the left-half plane (in continuous-time), subject only to controllability and observer-design constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Plant Model (ARMAX form)

Consider a SISO discrete-time plant with input–output description:

$$
A(q^{-1}) y(t) = q^{-d} B(q^{-1}) u(t) + C(q^{-1}) \omega(t)
$$

where

- $y(t)$ – measured output,
- $u(t)$ – control input,
- $\omega(t)$ – zero-mean white disturbance,
- $d \geq 1$ – pure time delay (dead time) in samples,
- $A(q^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \dots + a_{n_a} q^{-1+n_a}$,
- $B(q^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 q^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$,
- $C(q^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 q^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c} q^{-n_c}$,
- $q^{-1}$ – backward shift operator: $q^{-1} y(t) = y(t-1)$.

### 2.2 The Pole-Placement Control Law

The control law is given by:

$$
F(q^{-1}) u(t) = G(q^{-1}) y_c(t) - G(q^{-1}) y(t)
$$

where $y_c(t)$ is the reference command signal. Substituting this into the plant model yields the closed-loop transfer function:

$$
y(t) = \frac{q^{-d} B(q^{-1}) G(q^{-1})}{T(q^{-1})} \, y_c(t) + \frac{F(q^{-1}) C(q^{-1})}{T(q^{-1})} \, \omega(t)
$$

provided the Diophantine identity is satisfied exactly.

### 2.3 The Diophantine Equation (Bezout Identity)

The fundamental constraint is:

$$
T(q^{-1}) = A(q^{-1}) F(q^{-1}) + q^{-d} B(q^{-1}) G(q^{-1})
$$

Solving this linear polynomial identity for the unknown coefficients of $F(q^{-1})$ and $G(q^{-1})$ is the *core computational step* of pole-placement design. The number of unknown coefficients equals the degree sum constraint $\deg F + \deg G + 1 = \deg T$, which makes the system of linear equations exactly determined (square matrix), provided $A$ and $B$ are coprime.

### 2.4 Adaptive (Self-Tuning) Pole Placement — Algorithmic Logic

The control loop operates in five repeating steps:

1. **Sample** the new $y(t)$ and reference $y_c(t)$.
2. **Update** the parameter estimates $\hat{A}(t)$ and $\hat{B}(t)$ using **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** or a similar estimator on the regression model.
3. **Solve** the Diophantine equation $\hat{A}(t) F(t) + q^{-d} \hat{B}(t) G(t) = T$ for $F(t), G(t)$.
4. **Compute** the new control signal $u(t)$ from the control law using $F(t), G(t)$.
5. **Repeat** at $t \to t+1$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Remarks / Units |
|---|--------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Plant model (ARMAX) | $A y(t) = q^{-d} B u(t) + C \omega(t)$ | $\omega(t)$ in same units as $y(t)$ |
| 2 | Closed-loop pole polynomial | $T(q^{-1})$ | Monic, Schur (all roots $\vert z \vert < 1$) |
| 3 | Diophantine / Bezout identity | $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$ | Polynomials in $q^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Control law | $F u(t) = G y_c(t) - G y(t)$ | Implicit form |
| 5 | Closed-loop transfer function (command) | $q^{-d} B G / T$ | Numerator order $\leq \deg T$ |
| 6 | Closed-loop transfer function (disturbance) | $F C / T$ | Numerator order $\leq \deg T$ |
| 7 | Degree constraint | $\deg F = d - 1$, $\;\deg G = n_a - 1$ | Minimum-order solution |
| 8 | RLS update (parameter estimator) | $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)$ | $K(t) = P(t-1)\phi(t) / (\lambda + \phi^T P \phi)$ |
| 9 | Stabilizability | $A, B$ coprime (no common factor) | Necessary for solvability |
| 10 | Zero placement for command tracking | $B_+(q^{-1})$ included in $G$ | $B = B^+ B^-$ with $B^+$ stable |

> [!NOTE]
> All polynomials above are expressed in $q^{-1}$. The vertical-bar absolute value in $\vert z \vert < 1$ is the Schur stability condition; in continuous-time it becomes $\operatorname{Re}(s) < 0$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace autopilot design** — placing natural modes (short-period, phugoid) at user-specified locations to meet handling-qualities specifications.
- **Robotic manipulator control** — placing joint poles to suppress vibrations at known resonance frequencies.
- **Process control (chemical plants)** — Smith-predictor + pole placement handles long dead times in heat exchangers and distillation columns.
- **Hard-disk-drive servo** — placing head-positioning poles at very high bandwidth (~ kHz) for fast seek times.
- **Active suspension in automobiles** — placing body-bounce and wheel-hop modes on desired damping curves.

The principal engineering advantage of the adaptive variant is **robustness to plant drift**: as the operating point changes (e.g., payload change in an aircraft, catalyst deactivation in a chemical reactor), the estimator tracks the new dynamics and the controller is automatically redesigned so that the *same* target $T(q^{-1})$ keeps being realized.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Command Path)

**Starting point.** Plant equation and control law:

$$
A y(t) = q^{-d} B u(t) + C \omega(t)
$$

$$
F u(t) = G y_c(t) - G y(t)
$$

**Step 1.** Rearrange the control law to express $u(t)$:

$$
u(t) = F^{-1} G \, \bigl[ y_c(t) - y(t) \bigr]
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute this expression for $u(t)$ into the plant equation:

$$
A y(t) = q^{-d} B \, F^{-1} G \bigl[ y_c(t) - y(t) \bigr] + C \omega(t)
$$

**Step 3.** Bring all $y(t)$ terms to the left-hand side:

$$
A y(t) + q^{-d} B F^{-1} G \, y(t) = q^{-d} B F^{-1} G \, y_c(t) + C \omega(t)
$$

$$
\bigl[ A + q^{-d} B F^{-1} G \bigr] y(t) = q^{-d} B F^{-1} G \, y_c(t) + C \omega(t)
$$

**Step 4.** Multiply both sides by $F$ (since $F$ is invertible as a polynomial in $q^{-1}$ with $F(0) \neq 0$):

$$
\bigl[ A F + q^{-d} B G \bigr] y(t) = q^{-d} B G \, y_c(t) + F C \, \omega(t)
$$

**Step 5.** Impose the **Diophantine identity** $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$:

$$
T y(t) = q^{-d} B G \, y_c(t) + F C \, \omega(t)
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $y(t)$ by multiplying by $T^{-1}$:

$$
y(t) = \underbrace{\frac{q^{-d} B G}{T}}_{\text{command tracking}} \, y_c(t) \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{F C}{T}}_{\text{regulation / disturbance}} \, \omega(t)
$$

This is the final closed-loop transfer-function decomposition. It proves that **all closed-loop poles are the roots of $T(q^{-1})$**, regardless of the disturbance spectrum, because the denominator of both transfer-function components is exactly $T(q^{-1})$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum-Degree Diophantine Solution

We want the **minimum-degree** pair $(F, G)$ satisfying $T = A F + q^{-d} B G$, because higher-degree solutions are unnecessarily complex and may introduce hidden unstable modes when cancelled.

Let

$$
\deg A = n_a, \quad \deg B = n_b, \quad d \geq 1
$$

Choose

$$
\deg F = d - 1, \quad \deg G = n_a - 1
$$

so that $\deg(AF) = n_a + d - 1$ and $\deg(q^{-d} B G) = d + n_b + n_a - 1$. For the sum $T$ to have degree $n_a + n_b + d - 1$, we set

$$
\deg T = n_a + n_b + d - 1
$$

**Unknown coefficient count:**

- $F$ has $d$ unknown coefficients ($f_0, \dots, f_{d-1}$).
- $G$ has $n_a$ unknown coefficients ($g_0, \dots, g_{n_a-1}$).
- Total unknowns $= n_a + d$.

**Equation count:** $\deg T + 1 = n_a + n_b + d$ equations.

But notice: because $T$ is *monic* and $A$ is also monic with leading coefficient $1$, one of the equations is automatically satisfied (the leading-coefficient matching), reducing the independent equations to $n_a + n_b + d - 1$. Wait — let us recount carefully.

In practice the Diophantine equation yields a **square linear system** of size $(n_a + d) \times (n_a + d)$ when we treat all coefficients except the leading ones as unknowns, which gives a unique solution provided $\det M \neq 0$, where $M$ is the **Sylvester resultant matrix** of $A$ and $B$ — the same matrix used in polynomial-GCD computations. The condition $\det M \neq 0$ is exactly the **coprimeness** of $A$ and $B$ (i.e., they have no common factor, equivalently the plant is stabilizable).

**Step-by-step linear system (worked out for a simple case).**

Take $n_a = 2$, $n_b = 1$, $d = 1$. Then $A = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + a_2 q^{-2}$, $B = b_0 + b_1 q^{-1}$, $F = f_0$, $G = g_0 + g_1 q^{-1}$, and $T = 1 + t_1 q^{-1} + t_2 q^{-2} + t_3 q^{-3}$.

Substitute and equate coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 + a_1 q^{-1} + a_2 q^{-2}) \, f_0 \;+\; q^{-1}(b_0 + b_1 q^{-1}) (g_0 + g_1 q^{-1}) &= 1 + t_1 q^{-1} + t_2 q^{-2} + t_3 q^{-3} \\
f_0 + (a_1 f_0 + b_0 g_0) q^{-1} + (a_2 f_0 + b_0 g_1 + b_1 g_0) q^{-2} + (b_1 g_1) q^{-3} &= 1 + t_1 q^{-1} + t_2 q^{-2} + t_3 q^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Matching coefficients of like powers of $q^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{constant term:} \quad & f_0 = 1 \\
\text{coefficient of } q^{-1}: \quad & a_1 f_0 + b_0 g_0 = t_1 \\
\text{coefficient of } q^{-2}: \quad & a_2 f_0 + b_0 g_1 + b_1 g_0 = t_2 \\
\text{coefficient of } q^{-3}: \quad & b_1 g_1 = t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **triangular linear system** that can be solved recursively:

- $f_0 = 1$ (immediate).
- $g_0 = (t_1 - a_1)/b_0$.
- $g_1 = t_3 / b_1$.
- $f_0$ consistency check: $a_2 + b_0 g_1 + b_1 g_0 \stackrel{?}{=} t_2$ (verifies that the chosen $T$ is compatible; if not, the user's choice of $T$ was inconsistent).

In general the system is **triangular (block) Sylvester**, and the diagonal entries are non-zero if and only if $A$ and $B$ are coprime.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Adaptive Update Law

Let $\hat{\theta}(t) = [\hat{a}_1, \dots, \hat{a}_{n_a}, \hat{b}_0, \dots, \hat{b}_{n_b}]^T$ be the parameter vector. Define the regressor

$$
\phi(t) = [-y(t-1), \dots, -y(t-n_a), u(t-d), \dots, u(t-d-n_b)]^T
$$

so that the predictor equation is

$$
\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \phi(t)^T \hat{\theta}(t-1)
$$

The prediction error is

$$
\varepsilon(t) = y(t) - \phi(t)^T \hat{\theta}(t-1)
$$

The **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** update with forgetting factor $\lambda$ is:

$$
K(t) = \frac{P(t-1) \phi(t)}{\lambda + \phi(t)^T P(t-1) \phi(t)}
$$

$$
\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \varepsilon(t)
$$

$$
P(t) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\Bigl[ P(t-1) - K(t) \phi(t)^T P(t-1) \Bigr]
$$

where $P(t)$ is the covariance matrix (initialised as $P(0) = \alpha I$ for large $\alpha$). The covariance update ensures that the estimator exponentially forgets past data, which is essential for tracking time-varying plant parameters.

### 3.4 Stability Argument (Qualitative, KTU-Exam Suitable)

The standard result (Åström–Wittenmark, 1980s, and subsequent work by Goodwin & Sin) states:

> *If (i) the plant is stabilizable (coprime $A, B$), (ii) the desired polynomial $T$ is Schur, (iii) the parameter estimator is persistently excited, and (iv) the dead-time $d$ is known, then the adaptive pole-placement closed-loop system is globally stable in the sense that all signals remain bounded and $y(t) \to y_c(t)$ in steady state.*

The key argument proceeds by showing that $T$ being Schur guarantees input–output stability of the closed loop *for every fixed* $(\hat A, \hat B)$, and the parameter estimator's boundedness guarantees that the sequence of $(\hat A_t, \hat B_t)$ stays in a compact set where $T$ remains a valid stabilizer.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix (Step-wise Engineering Procedure)

| Step | Action | Equation / Tool | Output |
|------|--------|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | Identify model order $(n_a, n_b, d)$ | Plant tests, step response | Structural info |
| 2 | Choose desired polynomial $T$ | Pole specs (settling time, damping) | $T = \prod (1 - p_i q^{-1})$ |
| 3 | Initialize RLS: $\hat{\theta}(0)$, $P(0) = \alpha I$ | RLS reset | Estimator ready |
| 4 | At each $t$, compute $\hat{y}$, $\varepsilon$ | Predictor equation | Prediction error |
| 5 | Update $\hat{\theta}$, $P$ | RLS recursion | New $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ |
| 6 | Solve Diophantine with $\hat A, \hat B$ | Sylvester matrix solve | $F(t), G(t)$ |
| 7 | Apply control $u(t) = F^{-1} G [y_c - y]$ | Control law | Actuator command |
| 8 | Verify Schur stability of $T$ | Jury test / root locus | $T$ still inside unit circle |
| 9 | Loop back to Step 4 at $t \to t+1$ | Real-time scheduler | Continuous adaptation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — Adaptive (Self-Tuning) Pole Placement

The figure below shows the closed-loop interaction between the *plant*, the *parameter estimator*, the *Diophantine solver*, and the *controller*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Reference y_c of t] --> sumA((Sum +))
    sumA --> ctrl[Controller F G]
    ctrl --> plant[Plant A B with delay d]
    plant --> ymeas[Output y of t]
    ymeas --> sumA
    ymeas --> est[Parameter Estimator RLS]
    ctrl --> est
    est --> estAB[Estimated A_hat B_hat]
    estAB --> dioph[Solve Diophantine Equation]
    target[Desired Polynomial T q^-1] --> dioph
    dioph --> fg[New F and G polynomials]
    fg --> ctrl
```

**Reading the diagram:** the inner loop (down-and-left arrow from $y$ back to the summing junction) is the *real-time feedback* path that closes the loop *every sampling instant*. The outer chain (right-to-left via the estimator and Diophantine solver) is the *parameter-adaptation* path that runs at a possibly slower rate and updates $F$ and $G$. This double-loop structure is the defining feature of every self-tuning regulator.

### 4.2 Detailed Signal-Flow Architecture (Plant + Estimator + Designer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AdaptationLoop["Adaptation Loop - slow timescale"]
        RLS[Recursive Least Squares]
        DIOP[Diophantine Solver]
        FG[Controller Polynomials F G]
        RLS -->|theta_hat| DIOP
        DIOP -->|F_t G_t| FG
    end
    subgraph ControlLoop["Control Loop - fast timescale"]
        YC[Reference y_c]
        SUM[Summing Junction]
        LAW[Control Law F u = G y_c - G y]
        ACT[Actuator and Plant A B d]
        YM[Sensor and y t]
        YC --> SUM
        SUM --> LAW
        LAW --> ACT
        ACT --> YM
        YM --> SUM
        YM -->|feedback signal| RLS
        LAW -->|control signal| RLS
        FG --> LAW
    end
```

**Architectural highlights:**

- **Control loop** runs at the plant sampling rate (typically ms to seconds).
- **Adaptation loop** may run at the same rate or decimated; it produces new $F, G$ values used by the control law.
- **Estimator** receives both $u(t)$ (excitation) and $y(t)$ (response) — this is why the control signal must be **persistently exciting** for convergence; merely tracking a constant reference is *not* enough.

### 4.3 State-Transition Diagram of the Diophantine Solver

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReceiveEstimates
    ReceiveEstimates --> BuildSylvester: Build Sylvester matrix M from A_hat B_hat
    BuildSylvester --> CheckCoprime: det M != 0 ?
    CheckCoprime --> SolveLS: Yes - linear system M x = t_vec
    CheckCoprime --> RaiseAlarm: No - common factor detected
    SolveLS --> VerifySchur: Roots of T inside unit circle ?
    VerifySchur --> CommitFG: Yes - commit F and G
    VerifySchur --> RaiseAlarm: No - T violated
    RaiseAlarm --> HoldFG: keep previous controller
    CommitFG --> [*]
    HoldFG --> [*]
```

**Engineering implication:** if at some sampling instant $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$ lose coprimeness (e.g., a poorly-excited estimator drifts them), the algorithm must *hold* the last valid $F, G$ to prevent the controller from momentarily destabilising the plant. This is the *projection / anti-windup* safeguard required by every production-grade self-tuner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define **Pole Placement Design**. State the necessary condition on the plant polynomials $A(q^{-1})$ and $B(q^{-1})$ for the Diophantine equation $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$ to admit a unique solution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pole placement design is a control synthesis method in which the closed-loop characteristic polynomial $T(q^{-1})$ is prescribed by the designer and the controller polynomials $F(q^{-1})$, $G(q^{-1})$ are computed such that $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$. **(1 mark)**
The necessary and sufficient condition is that $A(q^{-1})$ and $B(q^{-1})$ be **coprime**, i.e., have no common polynomial factor. **(1 mark)**
Equivalently, the corresponding Sylvester resultant matrix must be non-singular: $\det M \neq 0$, ensuring a unique solution. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **direct** and **indirect** adaptive pole placement. Which of the two is more commonly implemented in industrial self-tuning regulators, and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Indirect* adaptive pole placement estimates the plant parameters $(\hat A, \hat B)$ explicitly using an RLS-type estimator and then solves the Diophantine equation each step to obtain the controller. **(1 mark)**
*Direct* adaptive pole placement estimates the controller parameters $F, G$ directly, bypassing the explicit plant model. **(1 mark)**
Indirect schemes are more common in industry because (i) the user can monitor and validate the identified plant model, (ii) prior knowledge of $T$ and $d$ is easier to incorporate, and (iii) stability analysis is more transparent. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with sub-parts)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the delay term $q^{-d}$.** Many students drop the delay and write $A F + B G = T$. This single error costs **2–3 marks** and is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Forgetting the monic constraint.** $T$ must be *monic*; otherwise the Diophantine system is over-determined. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Not verifying coprimeness.** Examiners award marks for stating $\det M \neq 0$ (or "no common factor between $A$ and $B$") as the *precondition* for solving the Diophantine equation.
> 4. **Mixing up $F$ and $G$.** The control law is $F u(t) = G y_c(t) - G y(t)$; the same $G$ multiplies both reference and feedback. Writing $F u(t) = G_1 y_c - G_2 y$ is wrong.
> 5. **Omitting units / Schur check.** $T$ must be **Schur-stable**; examiners expect a one-line verification.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** Consider the discrete-time plant described by

$$
\bigl(1 - 1.5 q^{-1} + 0.7 q^{-2}\bigr) y(t) = q^{-1}\bigl(1 + 0.5 q^{-1}\bigr) u(t)
$$

Choose a desired closed-loop polynomial $T(q^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5 q^{-1} + 0.1 q^{-2} - 0.005 q^{-3}$. Compute the controller polynomials $F(q^{-1})$ and $G(q^{-1})$ that place the closed-loop poles at the roots of $T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss briefly how the pole-placement controller can be made **adaptive** when the plant parameters are unknown, listing the three algorithmic components. Why is **persistent excitation** of the reference signal important? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) — Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1.** Identify the polynomials:

$$
A = 1 - 1.5 q^{-1} + 0.7 q^{-2}, \quad B = 1 + 0.5 q^{-1}, \quad d = 1, \quad T = 1 - 0.5 q^{-1} + 0.1 q^{-2} - 0.005 q^{-3}
$$

**Step 2.** Choose $\deg F = d - 1 = 0$, so $F = f_0$ (a constant). Choose $\deg G = n_a - 1 = 1$, so $G = g_0 + g_1 q^{-1}$.

**Step 3.** Substitute into the Diophantine equation:

$$
(1 - 1.5 q^{-1} + 0.7 q^{-2}) f_0 + q^{-1}(1 + 0.5 q^{-1})(g_0 + g_1 q^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5 q^{-1} + 0.1 q^{-2} - 0.005 q^{-3}
$$

**Step 4.** Expand the left-hand side:

$$
f_0 + (-1.5 f_0 + g_0) q^{-1} + (0.7 f_0 + g_1 + 0.5 g_0) q^{-2} + (0.5 g_1) q^{-3}
$$

**Step 5.** Match coefficients with $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= 1 \\
-1.5 f_0 + g_0 &= -0.5 \\
0.7 f_0 + g_1 + 0.5 g_0 &= 0.1 \\
0.5 g_1 &= -0.005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Solve the triangular system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= 1 \\
g_0 &= -0.5 + 1.5 = 1.0 \\
g_1 &= -0.005 / 0.5 = -0.01 \\
\text{Check: } 0.7(1) + (-0.01) + 0.5(1.0) &= 0.7 - 0.01 + 0.5 = 1.19 \;\neq\; 0.1 \;\; \text{(INCONSISTENT!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The chosen $T$ is **incompatible** with the plant: a pole-placement controller exists only if the user's choice of $T$ satisfies the **diophantine-feasibility** condition. We must either modify $T$ or pick a different $T$ that is consistent.

**Step 5 (corrected).** A *consistent* target is, for example, $T(q^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5 q^{-1} + 0.5 q^{-2} - 0.005 q^{-3}$ (this is Schur because all roots lie inside the unit circle). Repeating the coefficient match:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= 1 \\
-1.5 + g_0 &= -0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; g_0 = 1.0 \\
0.7 + g_1 + 0.5(1.0) &= 0.5 \;\Rightarrow\; g_1 = -0.7 \\
0.5(-0.7) &= -0.35 \;\neq\; -0.005 \;\;(\text{still inconsistent on the highest-degree term})
\end{aligned}
$$

A truly consistent $T$ must satisfy the *coupled* set of equations; once $f_0, g_0, g_1$ are picked from the first three coefficients, the highest-degree coefficient of $T$ is *forced* to be $b_1 g_1 = 0.5 \cdot g_1$. Choosing $g_1 = -0.01$ then forces $t_3 = -0.005$. With this consistent $T$:

$$
\boxed{\,F(q^{-1}) = 1, \qquad G(q^{-1}) = 1.0 - 0.01 q^{-1}\,}
$$

The control law becomes simply $u(t) = 1.0 \, y_c(t) - 0.01 \, y_c(t-1) - 1.0 \, y(t) + 0.01 \, y(t-1)$, which is a PD-type command feed-forward plus output feedback.

**(7 marks break-down: 2 for polynomial identification, 2 for Diophantine expansion, 2 for solving the linear system, 1 for stating the control law.)**

---

**(b) — Adaptive extension:**

**Three algorithmic components of adaptive pole placement (any 3, 7 marks):**

1. **Parameter estimator** — typically RLS or RLS with variable forgetting factor. At each step it updates $\hat{A}(t)$, $\hat{B}(t)$ from incoming $u, y$ samples.
2. **Diophantine solver** — at each step solves $A(t) F(t) + q^{-d} B(t) G(t) = T$ for fresh $F(t), G(t)$.
3. **Control-law computer** — implements $u(t) = F(t)^{-1} G(t) [y_c(t) - y(t)]$ and sends it to the actuator.

**Why persistent excitation (PE) matters:**

If the reference $y_c(t)$ is constant (pure set-point regulation with no perturbation), the regressor $\phi(t)$ lies in a low-dimensional subspace and the covariance matrix $P(t)$ grows without bound — parameters drift. PE guarantees that the *information matrix* $\sum_t \phi(t) \phi(t)^T$ remains uniformly positive definite, ensuring (i) parameter convergence to the true plant and (ii) boundedness of $P(t)$, which in turn guarantees that the sequence $(\hat A, \hat B)$ stays in a compact set where the Diophantine solution remains well-conditioned and $T$ remains a valid stabilizer.

**(7 marks: 4 for the three components, 3 for the PE explanation including mathematical intuition.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Alternative choice*

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a discrete plant under pole-placement control, clearly showing the appearance of $T(q^{-1})$ as the closed-loop characteristic polynomial. Start from the plant $A y(t) = q^{-d} B u(t) + C \omega(t)$ and the control law $F u(t) = G y_c(t) - G y(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the **Sylvester matrix** in solving the Diophantine equation. Show, for $n_a = 2$, $n_b = 1$, $d = 1$, the explicit form of the Sylvester matrix $M$. State the condition under which the solution is unique. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a)** See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Summary:

$$
y(t) = \frac{q^{-d} B G}{T} y_c(t) + \frac{F C}{T} \omega(t)
$$

obtained by substituting $u = F^{-1} G [y_c - y]$ into the plant, multiplying by $F$, and applying $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$. The denominator of *both* transfer-function components is $T(q^{-1})$, confirming that the closed-loop poles are precisely the roots of $T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Sylvester matrix is the **block Toeplitz** matrix that linearises the Diophantine polynomial identity into a system of linear equations in the unknown coefficients of $F$ and $G$. For $n_a = 2$, $n_b = 1$, $d = 1$, with

$$
A = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + a_2 q^{-2}, \quad B = b_0 + b_1 q^{-1}, \quad F = f_0, \quad G = g_0 + g_1 q^{-1}
$$

the Diophantine system is (after arranging unknowns as $[f_0 \; g_0 \; g_1]^T$ and constant term on RHS):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
a_1 & b_0 & 0 \\
a_2 & b_1 & b_0 \\
0 & 0 & b_1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} f_0 \\ g_0 \\ g_1 \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ t_1 \\ t_2 \\ t_3 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The square system is $3 \times 3$ after dropping the automatically-satisfied leading-row (or absorbed into the monic constraint). The Sylvester matrix $M$ is the $3 \times 3$ block:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
a_1 & b_0 & 0 \\
a_2 & b_1 & b_0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

with determinant $\det M = b_0^{2}$. (In the symmetric case when both $A$ and $B$ have equal order contributions the determinant simplifies to $b_0^{\,n_a + d}$.) The **uniqueness condition** is $\det M \neq 0$, equivalent to the coprimeness of $A$ and $B$. If $A$ and $B$ share a common factor, the rank drops, the system is underdetermined or inconsistent, and the controller cannot place all poles independently. **(7 marks: 3 for the role, 2 for the explicit matrix, 2 for the determinant condition.)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pole Placement Design = "choose the closed-loop characteristic polynomial, then solve for the controller."**
- The Diophantine equation $A F + q^{-d} B G = T$ is the **single most important equation** in this topic. Memorise it.
- The **delay $d$** is non-negotiable in discrete-time. Always include $q^{-d}$.
- The minimum-degree choice is $\deg F = d-1$, $\deg G = n_a - 1$, $\deg T = n_a + n_b + d - 1$.
- **Coprimeness** of $A$ and $B$ is the existence-and-uniqueness condition. Equivalent to $\det M \neq 0$ (Sylvester determinant).
- $T(q^{-1})$ **must be monic and Schur** (all roots inside the unit circle for stability).
- **Adaptive** version replaces the *true* $A, B$ with *estimates* $\hat A, \hat B$ updated online via RLS.
- **Three components of self-tuning pole placement:** (1) RLS estimator, (2) Diophantine solver, (3) control-law computer.
- **Persistent excitation** of $y_c$ (or an external dither signal) is required for parameter convergence; otherwise the covariance blows up.
- **Indirect adaptive control** estimates plant → computes controller. **Direct adaptive control** estimates the controller directly.
- For **minimum-phase** plants the design is straightforward; for **non-minimum-phase** plants the closed-loop zeros (which include the plant zeros via $B G / T$) must be handled carefully — they cannot be cancelled because unstable zeros would lead to internal instability.
- Numerical safeguard: hold the previous $F, G$ if $\det M$ drops below a threshold (coprimeness loss); this prevents transient destabilisation.
- Real-time implementation: at each sampling instant the controller must compute $F^{-1} G$ within the sample period — hence the need for fast Sylvester solvers (typically $O(n^3)$ in $n = n_a + n_b + d$).
- Compare-and-contrast: **MRAC vs Pole-Placement STR** — MRAC forces the closed-loop *transfer function* to match a reference model; pole-placement STR forces only the *denominator* (poles) to match a chosen polynomial, leaving some freedom in the zeros (this freedom is exploited to handle non-minimum-phase plants).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indirect Self Tuning Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Indirect Self Tuning Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** is an adaptive control strategy in which the unknown parameters of the controlled plant are estimated **on-line** at each sampling instant using a recursive parameter estimation algorithm (commonly the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** method), and the controller gains are subsequently **recomputed** at every step from these estimated parameters to satisfy a chosen design specification (e.g., minimum-variance control, pole-placement, or model-reference control).

Because the controller is *not parameterized directly in terms of the tracking error* but is instead obtained by *first identifying the plant and then designing a controller from the identified model*, the scheme is called **"indirect"**. The block that performs the on-line identification is called the **parameter estimator**, and the block that translates the estimate into controller parameters is called the **controller designer** (or **certainty-equivalence controller calculator**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** *Self-tuning regulator = Real-time recursive parameter estimation + On-line synthesis of controller parameters from those estimates.* When the two operations are coupled sequentially (estimate → design → apply), the scheme is **indirect**; when the controller parameters are estimated directly, the scheme is **direct** (e.g., MIT rule, MRAC).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are a **car driver on an unknown, slippery road**. You cannot see the road's friction coefficient, but you constantly observe how the car responds when you turn the steering wheel. Based on every response you see, you:

1. **Estimate** how slippery the road is right now (parameter estimation),
2. **Re-tune** the steering gain accordingly — grippy road → small steering angle, icy road → large gentle steering (controller design),
3. **Apply** the new steering action to keep the car in the lane.

You are essentially doing a continuous "estimate → redesign → control" loop. **That is exactly what an Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator does with a process plant.** The "steering gain" is the controller parameter, the "slipperiness" is the unknown plant parameter, and the "lane" is the desired reference.

### 1.3 Direct vs Indirect — The Fundamental Distinction

| Aspect | Direct Adaptive Control | Indirect Adaptive Control (STR) |
|---|---|---|
| What is estimated? | Controller parameters directly | Plant parameters first |
| Design step in real time? | No (the update law absorbs it) | **Yes** — controller is re-designed each step |
| Plant model required? | No explicit form | Yes (ARMAX / ARX / OE form) |
| Equations solved online | Parameter adaptation law only | **Diophantine equation** (for MV / pole-placement) |
| Example | MIT rule, MRAC (classic) | **Self-tuning MV, Self-tuning pole-placement** |
| KTU exam mnemonic | "Estimate the *knob* directly" | "Estimate the *plant*, then *rebuild* the knob" |

### 1.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The overall loop of an indirect STR contains **four interacting blocks** operating every sampling period $k$:

1. **Plant** $G_p(z^{-1})$ — unknown, possibly time-varying.
2. **Parameter Estimator** — RLS, RELS, or ELS, producing $\hat{\theta}(k)$.
3. **Controller Designer (CE block)** — Solves a Diophantine / Riccati / pole-placement equation using $\hat{\theta}(k)$.
4. **Controller** $C(z^{-1}, \hat{\theta}(k))$ — Applies $u(k)$ to the plant.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Certainty-Equivalence (CE)** principle states: at each step, treat the current estimate $\hat{\theta}(k)$ as if it were the true parameter $\theta$, and design the controller accordingly. This is the conceptual foundation of every indirect STR.

### 1.5 Why Indirect STR Matters in Engineering

Indirect self-tuning regulators are the **workhorse of industrial adaptive control** because most process plants (chemical reactors, distillation columns, paper machines, rolling mills, robotic manipulators) have *slowly time-varying or poorly known* dynamics. By continuously re-identifying the plant, the controller self-compensates for drift, aging, and changing operating points — a feature no fixed-gain PID can deliver.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Plant Model — ARMAX Form

Almost all indirect STRs begin by writing the plant in the **AutoRegressive Moving Average with eXogenous input (ARMAX)** form:

$$A(z^{-1})\,y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,u(k-d) + C(z^{-1})\,\omega(k)$$

where $\omega(k)$ is a zero-mean white noise sequence with variance $\sigma^2$, and $d \geq 1$ is the **pure time delay** (transport delay) in samples.

The polynomial definitions are:

$$A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}$$

$$B(z^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$$

$$C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c}} z^{-n_{c}}$$

Forgetting the noise part gives the **ARX** model used in many practical STRs:

$$A(z^{-1})\,y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,u(k-d) + \omega(k)$$

### 2.2 The Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Estimator

Define the **regression vector** $\varphi(k)$ and the **parameter vector** $\theta$ as:

$$\varphi^T(k) = \left[-y(k-1), -y(k-2), \dots, -y(k-n_a), \, u(k-d), u(k-d-1), \dots, u(k-d-n_b)\right]$$

$$\theta^T = \left[a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n_a}, \, b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n_b}\right]$$

so that the ARX model becomes the **linear regression**:

$$y(k) = \varphi^T(k)\,\theta + \omega(k)$$

The RLS update equations (KTU board favourite) are:

$$ \hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,\left[y(k) - \varphi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)\right] $$

$$ K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)}{\lambda + \varphi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)} $$

$$ P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[I - K(k)\,\varphi^T(k)\right] P(k-1) $$

where $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ is the **forgetting factor** (commonly $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$). The matrix $P(k)$ is the parameter covariance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** When $\lambda < 1$, $P(k)$ can *blow up* if the input is persistently exciting only weakly. The KTU board often asks what happens if the plant is *not persistently excited* — answer: estimator may drift and the closed-loop becomes unstable.

### 2.3 Minimum-Variance (MV) Control Law — The Foundation of Self-Tuning MV

The celebrated **minimum-variance controller** (Åström, 1970) minimises $\mathbb{E}\{y^2(k+d)\}$. The derivation relies on the **Diophantine identity**:

$$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}\,G(z^{-1})$$

with

$$F(z^{-1}) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + \dots + f_{d-1} z^{-(d-1)}$$

$$G(z^{-1}) = g_0 + g_1 z^{-1} + \dots + g_{n_a - 1} z^{-(n_a-1)}$$

Substituting into the ARMAX model yields the **d-step ahead predictor**:

$$\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) = \frac{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,u(k) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,y(k)$$

The MV control law that drives $\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) = 0$ (for regulation) is:

$$F(z^{-1})\,B(z^{-1})\,u(k) + G(z^{-1})\,y(k) = 0 \quad \text{(regulation to zero)}$$

For **set-point tracking** $y^*(k)$:

$$F(z^{-1})\,B(z^{-1})\,u(k) + G(z^{-1})\,y(k) = C(z^{-1})\,y^*(k+d)$$

### 2.4 Indirect Self-Tuning MV Regulator — The Algorithm

The indirect self-tuning minimum-variance regulator **at every step $k$** does:

1. Read $y(k)$, refresh $\varphi(k)$.
2. Update $\hat{\theta}(k) = [\hat{a}_i, \hat{b}_i]$ using RLS.
3. Form $\hat{A}(z^{-1})$, $\hat{B}(z^{-1})$ from $\hat{\theta}(k)$.
4. Solve the **Diophantine equation** $\hat{C} = \hat{A} F + z^{-d} G$ for $F$ and $G$ (often $\hat{C}=1$ if noise whitening is assumed).
5. Compute $u(k)$ from the MV law using the **latest** $F$ and $G$.
6. Apply $u(k)$, shift $k \to k+1$, repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the name "self-tuning"?** Because the controller coefficients are *not* chosen once at design time — they are *continuously re-tuned* so as to maintain the MV (or pole-placement) property even as the plant drifts.

### 2.5 Indirect Self-Tuning Pole-Placement Regulator

When the plant has **non-minimum-phase zeros**, the MV controller becomes unstable. The robust alternative is the **self-tuning pole-placement** controller. The closed-loop target polynomial is $T(z^{-1})$ (designer-chosen), and the control law satisfies:

$$B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})\,u(k) + \left[T(z^{-1}) - B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})\right]\,y(k) = T(z^{-1})\,y^*(k)$$

where $F(z^{-1})$ solves the **Diophantine identity**:

$$A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}\,G(z^{-1}) = T(z^{-1})$$

with $\deg F = d-1$ and $\deg G = \deg A - 1$. The same algorithm as above is used, replacing the MV block with the pole-placement synthesis.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| ARMAX plant | $A\,y(k) = B\,u(k-d) + C\,\omega(k)$ | Universal plant model for STR |
| Regression | $y(k) = \varphi^T(k)\,\theta + \omega(k)$ | Enables linear estimation |
| RLS gain | $K(k) = P(k-1)\varphi(k)\big/\bigl[\lambda + \varphi^T P(k-1)\varphi\bigr]$ | Update direction |
| RLS update | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,e(k)$ | $e(k) = y(k) - \varphi^T \hat{\theta}(k-1)$ |
| Covariance | $P(k) = \lambda^{-1}\bigl[I - K\varphi^T\bigr]P(k-1)$ | Error covariance recursion |
| Diophantine | $C = A\,F + z^{-d} G$ | Core of MV / pole-placement design |
| MV regulator | $F\,B\,u + G\,y = 0$ | Regulation to zero |
| MV tracker | $F\,B\,u + G\,y = C\,y^*(k+d)$ | Set-point tracking |
| Pole-placement | $A F + z^{-d} G = T$ | Closed-loop polynomial $T$ |
| PP control law | $B F u + (T - B F) y = T y^*$ | Input computation |
| CE principle | $C(\cdot) = $ design at $\hat{\theta}$ as if true | Justifies indirect scheme |
| Stability | $\hat{\theta} \to \theta$ and persistent excitation $\Rightarrow$ closed-loop stable | Conditional result |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replace the vertical pipe** `\vert x \vert` (e.g., absolute value, determinants) with `\mid` when writing inside a markdown table — the system prohibits raw `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking the parser.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Petrochemical industry** — adaptive composition control in distillation columns where the operating point drifts due to feed changes.
* **Rolling mills** — self-tuning thickness control compensates for roll wear and temperature drift.
* **Ship autopilot** — indirect STRs adapt course-keeping to varying sea states and loading.
* **Robotic manipulators** — adaptive control handles unknown payloads.
* **Biomedical** — adaptive drug infusion controllers, anaesthesia depth control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the RLS Update Equations (Start to Finish)

We seek the parameter vector $\theta$ that minimises the **exponentially weighted cumulative squared error**:

$$J_N(\theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda^{N-k} \left[y(k) - \varphi^T(k)\,\theta\right]^2$$

Setting $\partial J_N / \partial \theta = 0$ and solving for $\theta$ analytically, the well-known **off-line least-squares solution** is:

$$\hat{\theta}_N = \left[\sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda^{N-k}\,\varphi(k)\varphi^T(k)\right]^{-1} \left[\sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda^{N-k}\,\varphi(k) y(k)\right]$$

Defining $P^{-1}(N) = \sum \lambda^{N-k}\varphi\varphi^T$, we obtain the **recursive** version by algebraic manipulation. The detailed steps are:

**Step 1 — Define the inverse covariance update.**
Start with $P^{-1}(k) = \lambda\,P^{-1}(k-1) + \varphi(k)\,\varphi^T(k)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the matrix inversion lemma.**
The Woodbury identity $(A + UCV)^{-1} = A^{-1} - A^{-1}U(C^{-1} + V A^{-1} U)^{-1} V A^{-1}$ applied with $A = \lambda P^{-1}(k-1)$, $U = \varphi(k)$, $C = 1$, $V = \varphi^T(k)$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(k) &= \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(k-1) - \frac{P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)\,\varphi^T(k)\,P(k-1)}{\lambda + \varphi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[I - K(k)\,\varphi^T(k)\bigr]\,P(k-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K(k) = P(k-1)\varphi(k)\big/\bigl[\lambda + \varphi^T P(k-1)\varphi\bigr]$.

**Step 3 — Derive the parameter update.**
Starting from $\hat{\theta}(k) = P(k)\bigl[\lambda P^{-1}(k-1)\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \varphi(k) y(k)\bigr]$ and substituting the Woodbury result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\theta}(k) &= \hat{\theta}(k-1) + P(k)\,\varphi(k)\left[y(k) - \varphi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)\right] \\
&= \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,e(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

with the **innovation** $e(k) = y(k) - \varphi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)$.

This completes the **RLS derivation**. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum-Variance Predictor

Starting from the ARMAX plant $A\,y(k) = B\,u(k-d) + C\,\omega(k)$, we multiply by $E(z^{-1}) / C(z^{-1})$ where $E$ is to be chosen. The classic route is the **Åström predictor**.

**Step 1 — Decompose $C/A$ as a partial fraction of delay $d$.**
There exist unique polynomials $F(z^{-1})$ and $G(z^{-1})$ of degrees $d-1$ and $n_a - 1$ such that:

$$ \frac{C(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})} = F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}\,\frac{G(z^{-1})}{A(z^{-1})} $$

Clearing denominators gives the **Diophantine equation**:

$$ C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}\,G(z^{-1}) $$

**Step 2 — Multiply the ARMAX by $F/C$ and rearrange.**
We obtain:

$$ y(k) = \frac{B\,F}{C}\,u(k-d) + \frac{G}{C}\,y(k-d) + F(z^{-1})\,\omega(k) $$

**Step 3 — Identify the d-step predictor.**
Since $F$ has degree $d-1$, the term $F(z^{-1})\omega(k)$ contains $\omega(k), \omega(k-1), \dots, \omega(k-d+1)$, **all of which are uncorrelated with future information available at time $k$**. Hence the conditional expectation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}(k+d \mid k) &= \frac{B(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,u(k) + \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}\,y(k) \\
\text{minimum variance } y(k+d) \text{ given past data}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimise the variance.**
The minimum of $\mathbb{E}\{y^2(k+d)\}$ is achieved when:

$$ F(z^{-1})\,B(z^{-1})\,u(k) + G(z^{-1})\,y(k) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y(k+d) = F(z^{-1})\,\omega(k+d) $$

The output becomes a **moving-average process of order $d-1$** of the noise — the minimum-variance property.

### 3.3 Indirect Self-Tuning MV — Closed-Form Control Law

Once $\hat{\theta}(k)$ gives $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$:

**Step 1.** Solve $\hat{C}(z^{-1}) = \hat{A}(z^{-1})\,F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}\,G(z^{-1})$ for $F, G$ (often with $\hat{C} \equiv 1$).

**Step 2.** Form the linear equation for $u(k)$:

$$ F(z^{-1})\,\hat{B}(z^{-1})\,u(k) = -G(z^{-1})\,y(k) \quad (\text{regulation}) $$

**Step 3.** Solve for $u(k)$ by moving all past $u$ terms to the right-hand side and dividing by the leading coefficient of $F \hat{B}$. In scalar form, if $F \hat{B} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 z^{-1} + \dots$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(k) &= \frac{1}{\beta_0}\left[-\beta_1 u(k-1) - \beta_2 u(k-2) - \dots - G(z^{-1})\,y(k)\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\beta_0}\left[-\sum_{i \geq 1} \beta_i\,u(k-i) - \sum_{j} g_j\,y(k-j)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **self-tuning minimum-variance law** that is updated every sampling instant. ✔

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Order of Operations per Sampling Step

| Step | Block | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor | Read $y(k)$ | Measurement |
| 2 | Estimator | Build $\varphi(k)$, update $P(k)$, compute $K(k)$ | Updated covariance |
| 3 | Estimator | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\bigl[y(k) - \varphi^T\hat{\theta}(k-1)\bigr]$ | New plant estimate |
| 4 | Designer | Solve Diophantine with $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ | $F, G$ |
| 5 | Controller | Compute $u(k)$ from MV or PP law | Control signal |
| 6 | Actuator | Apply $u(k)$ to plant | New state |
| 7 | Loop | $k \to k+1$ | Repeat |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Indirect Self-Tuning Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[R REF] --> sumA[SUM E]
    sumA --> plantY[PLANT GP]
    plantY -- y k --> sumA
    plantY -- y k --> est[PARAM ESTIMATOR RLS]
    est -- theta hat --> design[CONTROLLER DESIGNER DIOPHANTINE]
    design -- F G --> ctrl[CONTROLLER LAW]
    ctrl -- u k --> plantY
    r --> ctrl
```

### 4.2 Information Flow Within the Indirect STR

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LoopA[IDENTIFICATION LOOP]
      yk[Y K] --> reg[REGRESSION PHI K]
      reg --> rls[RLS UPDATE]
      rls --> Pnew[COVARIANCE P K]
      rls --> th[THETA HAT K]
    end
    subgraph LoopB[CONTROL SYNTHESIS LOOP]
      th --> dio[DIOPHANTINE SOLVER]
      dio --> FG[F AND G]
      FG --> law[CONTROL LAW U K]
      yk --> law
    end
    law --> act[ACTUATOR]
    act --> pl[PLANT]
    pl --> yk
```

### 4.3 Algorithm State Machine of the Self-Tuner

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> ESTIMATE : Read y k
    ESTIMATE --> DESIGN : Theta updated
    DESIGN --> CONTROL : F G computed
    CONTROL --> ESTIMATE : Apply u k and wait one sample
    ESTIMATE --> ALARM : Persistent excitation lost
    ALARM --> INIT : Reset
    CONTROL --> [*] : Shut down
```

### 4.4 Mapping of Indirect STR Components to Industrial Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s[SENSOR PT OR TT] --> dcs[DCS CONTROLLER]
    dcs --> rlsb[RLS SOFTWARE MODULE]
    rlsb --> des[DESIGN MODULE]
    des --> pid[ADAPTIVE PID U K]
    pid --> v[VALVE OR VFD]
    v --> plant[PROCESS PLANT]
    plant --> s
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-level functional diagrams** are used (instead of physical sketches) because the indirect STR's essence is the *information flow* between estimation, design, and control — not the geometry of any single component.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is meant by an indirect self-tuning regulator? Distinguish it from a direct adaptive controller."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **indirect self-tuning regulator (STR)** is an adaptive control scheme in which the unknown plant parameters are first estimated on-line using a recursive estimator (RLS/RELS), and then the controller parameters are **recomputed** at every sampling instant from these estimates by solving a design equation (Diophantine, Riccati, etc.). Because the design step is performed explicitly on the identified model, the scheme is called *indirect*.
A **direct adaptive controller**, in contrast, estimates the *controller parameters* directly, with the parameter adaptation law absorbing the design step implicitly. The MIT rule and the classical MRAC are direct schemes. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"State the Certainty Equivalence (CE) principle used in indirect self-tuning regulators."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Certainty Equivalence (CE) principle** states that, at every sampling instant, the current estimated plant parameters $\hat{\theta}(k)$ are *treated as if* they were the true parameters $\theta$ when designing the controller. In other words, the controller $C(\hat{\theta}(k))$ is computed as if $\hat{\theta}(k) = \theta$. The CE principle is the conceptual foundation of indirect adaptive control: it decouples estimation from design, allowing the two problems to be solved separately. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **indirect self-tuning minimum-variance regulator** for an ARMAX plant. Clearly show the role of the parameter estimator, the controller designer, and the control law. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the plant
$A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.9 z^{-1}, \; B(z^{-1}) = 0.5, \; d = 1, \; C(z^{-1}) = 1$, derive the **minimum-variance control law** using the Diophantine equation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of the Indirect Self-Tuning MV Regulator (7 marks):**

1. **Plant model** — ARMAX: $A\,y(k) = B\,u(k-d) + C\,\omega(k)$ (1 mark).
2. **Block diagram** — Plant → Sensor → Regression vector → RLS Estimator → Designer (Diophantine) → Controller law → Actuator → Plant (2 marks).
3. **Per-step operation**:
   * Read $y(k)$, form $\varphi(k)$.
   * Update $\hat{\theta}(k)$ via RLS.
   * Solve $C = A F + z^{-d} G$ using $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$.
   * Compute $u(k)$ from $F B u + G y = 0$. (3 marks)
4. **CE principle** explicitly invoked, noting that the controller is "self-tuned" because the design is re-run every sample. (1 mark)

**(b) Derivation of the MV Law (7 marks):**

Given $A = 1 - 0.9 z^{-1}$, $B = 0.5$, $d = 1$, $C = 1$. With $d = 1$, the polynomial $F$ has degree $d - 1 = 0$, i.e. $F = f_0$ (a constant). Also $G$ has degree $n_a - 1 = 0$, so $G = g_0$.

The Diophantine equation $C = A F + z^{-1} G$ becomes:

$$ 1 = (1 - 0.9 z^{-1})\,f_0 + z^{-1}\,g_0 $$

Equating coefficients of $z^{0}$ and $z^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z^{0} &: \quad 1 = f_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_0 = 1 \\
z^{-1} &: \quad 0 = -0.9 f_0 + g_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; g_0 = 0.9
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $F = 1$ and $G = 0.9$. Substituting into the MV law $F B u(k) + G y(k) = 0$:

$$ (1)(0.5)\,u(k) + 0.9\,y(k) = 0 $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5\,u(k) &= -0.9\,y(k) \\
u(k) &= -1.8\,y(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

This **proportional** law is the **minimum-variance control law** for the given plant. ✔ (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS) algorithm** with equations. Discuss the role of the **forgetting factor** $\lambda$ and the condition of **persistent excitation**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A first-order plant is given by $y(k) = 0.8\,y(k-1) + b_0\,u(k-1) + \omega(k)$, with $b_0$ unknown. Using **one step of RLS** with initial $\hat{\theta}(0) = 0.5$ and $P(0) = 100$, $\lambda = 1$, given $u(0) = 1$, $y(1) = 1.5$, find $\hat{b}_0(1)$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RLS Algorithm — Theory (7 marks):**

Define regression $\varphi(k)$ and parameter vector $\theta$ so that $y(k) = \varphi^T(k)\theta + \omega(k)$. (1 mark)

RLS equations (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(k) &= \frac{P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)}{\lambda + \varphi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\,\varphi(k)} \\
\hat{\theta}(k) &= \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\bigl[y(k) - \varphi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)\bigr] \\
P(k) &= \frac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[I - K(k)\,\varphi^T(k)\bigr]P(k-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forgetting factor** $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ (typically $0.95$–$0.99$): weights past data less, allowing the estimator to *track* time-varying parameters. $\lambda = 1$ gives standard RLS (no forgetting). If $\lambda$ is too low, $P(k)$ blows up. (2 marks)

**Persistent excitation (PE)**: an input is PE of order $n$ if the limit $\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \varphi(k) \varphi^T(k)$ is positive definite. Without PE, $\hat{\theta}$ does not converge and the closed-loop may be unstable. (2 marks)

**(b) One-step RLS Update (7 marks):**

Plant: $y(k) = 0.8 y(k-1) + b_0 u(k-1) + \omega(k)$.
Hence $\varphi^T(k) = [y(k-1), \, u(k-1)]$ and $\theta = [0.8, \, b_0]^T$.

At $k = 1$, with $u(0) = 1$ and $y(0)$ not given, we focus on the parameter $b_0$ alone. Treat the simplified regression $y(1) = b_0 u(0) + \omega(1)$, so $\varphi(1) = u(0) = 1$ and we want to update the scalar $\hat{b}_0$. (1 mark)

**Prior prediction error:**
$e(1) = y(1) - \varphi(1)\,\hat{\theta}(0) = 1.5 - (1)(0.5) = 1.0$ (1 mark)

**Gain:**
$K(1) = P(0) \varphi(1) / [\lambda + \varphi^2(1) P(0)] = (100)(1) / [1 + (1)(100)] = 100/101 \approx 0.9901$ (2 marks)

**Update:**
$\hat{b}_0(1) = \hat{\theta}(0) + K(1) e(1) = 0.5 + 0.9901 \times 1.0 \approx 1.4901$ (2 marks)

**Covariance:**
$P(1) = [P(0) - K(1) \varphi(1) P(0)] = 100 - 0.9901 \times 100 = 0.99$ (1 mark)

Hence $\hat{b}_0(1) \approx 1.49$. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Indirect STR):**
> * Forgetting to define the **regression vector** $\varphi(k)$ before writing the RLS update — *loses 1 mark*.
> * Confusing the **direct** and **indirect** schemes in the definition — *loses 1 mark*.
> * Failing to state the **degrees** of $F$ and $G$ in the Diophantine equation ($\deg F = d-1$, $\deg G = n_a - 1$) — *loses 1 mark*.
> * Writing MV law for a **non-minimum-phase** plant without switching to pole-placement — *loses 2 marks*.
> * Forgetting the **CE principle** while describing the indirect STR — *loses 1 mark*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Indirect STR = recursive estimation + on-line control design.** Direct STR estimates controller parameters directly.
- The **ARMAX / ARX model** is the universal starting point; the order $n_a, n_b, d$ must be fixed a priori (over-parameterisation is sometimes safer).
- The **RLS update** is the heart of the estimator — remember the three equations (gain, parameter, covariance) and the role of $\lambda$.
- The **Certainty Equivalence (CE) principle** justifies treating $\hat{\theta}$ as $\theta$ for design.
- The **Diophantine equation** $C = A F + z^{-d} G$ is the bridge from plant estimate to controller law.
- **Minimum-variance law**: $F B u + G y = 0$ — but use **pole-placement** when the plant is non-minimum-phase.
- The estimator converges only under **persistent excitation**; otherwise the closed loop may destabilise.
- **Forgetting factor** $\lambda < 1$ enables tracking of slow drift but risks covariance blow-up.
- Industrial applications: distillation columns, rolling mills, ship autopilots, robotic manipulators, biomedical infusion.
- KTU 2024 examiner expects: clear block diagram, well-defined regression vector, explicit Diophantine solution, and a final closed-form $u(k)$ expression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Time Self Tuners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Time Self Tuners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuous Time Self Tuner** (also known as a **Continuous-Time Self-Tuning Regulator, CT-STR**) is an adaptive control architecture in which both the **parameter identification** of the unknown/stochastic plant and the **control law synthesis** are executed continuously (in the continuous time-domain, $t$), as opposed to operating on sampled discrete-time measurements. It is the natural continuous-time counterpart of the Åström–Wittenmark discrete self-tuner.

The plant is modelled as a linear, time-invariant, SISO continuous-time system with a known structure but unknown parameters:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{B(s)}{A(s)} = \frac{b_0 s^m + b_1 s^{m-1} + \dots + b_m}{s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n}, \quad n > m$$

The identifier continuously estimates the parameter vector $\hat{\theta}(t) = [\hat{a}_1(t), \dots, \hat{a}_n(t), \hat{b}_0(t), \dots, \hat{b}_m(t)]^T$, and the **design calculator** uses $\hat{\theta}(t)$ to update the controller parameters on-the-fly so that the closed-loop dynamics match a pre-specified reference model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Continuous-time self-tuners occupy a central place in adaptive control theory. They bridge the gap between classical continuous-time control design (Bode, Nyquist, Root-Locus) and modern parameter-adaptive schemes, allowing direct placement of poles in the $s$-plane rather than the $z$-plane.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Autonomous Driving Assistant":**
> Imagine you are driving a car whose steering response changes as fuel is consumed, tyres wear, and the road surface shifts from dry asphalt to wet mud. A **continuous-time self-tuner** is the equivalent of an *autonomous driving assistant that recalculates the steering gain 100 times every second*. It uses a real-time mathematical "guess" of the car's current handling physics and immediately re-tunes the steering controller — never waiting for a snapshot every few seconds (which is what a discrete-time self-tuner would do). The result is a smoother, latency-free ride even as conditions drift.

### 1.3 Why Continuous-Time? (Key Motivations)

- **High-bandwidth processes** (motor drives, flight control, hydraulic actuators) require sub-millisecond adaptation — sampling introduces unavoidable aliasing and delay.
- **Direct physical interpretation** in the $s$-plane (poles, damping, natural frequency).
- **Avoids ZOH (Zero-Order Hold) distortion** inherent to sampled-data systems.
- The design calculator can use classical tools: pole-placement, LQG, root-locus — *all natively defined in continuous time*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Continuous-Time Adaptive Control

- **Adaptation gain** $\gamma$ — typically in the range **$\mathbf{1 \le \gamma \le 10^4}$**.
- **Sampling/observation period** of identifier $T_0$ — for CT schemes, parameter updates occur every $\Delta t \to 0$ in the limit (practically, every microprocessor tick).
- **$\sigma$-modification factor** $\sigma \in [\mathbf{0.001, 0.1}]$ — prevents parameter drift.
- **Normalisation signal** $m(t) = \sqrt{1 + \phi^T(t)\phi(t)}$ — keeps the identification law bounded.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Continuous-Time Self Tuner

The CT self-tuner is composed of **three interconnected functional blocks** operating in the $s$-domain:

1. **Re-parameterised Plant (Estimator Model):**
   Filters the input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$ through stable filters $\frac{1}{\Lambda(s)}$ to construct the regression vector $\phi(t)$, eliminating derivatives of noisy signals.
2. **Recursive Parameter Estimator:**
   Continuously updates $\hat{\theta}(t)$ using a continuous-time gradient or least-squares law.
3. **Design Calculator / Control Law:**
   Synthesises the controller $C(s,t)$ (whose coefficients are functions of $\hat{\theta}(t)$) using the **Certainty Equivalence Principle** — treating the current estimate as if it were the true parameter.

### 2.2 The Re-Parameterised Estimator Model

A common formulation (the **Landau / MRAC form**) introduces a stable monic Hurwitz polynomial $\Lambda(s) = s^n + \lambda_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + \lambda_n$ and writes the plant as:

$$y(t) = \frac{B(s)}{A(s)}u(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{m} b_i s^{m-i}}{\sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i s^{n-i}} u(t), \quad a_0 = 1$$

Multiplying both sides by $\frac{1}{\Lambda(s)}$:

$$\frac{s^n}{\Lambda(s)} y(t) = \theta^{*T} \phi(t)$$

where the regression vector is:

$$\phi^T(t) = \left[ \frac{s^{n-1}}{\Lambda(s)} u(t), \dots, \frac{s^{m}}{\Lambda(s)} u(t), \frac{-s^{n-1}}{\Lambda(s)} y(t), \dots, \frac{-s}{\Lambda(s)} y(t), \frac{-1}{\Lambda(s)} y(t) \right]$$

and $\theta^* = [b_0, b_1, \dots, b_m, a_1, \dots, a_n]^T$ is the **true (unknown) parameter vector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Filtering by $\frac{1}{\Lambda(s)}$ is *not optional*. It converts the unmeasurable derivatives of $y$ and $u$ into measurable filtered signals — a critical engineering trick that makes continuous-time identification implementable with only input/output measurements.

### 2.3 Continuous-Time Identification Laws

#### (a) Continuous-Time Gradient (MIT-Rule in CT)
$$\boxed{\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\gamma \, \phi(t) \, \varepsilon(t), \quad \varepsilon(t) = \frac{s^n}{\Lambda(s)}y(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t)\phi(t)}$$

#### (b) Continuous-Time Normalised Gradient
$$\boxed{\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\gamma \, \frac{\phi(t) \, \varepsilon(t)}{1 + \phi^T(t)\phi(t)}}$$

#### (c) Continuous-Time Recursive Least Squares (CT-RLS)
$$\boxed{\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -P(t)\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t), \quad \dot{P}(t) = P(t) - P(t)\phi(t)\phi^T(t)P(t)}$$

$P(t)$ is the **covariance matrix** — a positive-definite matrix in $\mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ where $p = n+m+1$.

#### (d) CT-RLS with $\sigma$-Modification (for robustness)
$$\dot{P}(t) = \beta P(t) - P(t)\phi(t)\phi^T(t)P(t), \quad \beta \in [0,1]$$

### 2.4 The Certainty Equivalence Control Law (Pole-Placement Form)

Once $\hat{\theta}(t)$ is available, the design calculator solves a **Diophantine equation in the $s$-domain**:

$$\boxed{A(s)\,L(s) + B(s)\,P(s) = A_m(s)}$$

where $A_m(s)$ is the desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial. The controller takes the standard RST/2-DOF form:

$$U(s) = \frac{P(s)}{L(s)} R(s) - \frac{Q(s)}{L(s)} Y(s)$$

In the CT self-tuner, the coefficients of $L(s)$ and $P(s)$ are *re-computed every adaptation step* using $\hat{\theta}(t)$ instead of $\theta^*$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Domain |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | CT Plant Model | $Y(s)/U(s) = B(s)/A(s)$ | $s$-plane, deg $A = n$, deg $B = m$ |
| 2 | Regressor Filter | $\phi_i(t) = \frac{s^{n-i}}{\Lambda(s)}u(t)$ or $y(t)$ | Volts / (rad/s)^i |
| 3 | Estimation Error | $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{s^n}{\Lambda(s)}y - \hat{\theta}^T \phi$ | Volts |
| 4 | CT Gradient Law | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \phi \varepsilon$ | $\theta$-units / s |
| 5 | CT-RLS Law | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -P \phi \varepsilon$ | $\theta$-units / s |
| 6 | Covariance Update | $\dot{P} = P - P\phi\phi^T P$ | dimensionless / s |
| 7 | Pole-Placement Diophantine | $A L + B P = A_m$ | polynomials in $s$ |
| 8 | Controller Law | $U(s) = \frac{P(s)}{L(s)}[R(s) - Y(s)]$ | Volts |
| 9 | Reference Model | $Y_m(s) = \frac{B_m(s)}{A_m(s)} R(s)$ | $s$-plane |
| 10 | Tracking Error | $e(t) = y(t) - y_m(t)$ | Volts |
| 11 | Adaptation Gain | $\gamma > 0$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\sigma$-Modification | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \phi \varepsilon - \sigma \hat{\theta}$ | $\theta$-units / s |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic manipulators:** Joint-friction compensation when payload is unknown.
- **Aircraft autopilots:** Re-trim during altitude / Mach changes (CT self-tuners pioneered by researchers at NASA and Toulouse).
- **Ship-steering autopilots:** Wave-disturbance rejection (one of the earliest industrial applications, ~1980s).
- **High-speed CNC machining:** Spindle-speed adaptation to varying tool wear.
- **Power-system stabilisers:** Damping of inter-area oscillations when line impedance is uncertain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous-Time Gradient Identification Law

**Starting point:** The filtered plant equation,

$$\frac{s^n}{\Lambda(s)} y(t) = \theta^{*T} \phi(t)$$

Define the *a-posteriori* prediction of the filtered output as $\hat{y}_f(t) = \hat{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t)$ and the *a-priori* estimation error,

$$\varepsilon(t) = y_f(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t) \phi(t), \quad y_f(t) \equiv \frac{s^n}{\Lambda(s)} y(t)$$

**Step 1 — Choose a quadratic cost function** in continuous time:

$$J(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^2(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[y_f(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t)\phi(t)\right]^2$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient of $J$ w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = -\phi(t) \left[y_f(t) - \hat{\theta}^T(t)\phi(t)\right] = -\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuous-time gradient descent** (steepest descent on $J$):

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = -\gamma\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)$$

with $\gamma > 0$ as the adaptation gain. This is the **CT gradient law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The discrete-time update $\hat{\theta}(k+1) = \hat{\theta}(k) - \gamma \nabla J$ becomes, as $T_s \to 0$, a continuous-time ODE: $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -(\gamma/T_s)\nabla J$. The redefinition $\gamma \leftarrow \gamma/T_s$ yields the canonical CT form.

### 3.2 Derivation of the CT Recursive Least Squares Law

Begin with the CT cost (integral of squared error over $[0, t]$):

$$J(\hat{\theta}, t) = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^t \left[y_f(\tau) - \hat{\theta}^T\phi(\tau)\right]^2 d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $J$ w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}$ and set to zero** (least-squares optimality condition):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = -\int_0^t \phi(\tau)\left[y_f(\tau) - \hat{\theta}^T \phi(\tau)\right] d\tau = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the (integral) covariance matrix:**

$$R(t) = \int_0^t \phi(\tau)\phi^T(\tau) d\tau, \quad Q(t) = \int_0^t \phi(\tau) y_f(\tau) d\tau$$

so that the optimal estimate satisfies:

$$R(t)\,\hat{\theta}(t) = Q(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \hat{\theta}(t) = R^{-1}(t)\,Q(t)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\hat{\theta}(t) = R^{-1}Q$ w.r.t. $t$** (this is the crux of the derivation):

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = \dot{R^{-1}}(t)\,Q(t) + R^{-1}(t)\,\dot{Q}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Use the matrix inversion lemma** (Woodbury) and the identities $\dot{R}(t) = \phi(t)\phi^T(t)$ and $\dot{Q}(t) = \phi(t) y_f(t)$:

$$\dot{R^{-1}} = -R^{-1} \dot{R} R^{-1} = -R^{-1} \phi \phi^T R^{-1}$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -R^{-1}(t)\phi(t)\phi^T(t)R^{-1}(t)Q(t) + R^{-1}(t)\phi(t)\,y_f(t)$$

**Step 5 — Recognise** that $R^{-1}Q = \hat{\theta}$ and that $y_f - \hat{\theta}^T\phi = \varepsilon$:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -R^{-1}(t)\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t)$$

**Step 6 — Define $P(t) \equiv R^{-1}(t)$** (so $P$ is the inverse of the integrated regressor covariance). Then:

$$\boxed{\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -P(t)\,\phi(t)\,\varepsilon(t), \quad \dot{P}(t) = P(t) - P(t)\phi(t)\phi^T(t)P(t)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Self-Tuning Pole-Placement Law

Assume the controller structure:

$$L(s)\,U(s) = P(s)\,[R(s) - Y(s)]$$

Closed-loop transfer function (from $R$ to $Y$):

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{B(s)\,P(s)}{A(s)\,L(s) + B(s)\,P(s)}$$

**Step 1 — Specify the desired closed-loop polynomial** $A_m(s)$ (Hurwitz, degree $\le n + m - 1$).

**Step 2 — Impose the design equation:**

$$A(s)\,L(s) + B(s)\,P(s) = A_m(s)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Diophantine equation** for unknown polynomials $L(s)$ and $P(s)$ of degrees $n-1$ and $m-1$ respectively. With $A$ degree $n$ and $B$ degree $m$:

$$A(s)\,L(s) = (s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n)(l_0 s^{n-1} + l_1 s^{n-2} + \dots + l_{n-1})$$
$$B(s)\,P(s) = (b_0 s^m + b_1 s^{m-1} + \dots + b_m)(p_0 s^{m-1} + p_1 s^{m-2} + \dots + p_{m-1})$$

The LHS must equal $A_m(s)$, of degree $n+m-1$. Equating coefficients of $s^k$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n+m-1$ yields a **linear system of $n+m$ equations** in $n+m$ unknowns $(l_0, \dots, l_{n-1}, p_0, \dots, p_{m-1})$.

**Step 4 — Certainty-Equivalence step:** Replace the *true* coefficients $(a_i, b_i)$ by the *current estimates* $(\hat{a}_i(t), \hat{b}_i(t))$. The Diophantine equation is re-solved at every adaptation step, producing time-varying polynomials $L(s,t)$ and $P(s,t)$.

$$\boxed{\hat{A}(s,t)\,L(s,t) + \hat{B}(s,t)\,P(s,t) = A_m(s)}$$

### 3.4 Stability Argument (Lyapunov)

Define the parameter-error vector $\tilde{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t) - \theta^*$ and the candidate Lyapunov function:

$$V(\tilde{\theta}, P) = \frac{1}{2}\tilde{\theta}^T P^{-1}(t) \tilde{\theta}$$

Differentiating and using $\dot{P} = P - P\phi\phi^T P$:

$$\dot{V} = \tilde{\theta}^T P^{-1}\dot{\tilde{\theta}} - \frac{1}{2}\tilde{\theta}^T P^{-1}\dot{P}\,P^{-1}\tilde{\theta}$$

$$= -\tilde{\theta}^T\phi\,\varepsilon - \frac{1}{2}\tilde{\theta}^T\phi\phi^T \tilde{\theta}$$

$$= -\varepsilon \phi^T \tilde{\theta} - \frac{1}{2}(\phi^T \tilde{\theta})^2$$

Since $\varepsilon = y_f - \hat{\theta}^T\phi = -\phi^T\tilde{\theta}$, we get $\phi^T\tilde{\theta} = -\varepsilon$:

$$\dot{V} = \varepsilon^2 - \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon^2 \ge 0$$

Wait — this signals potential instability! The cure is **normalisation** by $m^2(t) = 1 + \phi^T\phi$ or the addition of a **$\sigma$-modification** term. With normalisation:

$$\dot{V} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon^2}{1+\phi^T\phi} \le 0$$

which guarantees $\tilde{\theta} \in \mathcal{L}_\infty$ (bounded parameters) — a foundational KTU result.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Continuous-Time Self Tuner

```mermaid
graph TD
    refSig[Reference Input R of t] --> sumAdd[Summing Junction]
    sumAdd --> ctrlOut[Controller U of t]
    ctrlOut --> plantBlock[Unknown Plant B of s over A of s]
    plantBlock --> yMeas[Measured Output Y of t]
    plantBlock --> yDot[Filtered Output Yf via Lambda of s]
    ctrlOut --> uFilt[Filtered Input Uf via Lambda of s]
    yMeas --> fbPath[Feedback Path]
    fbPath --> sumAdd
    yDot --> regVec[Regressor Vector Phi of t]
    uFilt --> regVec
    regVec --> estErr[Estimation Error Epsilon of t]
    estErr --> idLaw[CT RLS or Gradient Identifier]
    idLaw --> thetaHat[Estimated Parameters Theta hat of t]
    thetaHat --> desCalc[Design Calculator Solves Diophantine]
    desCalc --> ctrlOut
    yMeas --> perErr[Tracking Error E of t equals Y minus Ym]
    refSig --> refMod[Reference Model Bm over Am]
    refMod --> perErr
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow of the CT Identification Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    yIn[Process Output Y] --> filtY[Filter one over Lambda of s]
    uIn[Control Signal U] --> filtU[Filter one over Lambda of s]
    filtY --> phi1[Phi components from Y]
    filtU --> phi2[Phi components from U]
    phi1 --> regVec[Regressor Phi of t]
    phi2 --> regVec
    regVec --> dotProd[Inner Product Theta hat transpose Phi]
    dotProd --> pred[Predicted Filtered Output Yhat f]
    filtY --> errComp[Comparator]
    pred --> errComp
    errComp --> epsilon[Estimation Error Epsilon]
    epsilon --> gainMul[Multiply by Phi]
    epsilon --> gainMul2[Multiply by Covariance P]
    gainMul --> gradUpdate[Gradient Descent Step]
    gainMul2 --> rlsUpdate[CT RLS Update]
    gradUpdate --> thetaUpdate[Update Theta hat]
    rlsUpdate --> thetaUpdate
    thetaUpdate --> dotProd
```

### 4.3 Self-Tuning Loop Topology (Identification ⇄ Control)

```mermaid
graph TD
    loopA[Identification Loop updates Theta hat] --> loopB[Certainty Equivalence Principle]
    loopB --> loopC[Controller Recalculation]
    loopC --> loopD[New Control Signal U of t]
    loopD --> loopE[Plant Response Y of t]
    loopE --> loopF[Filtered Regressors Phi]
    loopF --> loopA
    loopD --> loopG[Closed Loop Poles converge to Am]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The plant is a "black box" but the regressor-filtering and estimator together emulate a real-time *observer* for the unknown plant dynamics. The "design calculator" block is where the Diophantine equation is re-solved at every step — it is the *brain* of the self-tuner.

### 4.4 Detailed Block Diagram (Engineering Schematic View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    rSig[R(s)] --> sJ1[Summing Node e1]
    sJ1 --> ctrlS[Controller P(s)/L(s)]
    ctrlS --> uSig[U(s)]
    uSig --> plantDyn[Plant B(s)/A(s)]
    plantDyn --> ySig[Y(s)]
    ySig --> fbJ[Summing Node e2]
    fbJ --> sJ1
    ySig --> lambdaF[1 over Lambda of s]
    uSig --> lambdaF2[1 over Lambda of s]
    lambdaF --> ident[Identifier dTheta hat over dt]
    lambdaF2 --> ident
    ident --> dCalc[Design Calculator]
    dCalc --> ctrlS
    ySig --> refMod[Reference Model Bm(s)/Am(s)]
    rSig --> refMod
    refMod --> eTrack[Tracking Error]
    ySig --> eTrack
```
