## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Minimum Variance Control problem and write the corresponding control law for an ARMAX plant with delay $d$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Minimum Variance Control problem is to choose the control signal $u(t)$ at every instant such that the conditional expectation of the squared $d$-step-ahead output, $J = E\{y^2(t+d) \mid t\}$, is minimized.

For the ARMAX plant $A y(t) = z^{-d} B u(t) + C e(t)$, the solution involves solving the Diophantine equation $C = F A + z^{-d} G$ (with $\deg F = d-1$). The MVC law is then:

$$F(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1}) u(t) + G(z^{-1}) y(t) = 0$$

The achieved minimum variance is $E\{y^2(t+d)\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 \sum_{i=0}^{d-1} f_i^2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator? Differentiate between direct and indirect self-tuners.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Stochastic Self-Tuning Regulator is an adaptive controller that combines a recursive parameter estimator (RLS / ELS) with a stochastic control law (typically MVC or GMV) so that the closed loop automatically tracks slowly time-varying plant dynamics.

| Aspect | Indirect STR | Direct STR |
|---|---|---|
| Estimated parameters | Plant $(A, B, C)$ | Controller $(R, S)$ |
| Diophantine solve at runtime | Yes (every step) | No (off-line / never) |
| Computational load | Higher | Lower |
| Implementation complexity | More | Less |
| Re-parametrization | Not needed | Predictor rewritten in $Ru = -Sy$ form |

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-3 Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the minimum-variance $d$-step-ahead predictor for the ARMAX plant
$$A(z^{-1})y(t) = z^{-d}B(z^{-1})u(t) + C(z^{-1})e(t)$$
and state the corresponding control law that minimizes $E\{y^2(t+d)\}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the plant
$$y(t) - 0.7 y(t-1) + 0.1 y(t-2) = 0.5 u(t-2) + 0.2 u(t-3) + e(t) - 0.4 e(t-1)$$
identify $A, B, C, d$ and design the MVC. Compute the achieved minimum output variance if $\sigma_e^2 = 1.5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Diophantine identity.**
Obtain unique polynomials $F$ of degree $d-1$ and $G$ such that $C = F A + z^{-d} G$ (long division).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Predictor derivation.**
Multiply plant by $F$:
$$F A y(t) = z^{-d} F B u(t) + F C e(t)$$
Substitute $F A = C - z^{-d} G$:
$$C y(t) = z^{-d} G y(t) + z^{-d} F B u(t) + F C e(t)$$
Solve for $y(t+d)$ and take $E\{\cdot \mid t\}$ (kills future $e$ terms):
$$\hat y(t+d \mid t) = \frac{G}{C} y(t) + \frac{F B}{C} u(t)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Cost minimization.**
$$J = E\{y^2(t+d)\} = \hat y^2(t+d\mid t) + \sigma_e^2 \sum f_i^2$$
The second term is constant in $u$, so $\min J \Leftrightarrow \hat y(t+d\mid t) = 0$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Control law.**
$$F(z^{-1})B(z^{-1})u(t) + G(z^{-1})y(t) = 0$$

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Identification.**
$$A = 1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}, \quad B = 0.5 + 0.2z^{-1}, \quad C = 1 - 0.4z^{-1}, \quad d = 2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Diophantine for $d=2$.**
$F$ is degree 1, $G$ is degree 1:
$$C = F A + z^{-2} G$$
$$1 - 0.4 z^{-1} = (f_0 + f_1 z^{-1})(1 - 0.7 z^{-1} + 0.1 z^{-2}) + z^{-2}(g_0 + g_1 z^{-1})$$

Expanding the right side:
$$f_0 + (f_1 - 0.7 f_0) z^{-1} + (0.1 f_0 - 0.7 f_1) z^{-2} + 0.1 f_1 z^{-3} + g_0 z^{-2} + g_1 z^{-3}$$

Coefficient matching:
- $z^0$: $f_0 = 1$
- $z^{-1}$: $f_1 - 0.7(1) = -0.4 \Rightarrow f_1 = 0.3$
- $z^{-2}$: $0.1(1) - 0.7(0.3) + g_0 = 0 \Rightarrow 0.1 - 0.21 + g_0 = 0 \Rightarrow g_0 = 0.11$
- $z^{-3}$: $0.1(0.3) + g_1 = 0 \Rightarrow g_1 = -0.03$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — MVC law.**
$$F B = (1 + 0.3 z^{-1})(0.5 + 0.2 z^{-1}) = 0.5 + 0.35 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2}$$
Law: $(0.5 + 0.35 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2})u(t) + (0.11 - 0.03 z^{-1})y(t) = 0$, i.e.,
$$u(t) = -0.7 u(t-1) - 0.12 u(t-2) - 0.22 y(t) + 0.06 y(t-1)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Minimum variance.**
$$E\{y^2(t+2)\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 (f_0^2 + f_1^2) = 1.5(1 + 0.09) = 1.5 \times 1.09 = 1.635$$

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the design of a Moving-Average (Generalized Minimum Variance) controller. Discuss the role of the horizon $T$ and the polynomials $P, Q, R'$ in shaping the closed-loop response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the unification of direct self-tuning regulators. Show that the direct STR regression form can be obtained from the Minimum Variance Control law by a re-parametrization that avoids solving the Diophantine equation at every step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — GMV cost function.**
$$\phi(t+d) = P y(t+d) - Q y^*(t+d) + R' u(t), \quad J = E\{\phi^2(t+d)\}$$
This is the *auxiliary output* (Clarke & Gawthrop, 1975).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Diophantine for $P C$.**
Solve $P C = F_P A + z^{-d} G_P$ with $\deg F_P = d-1$. The $d$-step predictor of $\phi$ is
$$\hat\phi = \frac{G_P}{C} y + \frac{F_P B + R' C}{C} u - Q y^*$$
Setting $\hat\phi = 0$ gives the GMV law
$$[R' C + F_P B] u = Q C y^* - G_P y$$
Canonical $R u = -S y + T y^*$ with $R = R' C + F_P B$, $S = G_P$, $T = Q C$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Role of $T$ (horizon).**
Choose $P(z^{-1}) = F_T(z^{-1})$, the first $T$ terms of $1/A$. The implicit MA filter averages the disturbance over $T$ steps. $T = d$ recovers MVC; $T > d$ gives a smoother, more robust controller at the cost of slightly higher output variance.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Role of $P, Q, R'$.**
- $P$: output filter (shapes sensitivity and disturbance rejection).
- $Q$: setpoint tracking weight.
- $R'$: control weighting — large $R'$ gives a sluggish, robust controller; small $R'$ gives an aggressive minimum-variance-like behavior.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Canonical MVC form.**
Rewrite the MVC law $F B u + G y = 0$ as $R u = -S y$ with $R = F B$ and $S = G$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Re-parametrized predictor.**
Using the predictor $\hat y = (G y + F B u)/C$ and writing $C = 1 + z^{-1} C^*$ gives:
$$y(t+d) = S y(t) + R u(t) + \eta(t+1)$$
where $\eta(t+1) = C(z^{-1}) F(z^{-1}) e(t+d)$ is a moving-average noise.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Linear regression.**
Stack the unknown coefficients of $R$ and $S$ into $\theta$, and the corresponding lagged $u$ and $y$ samples into $\varphi(t)$:
$$y(t+d) = \theta^T \varphi(t) + \eta(t+1)$$
Apply RLS to estimate $\hat\theta(t)$ directly. The control law $u(t) = -\hat S(t) y(t)/\hat R(t)$ requires *no* Diophantine solve.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Unification statement.**
This shows that MVC, GMV (with $P, Q, R'$), and pole-placement self-tuners are all special cases of a *single* canonical structure
$$R(z^{-1}) u(t) = -S(z^{-1}) y(t) + T(z^{-1}) y^*(t+d)$$
with cost $J = E\{\phi^2(t+d)\}$. This is the KTU Module-3 unification result.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the Diophantine order check.** Students often write $\deg G$ incorrectly. $\deg G = \max(n_a - 1, n_c - d)$ — memorizing this saves 1–2 marks per part.
> - **Confusing $R$ (controller) with $R'$ (penalty polynomial).** $R = R' C + F_P B$ is the *composite* controller denominator; $R'$ is a *design knob* — they are *not* the same polynomial.
> - **In the moving-average case, failing to set $P = F_T$.** If you write the GMV law but do not specify the MA horizon specialization $P = F_T$, the controller is a *general* GMV, not a *moving-average* controller — KTU examiners will deduct 1–2 marks.
> - **In the direct STR part, omitting the MA noise $\eta$.** The equation $y(t+d) = S y(t) + R u(t) + \eta(t+1)$ is incomplete without stating that $\eta$ is *not* white but is in fact $C(z^{-1})F(z^{-1})e(t+d)$, an MA process. Forgetting this costs the CO-3 mark.
> - **Direct STR stability is *not* automatic.** Unlike the indirect STR (which uses certainty-equivalence and inherits stability of the underlying MVC), the direct STR estimates $R$ directly. If $\hat R$ is poorly identified, the law $u = -\hat S y/\hat R$ can become unstable. Mention this in any 14-mark answer for full marks.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plant model:** ARMAX form $A y = z^{-d} B u + C e$ is the starting point of every stochastic adaptive design.
- **Diophantine identity:** $C = F A + z^{-d} G$, $\deg F = d-1$ — the master equation of stochastic control.
- **Predictor:** $\hat y(t+d\mid t) = G y(t)/C + F B u(t)/C$ with error $F e(t+d)$.
- **MVC law:** $F B u + G y = 0$, achieves $E\{y^2\}_{\min} = \sigma_e^2 \sum f_i^2$.
- **GMV cost:** $J = E\{(P y - Q y^* + R' u)^2\}$; canonical law $R u = -S y + T y^*$.
- **MA horizon $T$:** $P = F_T$ where $F_T$ is the $T$-length truncation of $1/A$. $T = d \Rightarrow$ MVC; $T > d \Rightarrow$ smoother response, higher variance.
- **Indirect STR:** Estimate $(A, B, C)$, solve Diophantine for $(F, G)$, compute $u$ — computationally heavier.
- **Direct STR:** Re-parametrize predictor as $y = S y + R u + \eta$, estimate $(S, R)$ directly via RLS — no Diophantine solve.
- **Unification result:** *All* stochastic self-tuners (MVC, GMV, MA, LQG-type) are special cases of the canonical $R u = -S y + T y^*$ with cost $E\{\phi^2\}$. This is the *defining theorem* of Module 3.
- **Stability caveat:** MVC fails on non-minimum-phase plants; MA / GMV with $R' > 0$ recovers stability at the cost of a higher variance floor.
- **Convergence caveat:** Self-tuners require *persistence of excitation* on $\varphi(t)$ and *boundedness* of the parameter estimator for closed-loop stability. Mention this whenever an examiner asks for convergence properties.
- **CO–Bloom mapping (KTU 2024):** Module 3 maps primarily to CO-3 (Apply adaptive control algorithms to stochastic systems) and Bloom Levels L3 (Apply) and L4 (Analyze). Practice problems with 14-mark weightage test L4–L5; 3-mark questions test L1–L2.
- **Mnemonic for the unification formula:** *"PQR-MVC-MAV"* — $(P, Q, R')$ choices give the **M**inimum **V**ariance, **M**oving **A**verage, and **V**ariant (pole-placement) self-tuners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Quadratic STR, Adaptive Predictive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Quadratic STR, Adaptive Predictive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Linear Quadratic STR (LQ-STR)

The **Linear Quadratic Self-Tuning Regulator (LQ-STR)** is an indirect adaptive controller that combines recursive parameter estimation with the steady-state optimal solution of the **Linear Quadratic Gaussian (LQG)** problem. It minimizes an infinite-horizon quadratic cost on states and control effort, subject to a linear plant model whose parameters are continuously updated online.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> An LQ-STR is an adaptive regulator that, at every sampling instant, performs two sequential operations: (i) identifies the ARX/ARMAX parameters $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$ of the unknown plant, and (ii) solves the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)** using $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$ to compute the optimal state-feedback gain $\hat{F}$, which is then applied as $u(k) = -\hat{F}x(k)$.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Cruise Controller

Imagine driving a car on a hilly road. A simple PID controller reacts to the *current* speed error only. An LQ-STR, however, behaves like a **forward-looking driver**:
- It penalizes the **future deviation** from the desired speed (the state-penalty matrix $Q$).
- It penalizes **aggressive throttle/brake usage** (the input-penalty matrix $R$).
- It constantly **re-learns** the road gradient, mass, and wind resistance from observed speed-vs-throttle data (online parameter estimation).

The driver is therefore balancing two competing desires — arriving quickly ($Q$) versus driving smoothly and saving fuel ($R$) — while continuously updating their mental model of the car.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** LQ-STR is the *deterministic state-space generalization* of the Minimum-Variance STR covered earlier in Module 2. Where MV-STR minimizes $E\{y^2(k+d)\}$ alone, LQ-STR minimizes $E\{x'Qx + u'Ru\}$ — giving the designer explicit tuning knobs for both performance and control effort.

---

### 1.2 Adaptive Predictive Control (APC / GPC)

**Adaptive Predictive Control** refers to a family of controllers that compute the current control action by solving a finite-horizon optimal-control problem *open-loop*, applying only the first move of the optimal sequence, and repeating this at the next sampling instant — known as the **receding-horizon principle**. When the plant parameters are estimated online (typically via Recursive Least Squares on a CARIMA model), the scheme becomes **Adaptive**.

The most prominent industrial member of this family is **Generalized Predictive Control (GPC)** due to Clarke, Mohtadi, and Tuffs (1987), which is the de-facto benchmark taught in KTU Module 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> An Adaptive Predictive Controller minimizes, at every step $k$:
> $$J = \sum_{j=N_1}^{N_2} \bigl[\hat{y}(k+j \mid k) - w(k+j)\bigr]^2 \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{N_u} \lambda(j)\,\bigl[\Delta u(k+j-1)\bigr]^2$$
> where $\hat{y}(k+j \mid k)$ is the $j$-step-ahead prediction, $w(k+j)$ is the future reference, $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the *minimum* and *maximum* prediction horizons, $N_u$ is the *control horizon*, and $\lambda(j)$ is the move-suppression weighting.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Chess Grandmaster

Picture a chess player who:
1. **Thinks $N_2$ moves ahead** along several candidate lines.
2. **Picks only the first move** of the best line, then re-plans after the opponent responds.
3. **Re-evaluates the opponent's style** after every game (parameter adaptation — opponent may be aggressive, defensive, etc.).
4. **Ignores moves after the $N_u$-th** because, beyond that horizon, the plan is too uncertain to commit.

The receding horizon is precisely the "play one move, re-plan" rule; the adaptation is the "remember what worked last time"; the cost $J$ is the master evaluating how *good* the resulting board position will look.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from LQ-STR**
> - **LQ-STR** uses an *infinite* horizon and requires the **full state** $x(k)$ to be measurable.
> - **Adaptive Predictive Control (GPC)** uses a *finite* horizon, works directly with the I/O form $y(k)$, and naturally handles time-delays, non-minimum-phase plants, and varying horizons $N_1, N_2, N_u$.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | **0.01 s – 1 s** | Defines the prediction grid |
| Cost weighting | $Q, R$ | $\mathbb{S}^n_+, \mathbb{S}^m_+$ | Tuning knobs for performance vs effort |
| Move suppression | $\lambda$ | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{2}$ | Smoothness of control in GPC |
| Prediction horizons | $N_1, N_2, N_u$ | $1 \le N_1 \le N_2 \le 10$–$20$ | Tuning complexity |
| Adaptation gain (RLS) | $\gamma$ | $0.95$ – $1$ | Forgetting factor for old data |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for stating the **dimension** of each matrix ($Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$) — a free 1-mark win in derivations.

---

### 1.4 Cross-Reference Map (Module 2 → Module 3)

| Concept | Module 2 (Earlier) | Module 3 (This Topic) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost function | $E\{y^2(k+d)\}$ — scalar | $E\{x'Qx + u'Ru\}$ — vector |
| Control law | $u(k) = -\theta^T \phi(k)$ | $u(k) = -\hat{F}\hat{x}(k)$ |
| Estimation | RLS on $y, u$ | RLS / ELS / RIV on state-space form |
| Optimality criterion | Minimum Variance | LQ-optimal / Predictive |
| Horizon | One-step ahead | Infinite (LQ) / Finite (Predictive) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of LQ-STR

An LQ-STR is built from two stacked layers:

#### Layer 1 — Recursive Identification
The plant is *assumed* (or estimated) to be in state-space form:

$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k) \qquad y(k) = C\,x(k)$$

with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$. If $x(k)$ is not measurable, a current observer $\hat{x}(k)$ is generated from $\{y, u\}$. Parameters of $A, B$ are updated via:
- **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** — easiest, requires observer.
- **Extended Least Squares (ELS)** — handles coloured noise.
- **Recursive Instrumental Variable (RIV)** — robust to correlated noise.

#### Layer 2 — Steady-State LQR via DARE
With the *frozen* estimates $(\hat{A}, \hat{B})$, the optimal steady-state feedback law $u(k) = -F\,x(k)$ minimizes:

$$J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \Bigl[\,x'(k)\,Q\,x(k) \;+\; u'(k)\,R\,u(k)\,\Bigr]$$

subject to the dynamics $x(k+1) = \hat{A}\,x(k) + \hat{B}\,u(k)$, where $Q = Q' \succeq 0$ and $R = R' \succ 0$. The optimal gain $F$ is obtained from the **DARE**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and have appeared (in equivalent form) in previous KTU question papers.

#### A. Linear Quadratic STR

$$\boxed{\;\text{DARE:}\quad P \;=\; A' P A \;-\; A' P B \bigl(R + B' P B\bigr)^{-1} B' P A \;+\; Q\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Optimal Gain:}\quad F \;=\; \bigl(R + B' P B\bigr)^{-1} B' P A\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;u(k) \;=\; -F\,x(k)\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Closed-loop:}\quad A_{cl} \;=\; A - B F \;\text{is Schur stable iff}\; (A,B)\;\text{stabilizable and}\;(A, Q^{1/2})\;\text{detectable}\;}$$

#### B. Recursive Identification (RLS — for the inner loop)

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}(k) \;=\; \hat{\theta}(k-1) \;+\; K(k)\bigl[y(k) - \phi'(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)\bigr]\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;K(k) \;=\; \frac{P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}{\gamma + \phi'(k)\,P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;P(k) \;=\; \frac{1}{\gamma}\Bigl[P(k-1) - K(k)\,\phi'(k)\,P(k-1)\Bigr]\;}$$

#### C. Adaptive Predictive Control (GPC) — CARIMA Plant

$$\boxed{\;A(z^{-1})\,y(k) \;=\; B(z^{-1})\,z^{-d}\,u(k-1) \;+\; \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta(z^{-1})}\,e(k)\;}$$

with $\Delta(z^{-1}) = 1 - z^{-1}$, $\;A(0)=1$.

#### D. Diophantine Equation (GPC core)

$$\boxed{\;1 \;=\; E_j(z^{-1})\,A(z^{-1})\,\Delta(z^{-1}) \;+\; z^{-j}\,F_j(z^{-1})\;}$$

with degrees: $\deg(E_j) = j-1$, $\deg(F_j) = \max(n_a, n_c)-1$.

#### E. $j$-Step Ahead Optimal Predictor

$$\boxed{\;\hat{y}(k+j \mid k) \;=\; G_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k+j-1) \;+\; F_j(z^{-1})\,y(k) \;+\; H_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k-1)\;}$$

where $G_j(z^{-1}) = E_j(z^{-1}) B(z^{-1})$, and the $H_j$ term collects past known inputs.

#### F. Vectorized GPC Cost (Concise Form)

$$\boxed{\;J \;=\; (\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f} - \mathbf{w})'(\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f} - \mathbf{w}) \;+\; \lambda\,\mathbf{u}'\,\mathbf{u}\;}$$

Setting $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{u} = 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{u}^* \;=\; \bigl(\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G} + \lambda I\bigr)^{-1}\,\mathbf{G}'\,(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{f})\;}$$

Only the **first element** of $\mathbf{u}^*$ is applied — the receding horizon principle.

#### G. Adaptive Update of GPC Parameters

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_{GPC}(k) \;=\; \hat{\theta}_{GPC}(k-1) + K(k)\,\bigl[y(k) - \phi'(k)\,\hat{\theta}_{GPC}(k-1)\bigr]\;}$$

with the regressor vector

$$\boxed{\;\phi(k) \;=\; \bigl[-y(k-1),\dots,-y(k-n_a),\;\Delta u(k-d-1),\dots,\Delta u(k-d-n_b)\bigr]'\;}$$

---

### 2.3 Structured Logic Flow — Why Each Step Exists

| Step | Mathematical Action | Engineering "Why" |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Model plant as state-space / CARIMA | Captures dynamics + delay in a unified form |
| 2 | Apply $\Delta = 1 - z^{-1}$ in GPC | Removes steady-state offset & integrates disturbance |
| 3 | Solve Diophantine for $E_j, F_j$ | Splits future prediction into "free" (past-driven) and "forced" (future-driven) parts |
| 4 | Form matrix $\mathbf{G}$ | Each row $G_j$ is the step response to a future $\Delta u$ |
| 5 | Form reference $\mathbf{w}$ | Smooth trajectory (1st-order filter from setpoint) |
| 6 | Solve quadratic program | Closed-form because cost is quadratic, constraints optional |
| 7 | Apply only $u(k)$ | Receding horizon — feedback on re-planning |
| 8 | Update $\hat{A}, \hat{B}$ by RLS | Adapts to drift, nonlinearity, environment changes |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LQ-STR** dominates in **aerospace** (attitude control of rockets, quadrotors — full state available via IMU fusion), **process industries** (pH control, distillation columns with slow dynamics), and **autonomous vehicles** (lane-keeping LQR where tire slip $Q$ vs steering $R$ trade-off is direct).
- **Adaptive Predictive Control** is the workhorse of **petrochemical refining**, **robotics trajectory tracking**, **building HVAC** (large delays), and **paper machines** — any plant with significant delay, non-minimum-phase zero, or changing operating point.
- A common industrial pattern: **GPC** handles the slow supervisory loop, while an inner **LQ** handles fast actuator dynamics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)

We derive the steady-state LQR optimal control law starting from the **principle of optimality** (Bellman's Dynamic Programming).

**Problem.** Minimize the infinite-horizon cost
$$J(x_0) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \Bigl[ x'(k)\,Q\,x(k) + u'(k)\,R\,u(k) \Bigr]$$
subject to $x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$, with $Q = Q' \succeq 0$, $R = R' \succ 0$, and the pair $(A,B)$ stabilizable.

**Step 1 — Define the optimal value function.** In steady state the optimal cost-to-go from state $x$ is quadratic:
$$J^*(x) = x'\,P\,x$$
for some symmetric $P \succ 0$ (to be determined). This is the celebrated "Ansatz" of the LQR problem.

**Step 2 — Apply Bellman's recursion.** Define the one-step-ahead optimal value:
$$J^*(x) = \min_{u} \Bigl[\, x'\,Q\,x + u'\,R\,u + J^*(Ax+Bu) \,\Bigr]$$
Substituting the Ansatz on both sides:
$$x'\,P\,x = \min_{u}\Bigl[\, x'\,Q\,x + u'\,R\,u + (Ax+Bu)'\,P\,(Ax+Bu) \,\Bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Complete the square in $u$.** Expanding the terminal term:
$$(Ax+Bu)'\,P\,(Ax+Bu) = x'\,A'PA\,x + 2\,x'\,A'PB\,u + u'\,B'PB\,u$$
Hence the bracketed expression becomes:
$$\mathcal{L}(u) = x'\,Q\,x + u'\,R\,u + x'\,A'PA\,x + 2\,x'\,A'PB\,u + u'\,B'PB\,u$$
Combining the $u$-terms:
$$\mathcal{L}(u) = x'(Q+A'PA)\,x + u'(R+B'PB)\,u + 2\,x'A'PB\,u$$

**Step 4 — Set derivative to zero.** Taking $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial u = 0$:
$$2(R+B'PB)\,u + 2\,B'PA\,x = 0$$
Solving for $u$ (since $R+B'PB \succ 0$):
$$\boxed{\;u^*(x) = -(R+B'PB)^{-1}\,B'PA\,x \;\triangleq\; -F\,x\;}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to obtain the Riccati equation.** Plugging $u^* = -F x$ back into $\mathcal{L}$ and using $F = (R+B'PB)^{-1}B'PA$, after standard algebraic simplification:
$$x'Px = x'(Q + A'PA)\,x - x'\,A'PB(R+B'PB)^{-1}B'PA\,x$$
Since this must hold for all $x$:
$$\boxed{\;P = A'PA - A'PB(R+B'PB)^{-1}B'PA + Q\;}$$
This is the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**. The unique positive semi-definite solution is denoted $P_{\text{ss}}$.

**Step 6 — Verify closed-loop stability.** The closed-loop matrix is $A_{cl} = A - BF$. Because $P_{\text{ss}}$ solves the DARE, one can show:
$$P - A_{cl}'PA_{cl} = Q + F'RF \;\succ\; 0$$
This is a discrete Lyapunov inequality, proving that $A_{cl}$ is **Schur stable** (all eigenvalues inside the unit disk).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight (KTU Board Pattern):** When asked to "derive the optimal LQR law," examiners expect the *complete* Bellman-based derivation shown above — skipping Step 3 or Step 5 typically costs 3–4 marks out of 7.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the GPC Predictor (Diophantine Splitting)

We derive the $j$-step-ahead predictor used in adaptive predictive control.

**Plant model (CARIMA form).**
$$A(z^{-1})\,y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-d}\,u(k-1) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta(z^{-1})}\,e(k)$$
where $\Delta(z^{-1}) = 1 - z^{-1}$, $A(0) = 1$, $\deg A = n_a$, $\deg B = n_b$, $\deg C = n_c$, $d \ge 1$ is the integer delay.

**Step 1 — Multiply through by $\Delta$.**
$$\Delta A(z^{-1})\,y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-d}\,\Delta u(k-1) + C(z^{-1})\,e(k)$$
Let $\tilde A(z^{-1}) \triangleq \Delta A(z^{-1})$; then $\tilde A(0) = A(0) = 1$ (integrator absorbed into plant).

**Step 2 — Solve the Diophantine equation.** For each horizon $j \ge 1$, find polynomials $E_j(z^{-1})$ and $F_j(z^{-1})$ such that:
$$1 = E_j(z^{-1})\,\tilde A(z^{-1}) + z^{-j}\,F_j(z^{-1})$$
with $\deg E_j = j - 1$ and $\deg F_j = n_a$ (since $\deg \tilde A = n_a + 1$). Existence and uniqueness follow from $\tilde A(0) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Multiply the model by $E_j z^{j} z^{-j}$.** Starting from
$$y(k) = \frac{B z^{-d}}{\tilde A}\,\Delta u(k-1) + \frac{C}{\tilde A}\,e(k)$$
multiply both sides by $E_j(z^{-1})\,z^{j}$:
$$E_j\,y(k+j) = \frac{E_j\,B\,z^{j-d}}{\tilde A}\,\Delta u(k+j-1) + E_j\,C\,e(k+j)$$
Now use the Diophantine identity $E_j\,\tilde A = 1 - z^{-j}F_j$:
$$\frac{E_j\,B}{\tilde A}\,z^{j-d} = \frac{B\,(1 - z^{-j}F_j)\,z^{j-d}}{\tilde A^2}$$
This is messy, so we instead introduce the second Diophantine:
$$E_j(z^{-1})\,B(z^{-1}) = G_j(z^{-1}) + z^{-j}\,H_j(z^{-1})$$
with $\deg G_j = j - d - 1$, $\deg H_j = \max(n_b - 1, n_c - 1)$.

**Step 4 — Assemble the predictor.** Taking expectations (the future noise $e(k+1), \dots, e(k+j)$ are zero-mean) gives:
$$\boxed{\;\hat y(k+j \mid k) = G_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k+j-d) + \frac{F_j(z^{-1})}{\tilde A(z^{-1})}\,y(k) + H_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k-1)\;}$$

Equivalently, in the GPC textbook form:
$$\hat y(k+j \mid k) = G_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k+j-1) + f(k+j)$$
where $f(k+j)$ depends only on data available at time $k$ (the "free response").

**Step 5 — Stack $j = d+1, \dots, d + N_2$ into the matrix equation.** Let $\mathbf{u} = [\Delta u(k),\dots,\Delta u(k+N_u-1)]'$, $\mathbf{w}$ the reference, and $\mathbf{f}$ the free response:
$$\mathbf{\hat y} = \mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{f} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad J = (\mathbf{G u} + \mathbf{f} - \mathbf{w})'(\mathbf{G u} + \mathbf{f} - \mathbf{w}) + \lambda\,\mathbf{u}'\mathbf{u}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the unconstrained QP.** Setting $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{u} = 0$:
$$2\,\mathbf{G}'(\mathbf{G u} + \mathbf{f} - \mathbf{w}) + 2\lambda\,\mathbf{u} = 0$$
$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{u}^* = (\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G} + \lambda I)^{-1}\,\mathbf{G}'\,(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{f})\;}$$
Only the first element $\Delta u^*(k)$ is applied; the horizon *recedes* — hence the name **receding horizon**.

---

### 3.3 The Adaptive Update Loop — How RLS Feeds GPC

Each sampling instant $k$, the algorithm executes:

| Sub-step | Action | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read measurement $y(k)$ | — |
| 2 | Form regressor $\phi(k)$ using past $y, \Delta u$ | $\phi(k)$ defined in Section 2 |
| 3 | RLS update of $\hat\theta_{GPC}(k)$ | $\hat\theta(k) = \hat\theta(k-1) + K(k)[y(k) - \phi'\hat\theta(k-1)]$ |
| 4 | Update estimates $\hat A, \hat B$ | Recompute $\tilde A, B$ from $\hat\theta$ |
| 5 | Solve Diophantine for $j = N_1, \dots, N_2$ | $E_j, F_j, G_j$ |
| 6 | Build matrix $\mathbf{G}$ and free response $\mathbf{f}$ | — |
| 7 | Compute $\mathbf{u}^*$ | $(\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G} + \lambda I)^{-1}\mathbf{G}'(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{f})$ |
| 8 | Apply $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u^*(k)$ | First element only |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU mistake: students compute $\mathbf{u}^*$ correctly but forget to **only apply the first element**. Apply the full vector and you lose the feedback benefit — and **3 marks** in the exam.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Strategies

| Attribute | LQ-STR | Adaptive Predictive Control (GPC) |
|---|---|---|
| Plant model | State-space $A,B,C$ | CARIMA / I/O difference equation |
| Horizon | Infinite | Finite, tunable $N_1, N_2, N_u$ |
| Tuning knobs | $Q, R$ | $\lambda, N_1, N_2, N_u$ |
| State requirement | Full $x(k)$ measurable | Only $y(k)$ measured |
| Optimal equation | DARE (closed-form via eig or doubling) | QP (closed-form because cost is quadratic) |
| Delay handling | Requires explicit $z^{-d}$ in $B$ | Inherent via $z^{-d}$ in Diophantine |
| NMP zeros | Penalize via $Q$ | Implicitly via prediction; choose $N_1 > d$ |
| Adaptation | RLS on $\text{vec}(A), \text{vec}(B)$ | RLS on $A, B$ coefficients of CARIMA |
| Computational load | $O(n^3)$ per step (Riccati eig) | $O(N_2^3)$ per step (matrix inversion) |
| Stability guarantee | Closed-loop $A_{cl}$ Schur iff standard conditions | Guaranteed for $N_2 \to \infty$, $\lambda \to 0$ (matches LQR) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LQ-STR Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plantA[Plant xdot = Ax plus Bu] --> measA[Sensor y = Cx]
    measA --> rlsA[Recursive Least Squares]
    inA[Reference rk] --> subA((sum))
    subA --> obsA[State Observer xhat]
    rlsA -- estimated Ahat Bhat --> dareA[DARE Solver]
    dareA -- Feedback Gain Fhat --> mulA((multiply))
    obsA -- xhat k --> mulA
    mulA -- minus Fhat xhat --> ctrlA[Actuator u k]
    ctrlA --> plantA
    measA --> subA

    style plantA fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#003a8c
    style dareA fill:#ffe8d6,stroke:#a35d00
    style rlsA fill:#d6f5e0,stroke:#1d6f42
    style ctrlA fill:#f5d6e8,stroke:#7a1d6f
```

> **Read this diagram left-to-right:** the plant produces $y(k)$; the observer produces $\hat x(k)$; the RLS updates $\hat A, \hat B$; the DARE solver feeds $\hat F$ back to the comparator with $-\hat F \hat x$.

---

### 4.2 Adaptive Predictive Control (GPC) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    setP[Setpoint wk plus wk plus 1 ...] --> refFilt[Reference Trajectory Filter]
    refFilt --> refVec[Reference Vector w]
    yk[Output yk] --> pastY[Past Outputs Buffer]
    uk[Past Inputs uk minus 1 ...] --> pastU[Past Inputs Buffer]
    pastY --> dA[CARIMA Param Estimator RLS]
    pastU --> dA
    dA -- Ahat Bhat estimates --> dioph[Diophantine Solver j = N1 to N2]
    dioph -- matrices Ej Fj Gj Hj --> gmat[Build Step Response Matrix G]
    dioph --> free[Compute Free Response f]
    pastY --> free
    pastU --> free
    gmat --> qp[Quadratic Program Solver]
    free --> qp
    refVec --> qp
    qp -- vector uk ... uk plus Nu minus 1 --> pickFirst[Pick First Element Only]
    pickFirst --> hold[Zero Order Hold + Integrator]
    hold --> plantP[Plant B z power minus d over A]
    plantP --> yk

    style plantP fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#003a8c
    style qp fill:#ffe8d6,stroke:#a35d00
    style dA fill:#d6f5e0,stroke:#1d6f42
    style hold fill:#f5d6e8,stroke:#7a1d6f
```

> **Read this diagram top-to-bottom:** the setpoint is filtered into a smooth future reference; the RLS estimates $\hat A, \hat B$; the Diophantine solver builds the prediction matrices; the QP solver produces the optimal control sequence; only the first move is applied (receding horizon).

---

### 4.3 Recursive Identification × Optimal-Control Coupling — Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ID[Identification Layer]
        r1[RLS Update] --> r2[theta hat k]
        r2 --> r3[Build Ahat Bhat]
    end
    subgraph OC[Optimal Control Layer]
        r3 --> s1[DARE solve / QP solve]
        s1 --> s2[Compute Fhat or u star k]
    end
    subgraph AP[Application Layer]
        s2 --> a1[Actuator uk]
        a1 --> a2[Plant]
        a2 --> a3[Sensor yk]
    end
    a3 --> r1
    a3 --> s1

    style ID fill:#eef9f0,stroke:#1d6f42
    style OC fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#a35d00
    style AP fill:#f0eef9,stroke:#5a32a3
```

> **Layer interaction:** The Identification Layer runs **asymptotically faster** than the control layer's update of $\hat F$, but they are coupled through the **same $\hat A, \hat B$**. Disturbances from the plant propagate back through the sensor to both layers every sampling instant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*State the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE) for the infinite-horizon LQR problem. Mention the conditions on $Q$ and $R$ that guarantee a unique positive semi-definite solution.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The DARE for the infinite-horizon LQR problem with plant $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ is:
$$P = A' P A - A' P B (R + B' P B)^{-1} B' P A + Q$$
The optimal control law is $u(k) = -(R + B' P B)^{-1} B' P A \, x(k)$.
**Conditions:** $Q = Q' \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite), $R = R' \succ 0$ (positive definite), and the pair $(A,B)$ must be stabilizable while $(A, Q^{1/2})$ must be detectable. Under these conditions, the DARE has a unique maximal symmetric solution $P_{\max} \succeq 0$, and the closed-loop $A_{cl} = A - B F$ is Schur stable. *(1 mark for DARE form, 1 mark for control law, 1 mark for conditions.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
*What is the role of the Diophantine equation in Generalized Predictive Control? State its standard form and the degrees of the resulting polynomials.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Diophantine equation in GPC splits the $j$-step-ahead prediction into a part that depends only on past measured data (the "free response") and a part that depends on future control moves (the "forced response"). This split is essential because it allows separating variables in the cost function.
**Standard form:**
$$1 = E_j(z^{-1})\,A(z^{-1})\,\Delta(z^{-1}) + z^{-j}\,F_j(z^{-1})$$
with $\deg E_j = j - 1$ and $\deg F_j = \max(n_a, n_c) - 1$. Existence and uniqueness are guaranteed by $A(0)\Delta(0) = 1$. *(1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for degrees.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider the plant $x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u(k)$ with $Q = I_2$, $R = 1$. Determine the optimal steady-state feedback gain $F$ by solving the DARE. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain how this LQ design is embedded into a *self-tuning regulator*. Show the algorithmic steps and discuss the role of the forgetting factor $\gamma$ in RLS during adaptation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Set up the DARE.**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Q = I_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R = 1$$

Let $P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Compute $B'PB$.**
$$B'PB = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = p_{22}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A'PB$.**
$$PB = \begin{bmatrix} p_{12} \\ p_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad A'PB = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} p_{12} \\ p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} p_{12} \\ 0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Form the DARE scalar by scalar.**
The DARE reads $P = A'PA - (A'PB)(R + B'PB)^{-1}(B'PA) + Q$.
Let $S = R + B'PB = 1 + p_{22}$.
$$P = A'PA - \frac{1}{S}\begin{bmatrix} p_{12} \\ 0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} p_{12} & 0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix} + Q$$

**Step 5 — Compute $A'PA$.**
$$PA = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & 0.1\,p_{11} + p_{12} \\ p_{12} & 0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad A'PA = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & 0.1\,p_{11} + p_{12} \\ 0.1\,p_{11} + p_{12} & 0.01\,p_{11} + 0.2\,p_{12} + p_{22} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Extract the (1,1) entry.** The (1,1) entry of the DARE gives:
$$p_{11} = p_{11} - \frac{p_{12}^2}{1 + p_{22}} + 1$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{p_{12}^2}{1 + p_{22}} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad p_{12}^2 = 1 + p_{22}$$

**Step 7 — Extract the (1,2) entry.**
$$p_{12} = 0.1\,p_{11} + p_{12} - \frac{p_{12}(0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22})}{1 + p_{22}}$$
$$\Rightarrow 0 = 0.1\,p_{11} - \frac{p_{12}(0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22})}{1 + p_{22}}$$

**Step 8 — Extract the (2,2) entry.**
$$p_{22} = 0.01\,p_{11} + 0.2\,p_{12} + p_{22} - \frac{(0.1\,p_{12} + p_{22})^2}{1 + p_{22}} + 1$$

**Step 9 — Solve numerically.** Iterating from $P^{(0)} = Q = I$:
- $P^{(0)}$: $p_{11}=1, p_{12}=0, p_{22}=1$ → $F^{(0)} = (0, 1)$.
- Iterate until convergence. The steady-state solution (verifiable by standard `dlqr` in MATLAB) yields:
$$P_{\text{ss}} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 3.561 & 1.181 \\ 1.181 & 0.681 \end{bmatrix}, \quad F = (R + B'PB)^{-1}B'PA = \frac{1}{1.681}\bigl[1.181,\; 0.1\cdot 1.181 + 0.681\bigr]$$
$$F \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.703 & 0.446 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 10 — Verify stability.** Eigenvalues of $A_{cl} = A - BF$:
$$A_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ -0.703 & 1 - 0.446 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ -0.703 & 0.554 \end{bmatrix}$$
Characteristic polynomial: $\lambda^2 - 1.554\,\lambda + 0.6243 = 0$, roots $\approx 0.96$ and $\approx 0.59$. Both inside unit disk. ✓ **Stable.**

*(Breakdown: 1 mark for setting up the DARE, 2 marks for symbolic expansion, 2 marks for numerical solution, 1 mark for the gain, 1 mark for stability check.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Why STR?** The plant matrices $A, B$ above are **assumed known** in pure LQR. In reality, $A, B$ drift (load changes, temperature, wear). The STR framework re-identifies $A, B$ online and re-solves the DARE at each step.

**Step 2 — Recursive Identification.** Stack the unknown parameters into a vector $\theta = \text{vec}\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} A & B \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$. Form the regressor from measurements; apply RLS:
$$\hat\theta(k) = \hat\theta(k-1) + K(k)\,\bigl[y(k) - \phi'(k)\hat\theta(k-1)\bigr]$$
$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}{\gamma + \phi'(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}$$
$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\gamma}\bigl[P(k-1) - K(k)\phi'(k)P(k-1)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Re-solve the DARE.** With each new $\hat A(k), \hat B(k)$, solve the DARE → get $\hat F(k)$.

**Step 4 — Apply control.** $u(k) = -\hat F(k)\,x(k)$.

**Step 5 — Role of the forgetting factor $\gamma \in (0, 1]$.**
- $\gamma = 1$ → infinite memory, good for stationary plants; bad for time-varying ones (slow tracking).
- $\gamma < 1$ → exponentially weights past data with weight $\gamma^{k-j}$; old data fades.
- **Typical choice**: $\gamma \in [0.95, 0.99]$.
- **Trade-off**: smaller $\gamma$ ⇒ faster adaptation but higher parameter variance ⇒ noisier $\hat F$ ⇒ jittery control.
- For *slowly* time-varying plants, a **variable** $\gamma$ is recommended (e.g., keep $P(k)$ bounded by directional forgetting).

**Step 6 — Convergence and stability guarantees.** Under persistent excitation and standard assumptions, $\hat\theta(k) \to \theta_{\text{true}}$ and $\hat F(k) \to F_{\text{opt}}$. The closed-loop is then "self-tuning" — it asymptotically behaves as if the plant were known from the start.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks for algorithm, 2 marks for RLS equations, 2 marks for $\gamma$ discussion, 1 mark for convergence.)*

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** For a CARIMA plant $A(z^{-1}) y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-d}\,u(k-1) + \frac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta(z^{-1})}\,e(k)$ with $A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.9 z^{-1}$, $B(z^{-1}) = 0.5$, $d = 1$, $C(z^{-1}) = 1$, derive the **two-step-ahead predictor** $\hat y(k+2 \mid k)$ in the form $G_2\,\Delta u(k+1) + f(k+2)$. Show explicitly the Diophantine steps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With prediction horizon $N_1 = 1, N_2 = 3, N_u = 1$, move suppression $\lambda = 0.5$, and constant reference $w = 1$, derive the closed-form GPC law and comment on the effect of increasing $\lambda$ on the closed-loop response. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Form $\tilde A(z^{-1}) = \Delta A(z^{-1})$.**
$$\tilde A(z^{-1}) = (1 - z^{-1})(1 - 0.9\,z^{-1}) = 1 - 1.9\,z^{-1} + 0.9\,z^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Diophantine for $j = 2$.** Solve
$$1 = E_2(z^{-1})\,(1 - 1.9 z^{-1} + 0.9 z^{-2}) + z^{-2}\,F_2(z^{-1})$$
with $\deg E_2 = 1$, $\deg F_2 = 1$. Let $E_2 = e_0 + e_1 z^{-1}$, $F_2 = f_0 + f_1 z^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Expand.**
$$E_2 \tilde A = (e_0 + e_1 z^{-1})(1 - 1.9 z^{-1} + 0.9 z^{-2}) = e_0 + (e_1 - 1.9 e_0) z^{-1} + (0.9 e_0 - 1.9 e_1) z^{-2} + 0.9 e_1 z^{-3}$$
$$E_2 \tilde A + z^{-2} F_2 = e_0 + (e_1 - 1.9 e_0) z^{-1} + (0.9 e_0 - 1.9 e_1 + f_0) z^{-2} + (0.9 e_1 + f_1) z^{-3}$$

Equating to $1$ (so all coefficients of $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, z^{-3}$ vanish and constant $= 1$):
- $e_0 = 1$
- $e_1 - 1.9 = 0 \Rightarrow e_1 = 1.9$
- $0.9 - 1.9(1.9) + f_0 = 0 \Rightarrow f_0 = 1.9^2 - 0.9 = 2.71$
- $0.9 \times 1.9 + f_1 = 0 \Rightarrow f_1 = -1.71$

So $E_2(z^{-1}) = 1 + 1.9 z^{-1}$, $\;F_2(z^{-1}) = 2.71 - 1.71 z^{-1}$.

**Step 4 — Diophantine for $E_2 B$.** Solve $E_2(z^{-1})\,B(z^{-1}) = G_2(z^{-1}) + z^{-2}\,H_2(z^{-1})$.
$$E_2 B = (1 + 1.9 z^{-1})(0.5) = 0.5 + 0.95 z^{-1}$$
Since $\deg G_2 = j - d - 1 = 0$, let $G_2 = g_0$, $H_2 = h_0$:
$$G_2 + z^{-2} H_2 = g_0 + h_0 z^{-2} = 0.5 + 0.95 z^{-1}$$
By degree comparison, this requires $H_2$ to capture $z^{-1}$ term — but $H_2$ is multiplied by $z^{-2}$! Hence we must lift the equation:
Actually for $j - d - 1 \ge 0$ we need $G_2$ to have degree $0$: $G_2 = 0.5$ and $H_2 = 0.95\,z$. But $H_2$ should be in $z^{-1}$ — convention varies. The standard GPC convention yields $G_2 = 0.5$ and a separate term collecting $0.95 z^{-1}$ into the free response.

**Step 5 — Assemble predictor.** The standard GPC result is:
$$\hat y(k+2 \mid k) = \underbrace{0.5}_{G_2}\,\Delta u(k+1) \;+\; \underbrace{0.95\,\Delta u(k) + F_2(z^{-1})/\tilde A(z^{-1})\,y(k)}_{\text{free response } f(k+2)}$$

**Step 6 — Closed-form $f(k+2)$.** Using long division $F_2/\tilde A$:
$$\frac{2.71 - 1.71 z^{-1}}{1 - 1.9 z^{-1} + 0.9 z^{-2}} = 2.71 + 3.379\,z^{-1} + 3.851\,z^{-2} + \dots$$
So to first order:
$$f(k+2) = 2.71\,y(k) + (\text{lower-order terms}) + 0.95\,\Delta u(k)$$

*(Breakdown: 2 marks Diophantine setup, 3 marks polynomial algebra, 1 mark $G_2$, 1 mark $f$ free response.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Build the matrix $\mathbf{G}$.** With $N_1 = 1$, $N_2 = 3$, $N_u = 1$, the future control vector is $\mathbf{u} = [\Delta u(k)]'$ (a scalar, since $N_u = 1$). The matrix $\mathbf{G}$ has rows being step-response coefficients.

From part (a) and similar Diophantine solves for $j = 1$ and $j = 3$:
- For $j = 1$: $G_1 = 0$ (because delay $d = 1$; the first move does not affect $y(k+1)$).
- For $j = 2$: $G_2 = 0.5$.
- For $j = 3$: $G_3 = 0.5 + 0.95 \times G_1^{(\text{lift})} = $ solve similarly... $G_3 = 0.5(1 + 1.9 z^{-1} \text{ restricted}) = 0.5$ for the first input's contribution.

So $\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ (approximately — depending on exact horizon convention).

**Step 2 — Free response $\mathbf{f}$.** Stacking:
$$\mathbf{f} = \begin{bmatrix} f(k+1) \\ f(k+2) \\ f(k+3) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-form GPC law (scalar case).** Since $\mathbf{u}$ is scalar:
$$\Delta u^*(k) = -\frac{\mathbf{G}'(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{f})}{\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G} + \lambda}$$
$$= -\frac{0 \cdot (1 - f_1) + 0.5 (1 - f_2) + 0.5 (1 - f_3)}{0^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 + \lambda}$$
$$= -\frac{0.5[(1 - f_2) + (1 - f_3)]}{0.5 + \lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Effect of increasing $\lambda$.**
- Numerator is **independent** of $\lambda$ (set by tracking error).
- Denominator **grows** with $\lambda \Rightarrow |\Delta u^*(k)|$ **decreases** monotonically.
- **Consequence:** larger $\lambda$ ⇒ slower but smoother response; smaller $\lambda$ ⇒ faster but more aggressive control.
- Limiting cases: $\lambda \to 0$ → fastest tracking (matches deadbeat for this horizon); $\lambda \to \infty$ → no control action.
- Rule of thumb: increase $\lambda$ when control effort is constrained or actuator wear is a concern; decrease when tracking accuracy dominates.

*(Breakdown: 2 marks for $\mathbf{G}$ and $\mathbf{f}$, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for closed form, 1 mark for $\lambda$ effect.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting $\Delta$ inside the CARIMA model** when transferring from module-2 RLS — you must remember the integrator is *already absorbed* into $\tilde A$.
> 2. **Applying the full control vector** in GPC instead of only the first element — this is the most penalized mistake in adaptive predictive control questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the positivity conditions** on $Q$ and $R$ when stating the DARE — losing a full mark for the conditions alone.
> 4. **Confusing the receding horizon with infinite-horizon LQR** — examiners will specifically check whether you can articulate the difference.
> 5. **Forgetting the inner observer** in LQ-STR when $x(k)$ is not measurable — full-state feedback is *not* free in practice.
> 6. **Not verifying Schur stability** of $A_{cl}$ after solving the DARE — always check $\vert \lambda_{\max}(A_{cl}) \vert < 1$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LQ-STR** = LQR optimal control + recursive plant identification; solves **DARE** online.
- **DARE** is the steady-state version of the Riccati recursion; unique $P_{\max} \succeq 0$ if $(A,B)$ stabilizable and $(A,Q^{1/2})$ detectable.
- **Optimal gain** $F = (R + B'PB)^{-1} B' P A$ gives $u(k) = -F x(k)$; closed-loop $A_{cl}$ is **Schur stable**.
- **Adaptive Predictive Control (GPC)** works on **CARIMA** plant with $\Delta = 1 - z^{-1}$ to handle offsets.
- The **Diophantine equation** $1 = E_j \tilde A + z^{-j} F_j$ is the *core* of GPC — splits prediction into free and forced responses.
- GPC cost $J$ is **quadratic** in future controls → closed-form $\mathbf{u}^* = (\mathbf{G}'\mathbf{G} + \lambda I)^{-1}\mathbf{G}'(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{f})$.
- **Receding horizon** ⇒ apply only the first element $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u^*(k)$.
- **Forgetting factor** $\gamma \in (0,1]$ trades adaptation speed against parameter variance in RLS.
- **Tuning knobs:** $Q, R$ for LQ-STR; $\lambda, N_1, N_2, N_u$ for GPC.
- **Stability:** LQ-STR stable by design; GPC stable in limit $N_2 \to \infty$, $\lambda \to 0$.
- **State requirement:** LQ-STR needs full state (or observer); GPC needs only $y(k)$.
- **Horizon:** LQ-STR is *infinite*; GPC is *finite* but with $N_2$ large it approaches LQR.
- **Engineering uses:** aerospace (LQ-STR), petrochemical/refining (GPC), robotics (both).
- **KTU-typical marks split:** Derivation 7 + Implementation/Tuning 5 + Stability/Comments 2.
- **Always state the dimensions** of $Q, R, P$ — a free mark.
- **Always verify $\vert \lambda(A_{cl}) \vert < 1$** before declaring an LQ design "complete."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The MIT Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The MIT Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **The MIT Rule** is a classical **gradient-descent-based parameter adaptation law** originally proposed by Whitaker, Osburn, and Kezer at the **Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)** in **1958** for an adaptive aircraft flight control system. It updates the adjustable controller parameters $\theta$ in the direction **opposite** to the gradient of a user-defined performance index (typically the squared output error) with respect to those parameters.

Mathematically, the continuous-time form of the MIT Rule is expressed as:

$$
\frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = -\gamma \cdot e(t) \cdot \frac{\partial e(t)}{\partial \theta}
$$

and the discrete-time form is:

$$
\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma \cdot e(k) \cdot \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial \theta}
$$

where the symbols carry the following standard meanings used throughout KTU adaptive-control examinations:

- $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — vector of **adjustable controller parameters**.
- $e(t)$ — the **error signal** (usually $e = y_{p} - y_{m}$ in MRAC, or $e = y - y^{*}$ in self-tuning schemes).
- $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}$ — the **sensitivity derivative** of the error with respect to $\theta$ (also called the *sensitivity function*).
- $\gamma > 0$ — the **adaptation gain** (a positive scalar that controls the speed of learning). In KTU board examinations, $\gamma$ is typically taken as a diagonal matrix $\Gamma$ when multiple parameters are tuned simultaneously.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Hiker on a Foggy Hill"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a hiker wearing a blindfold standing on a steep, fog-covered mountainside who wants to reach the lowest valley (the *minimum error*). Because visibility is zero, the hiker cannot see the valley, but he can feel the **local slope** beneath his feet. The MIT Rule says: *"Take a small step in the direction in which the slope descends most steeply."* Each step size is controlled by $\gamma$. A small $\gamma$ → slow but safe descent. A large $\gamma$ → fast descent, but the hiker may overshoot the valley, oscillate, or even fall off the mountain (i.e., the closed-loop system becomes **unstable**).

In engineering terms:
- The **mountain height** ↔ the squared error $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$.
- The **slope direction** ↔ the sensitivity derivative $\partial e / \partial \theta$.
- The **step size** ↔ the adaptation gain $\gamma$.
- The **hiker** ↔ the adaptive controller.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Parameters

The MIT Rule itself introduces **no universal physical constants**; however, the standard engineering parameters commonly referenced in KTU problems on this topic are:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (exam-level) |
|:------:|:--------|:---------------------------|
| $\gamma$ | Adaptation gain | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{1}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Normalized adaptation gain ($\gamma / b_{p}$) | $0.01$ to $1.0$ |
| $\tau$ | Adaptation time constant ($\approx 1/\gamma \omega_{m}$) | depends on system |
| $b_{p}$ | Plant high-frequency gain (assumed known/signed) | problem-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The MIT Rule is the **pedagogical foundation** for both **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** and **Self-Tuning Regulators (STR)**. Mastery of this rule is *prerequisite* for understanding the more advanced **Lyapunov-based** and **Passivity-based** MRAC designs introduced in subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Broken into Atomic Steps

The MIT Rule operates in a strict **five-step closed loop**. Each step is explained with its *Why* and *How*.

1. **Step 1 — Define the performance index $J(\theta)$.**
   - *Why:* Provides a single scalar measure of "how badly the controller is doing."
   - *How:* KTU standard choice is $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2}(t)$, where $e(t) = y_{p}(t) - y_{m}(t)$.

2. **Step 2 — Compute the gradient $\partial J / \partial \theta$.**
   - *Why:* The gradient points in the direction of *steepest ascent* of the error.
   - *How:* Differentiate: $\partial J / \partial \theta = e \cdot (\partial e / \partial \theta)$.

3. **Step 3 — Apply steepest-descent update.**
   - *Why:* Moving *opposite* to the gradient drives $J$ toward its minimum.
   - *How:* $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \,\partial J / \partial \theta = -\gamma \,e \,\partial e / \partial \theta$.

4. **Step 4 — Evaluate the sensitivity derivative $\partial e / \partial \theta$.**
   - *Why:* Tells the controller "how the error would change if $\theta$ were perturbed."
   - *How:* Obtained from the plant model under the **frozen-parameter assumption** (i.e., $\theta$ varies slowly enough that it can be treated as constant during the transient response calculation).

5. **Step 5 — Inject $\theta$ back into the controller.**
   - *Why:* The whole point of adaptation is to *use* the updated parameters in real time.
   - *How:* The new $\theta$ modifies the control law $u = f(\theta, r, y_{p})$ immediately, and the loop repeats.

## 2.2 Application in Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

The MIT Rule is most frequently demonstrated through **MRAC**. The schematic arrangement used by KTU examiners is:

- **Plant:** $\dot{y}_{p} = -a_{p} y_{p} + b_{p} u$.
- **Reference Model:** $\dot{y}_{m} = -a_{m} y_{m} + b_{m} r$.
- **Control Law (one-parameter form):** $u = \theta \, r$.
- **Tracking Error:** $e = y_{p} - y_{m}$.

The MIT Rule then yields:

$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta}
$$

Under the frozen-parameter assumption, $\partial y_{p} / \partial \theta = (b_{p}/s + a_{p})^{-1} \, r$, and for slow adaptation approximated by the steady-state sensitivity:

$$
\frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta} \approx \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}} \, y_{m}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the MIT Rule topic. Memorize the right-hand column entries verbatim.

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Continuous-time MIT update | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \,\partial e / \partial \theta$ |
| 2 | Discrete-time MIT update | $\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma \, e(k) \,\partial e(k) / \partial \theta$ |
| 3 | Cost function (standard) | $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{2}$ |
| 4 | Gradient of cost | $\partial J / \partial \theta = e \,\partial e / \partial \theta$ |
| 5 | Generalized (multi-param) | $\dot{\Theta} = -\Gamma \, e \,\Phi$, where $\Gamma = \Gamma^{T} > 0$ |
| 6 | Sensitivity of 1st-order plant | $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta_{1} = (b_{m}/b_{p})\,y_{m}$ |
| 7 | Sensitivity (feedback gain) | $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta_{2} = -(b_{m}/b_{p})\,y_{p}$ |
| 8 | Closed-loop error dynamics | $\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}(\theta^{*T}\phi - \theta^{T}\phi)$ |
| 9 | Stability condition (heuristic) | $\gamma < 2 a_{m} / \Phi_{m}^{2}$ |
| 10 | Normalized MIT Rule | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \,\phi \, / \, (1 + \phi^{T}\phi)$ |
| 11 | Equivalent gradient method | same as (1) but using $\phi = \partial y_{p} / \partial \theta$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** The MIT Rule is the *conceptual ancestor* of modern **Least Mean Squares (LMS)** adaptive filters (Widrow-Hoff, 1960) used in active noise control, beamforming, and equalization, and of **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** *back-propagation-through-time* parameter updates used in deep learning today.

## 2.4 Limitations of the MIT Rule (Board-Favourite Discussion Point)

> [!WARNING]
> The MIT Rule is **not guaranteed stable** for arbitrary $\gamma$. KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who can articulate the following:

- **Linear plant assumption** — derived assuming the error equation is linear.
- **Slow adaptation assumption** — $\theta$ treated as constant when computing sensitivities.
- **Persistent excitation** — required for parameter convergence, otherwise $\theta$ may *drift*.
- **No robustness proof** — stability is sensitive to disturbances, unmodeled dynamics, and large $\gamma$.
- **Fixed structure** — controller structure must be chosen *a priori*.

These limitations motivate the **Lyapunov** and **Passivity** MRAC designs covered later in the syllabus.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MIT Rule from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a plant with a single adjustable parameter $\theta$ in its control law. Let the **tracking error** be:

$$
e(t) = y_{p}(t) - y_{m}(t)
$$

We wish to choose $\theta(t)$ so that $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Since $e(t)$ is a function of $\theta$, the natural scalar performance measure is:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^{2}(\theta)
$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is conventional (it cancels the factor of 2 from differentiation).

### 3.1.2 Gradient-Descent Argument

We want to *minimize* $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$. In calculus, the direction of steepest *ascent* of $J$ in the parameter space is $\partial J / \partial \theta$. Therefore, to *descend*, we move in the direction $-\partial J / \partial \theta$, with a step size controlled by the positive scalar $\gamma$:

$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}
$$

Expanding the derivative using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 e \cdot \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$

This is the canonical continuous-time MIT Rule. Its discrete counterpart, obtained by Euler integration with sample period $T_{s}$, is:

$$
\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma T_{s} \, e(k) \, \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial \theta}
$$

which KTU examinations often write compactly as $\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma \, e(k) \, \partial e / \partial \theta$ by absorbing $T_{s}$ into $\gamma$.

## 3.2 Derivation: MIT Rule for a First-Order MRAC System

This is the most-asked derivation in KTU previous-year papers.

### 3.2.1 Plant and Reference Model

$$
\dot{y}_{p} = -a_{p} y_{p} + b_{p} u
$$

$$
\dot{y}_{m} = -a_{m} y_{m} + b_{m} r
$$

### 3.2.2 Control Law with Two Adjustable Parameters

$$
u = \theta_{1} r - \theta_{2} y_{p}
$$

### 3.2.3 Error Dynamics

Substituting $u$ into the plant equation:

$$
\dot{y}_{p} = -a_{p} y_{p} + b_{p}(\theta_{1} r - \theta_{2} y_{p}) = -(a_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{2}) y_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{1} r
$$

Subtracting the reference-model equation:

$$
\dot{e} = \dot{y}_{p} - \dot{y}_{m} = -(a_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{2}) y_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{1} r + a_{m} y_{m} - b_{m} r
$$

Grouping terms around $e$:

$$
\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}\!\left[\frac{b_{p}\theta_{1} - b_{m}}{b_{m}} r - \frac{a_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{2} - a_{m}}{b_{m}} y_{p}\right]
$$

Define the *ideal* parameter values that make $e \to 0$:

$$
\theta_{1}^{*} = \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}, \qquad \theta_{2}^{*} = \frac{a_{m} - a_{p}}{b_{p}}
$$

and the parameter-error vector $\phi = [\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}]^{T} = [\theta_{1} - \theta_{1}^{*}, \theta_{2} - \theta_{2}^{*}]^{T}$.

Then the error equation reduces neatly to:

$$
\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}\, \phi^{T} \omega
$$

where the *regressor vector* is $\omega = [\, r,\; -y_{p} \,]^{T}$.

### 3.2.4 MIT Rule for Each Parameter

Applying the MIT Rule to each component of $\theta$:

$$
\dot{\theta}_{1} = -\gamma_{1}\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_{1}}, \qquad \dot{\theta}_{2} = -\gamma_{2}\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_{2}}
$$

**Computing the sensitivity derivatives** under the frozen-parameter assumption:

Because adaptation is slow, we may replace $e$ by its first-order approximation $e \approx 0$ inside $\partial e / \partial \theta$, leaving:

$$
\frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_{1}} \approx \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{m}, \qquad \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta_{2}} \approx -\frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{p}
$$

> **Justification of the frozen-parameter assumption (verbal answer expected by KTU valuators):** "Because $\gamma$ is chosen small, $\theta$ changes slowly relative to the plant dynamics, so during a transient of duration $\sim 1/a_{m}$, $\theta$ can be considered constant. The sensitivity derivative is therefore computed from the linear time-invariant system with $\theta$ frozen."

Substituting:

$$
\dot{\theta}_{1} = -\gamma_{1}\, e\, \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{m} = -\gamma\, e\, y_{m}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_{2} = -\gamma_{2}\, e\, \left(-\frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\right) y_{p} = +\gamma\, e\, y_{p}
$$

(where we have absorbed $b_{m}/b_{p}$ into a redefined $\gamma$ for notational compactness, as KTU problems frequently do).

### 3.2.5 Normalized MIT Rule (KTU Advanced Note)

When the reference signal $r$ (and therefore $y_{m}$, $y_{p}$) is large, the update $\dot{\theta}$ becomes large, leading to instability. The **normalized MIT Rule** divides by the squared signal energy to give bounded adaptation:

$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, \frac{e \,\phi}{1 + \phi^{T}\phi}
$$

This is the form most commonly implemented in real-world adaptive systems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A plant $\dot{y}_{p} = -2 y_{p} + 3 u$ is to follow the reference model $\dot{y}_{m} = -3 y_{m} + 2 r$ using the MIT-rule-based adaptive controller $u = \theta r$. The adaptation gain is $\gamma = 1.5$. The reference input is $r(t) = 1$ (step), $y_{p}(0) = 0$, $y_{m}(0) = 0$. Find $\theta(0.5)$ using the explicit Euler method with $T_{s} = 0.1$ s. Assume the ideal $\theta^{*} = b_{m}/b_{p}$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Ideal parameter:**
$$
\theta^{*} = \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
$$

**Step 2 — Initial conditions and required derivatives:**
- Assume $\theta(0) = 0$ (no prior knowledge).
- Sensitivity derivative: $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta = (b_{m}/b_{p}) y_{m} = (2/3) y_{m}$.

**Step 3 — Iterate (explicit Euler):** at $k = 0$:

- $u(0) = \theta(0) \cdot r = 0$.
- $y_{p}(0) = 0$, $y_{m}(0) = 0$ → $e(0) = 0$.
- Therefore $\theta(1) = \theta(0) = 0$ — the controller does nothing on the first step.

> The KTU-valuation lesson: **if $\theta(0) = 0$ and $r(0) = 0$, the system must first be excited before $\theta$ can update.** Examiners often initialize either $y_{p}(0) \neq 0$ or $r(0) \neq 0$ to avoid this trivial case.

**Assume the question instead starts at $t = 0.2$ s after a transient**, with $y_{m}(0.2) = 0.5$, $y_{p}(0.2) = 0.1$, and $r = 1$. Recomputing:

- $e = y_{p} - y_{m} = 0.1 - 0.5 = -0.4$.
- $\partial y_{p} / \partial \theta = (2/3)(0.5) = 0.3333$.
- Discrete update: $\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma e \,\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta = 0 - 1.5 \cdot (-0.4) \cdot 0.3333 = +0.200$.

After five such steps the parameter converges toward $\theta^{*} = 0.6667$ (the precise trajectory depends on the time-varying values of $y_{m}$, which students would solve via the model equation $y_{m}(t) = (2/3)(1 - e^{-3t})$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's pedagogy:** KTU problems frequently *omit* numerical iteration and instead ask students to **(i) derive the adaptation law** and **(ii) comment on stability** as a function of $\gamma$. Always write out both halves of the answer.

## 3.4 Stability Analysis of the MIT Rule (Lyapunov-Flavoured Argument)

Choose the candidate Lyapunov function:

$$
V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2 \gamma} \, b_{m} \, (\theta - \theta^{*})^{T} (\theta - \theta^{*})
$$

Differentiating and substituting the MIT update $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ yields:

$$
\dot{V} = -a_{m} e^{2} + e \, b_{m}\, \phi^{T}\omega - e\, \phi^{T} \omega = -a_{m} e^{2} \le 0
$$

Hence **$V$ is non-increasing and bounded below by zero**, so $V \to V_{\infty}$ and **$e \to 0$** as $t \to \infty$ (provided $\omega$ remains bounded — i.e., the system stays within its linear regime).

> This Lyapunov analysis is *only* valid for small $\gamma$. For large $\gamma$, the cross-terms $e \,b_{m}\,\phi^{T}\omega$ cannot be cancelled by the $-\gamma e^{2}\phi^{T}\phi$ term, and instability results. **This is the textbook statement KTU expects.**

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Equation

The matrix below is the **decision flowchart** a KTU student should mentally walk through when faced with an exam question:

| Situation in the question | Equation to use |
|:--------------------------|:----------------|
| Single-parameter adaptation, continuous time | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \,\partial e / \partial \theta$ |
| Multi-parameter adaptation | $\dot{\Theta} = -\Gamma e \Phi$ |
| Discrete-time implementation | $\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma e(k)\,\partial e/\partial \theta$ |
| Plant is 1st-order, feed-forward gain | $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta_{1} \approx (b_{m}/b_{p})\,y_{m}$ |
| Plant is 1st-order, feedback gain | $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta_{2} \approx -(b_{m}/b_{p})\,y_{p}$ |
| Need stability check | $\gamma < 2 a_{m} / \Phi_{m}^{2}$ |
| Need bounded adaptation | Use normalized MIT Rule |
| Output grows without bound | Switch to **Lyapunov MRAC** (not MIT) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MIT Rule Adaptation Loop

The figure below is a **block-level functional architecture** of the MIT-rule-based MRAC system. It maps every signal flow between the plant, reference model, error generator, sensitivity block, and adaptation engine — the exact drawing expected in KTU board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input r] --> RM[Reference Model Gm]
    R --> CTRL[Controller u of theta]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant Gp]
    PLANT --> YP[Plant Output yp]
    RM --> YM[Reference Output ym]
    YP --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    YM --> SUM
    SUM --> E[Tracking Error e]
    E --> MIT[MIT Adaptation Engine]
    R --> SENS[Sensitivity Block dyp_dtheta]
    SENS --> MIT
    MIT --> THETA[Parameter Update theta]
    THETA --> CTRL
    E --> OUT[Performance Index J half e squared]
    THETA --> OUT

    classDef sigPath fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E;
    classDef adapt fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F;
    classDef plant fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D;
    class R,YP,YM,E sigPath
    class MIT,SENS,THETA,CTRL adapt
    class PLANT,RM plant
```

**Reading guide for KTU valuation:**

- The **blue path** is the signal-flow path: $r \to y_{p}, y_{m} \to e$.
- The **amber path** is the adaptation path: $r \to \phi \to \dot{\theta} \to \theta \to u$.
- The **green path** is the physical dynamics: plant and reference model.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Adaptation Cycle

The next diagram shows the **time-ordered sequence** of events during a single adaptation step, which is the kind of timeline figure KTU board examiners frequently ask students to sketch.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant R as Reference r of t
    participant C as Controller
    participant P as Plant
    participant M as Reference Model
    participant A as MIT Engine
    R->>C: provide reference signal
    R->>M: drive reference model
    C->>P: send control u of theta
    P-->>A: produce yp of t
    M-->>A: produce ym of t
    A->>A: compute error e = yp minus ym
    A->>A: compute sensitivity dyp_dtheta
    A->>A: update theta using MIT law
    A-->>C: feed new theta back to controller
    Note over A: adaptation gain gamma controls step size
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — MIT Rule vs Lyapunov MRAC

This is a **board-friendly conceptual map** that contrasts the MIT Rule with its successor (Lyapunov MRAC) — a comparison KTU frequently tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Need parameter<br/>adaptation?}
    Q -->|Yes| R{Require formal<br/>stability proof?}
    R -->|No, heuristic OK| MIT[Use MIT Rule<br/>gamma e dE_dtheta]
    R -->|Yes| LYAP[Use Lyapunov MRAC<br/>theta_dot = minus gamma e Phi]
    MIT --> L1[Pros: simple, intuitive]
    MIT --> L2[Cons: no stability guarantee]
    LYAP --> L3[Pros: formally stable]
    LYAP --> L4[Cons: harder to derive]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 marks each — short answer)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the MIT Rule for adaptive control and explain the role of the **adaptation gain** $\gamma$.

**Model Answer:**
The MIT Rule is a gradient-descent adaptation law given by
$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, e \,\partial e / \partial \theta$. The adaptation gain $\gamma > 0$ sets the **learning rate** of the controller — small $\gamma$ yields slow but stable adaptation, large $\gamma$ yields fast adaptation but can cause oscillations or instability. Its proper selection is crucial for the convergence of $\theta$ to its ideal value.

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the **sensitivity derivative** in the context of the MIT Rule. Why is it evaluated under the *frozen-parameter assumption*?

**Model Answer:**
The sensitivity derivative $\partial e / \partial \theta$ is the partial derivative of the tracking error with respect to the adjustable parameter. It indicates *how the error would change if the parameter were perturbed*. It is computed under the **frozen-parameter assumption** — i.e., treating $\theta$ as constant during the transient — because the adaptation gain $\gamma$ is kept small so that $\theta$ evolves much more slowly than the plant states. This linearization makes the sensitivity derivative computable from the standard plant equations and is essential for the tractability of the MIT Rule.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 marks — Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. **Students must answer either Question A OR Question B in full.**

---

### Question A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** *For the first-order plant $\dot{y}_{p} = -a_{p} y_{p} + b_{p} u$ and reference model $\dot{y}_{m} = -a_{m} y_{m} + b_{m} r$, design an MRAC system using the MIT Rule with the control law $u = \theta_{1} r - \theta_{2} y_{p}$. Derive the adaptation laws for $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ and write the complete error-dynamics equation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the MRAC system designed in part (a), derive the condition on the adaptation gain $\gamma$ that guarantees boundedness of the tracking error. Comment on what happens when $\gamma$ is too large.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — Plant and reference model:**
$$
\dot{y}_{p} = -a_{p} y_{p} + b_{p} u, \qquad \dot{y}_{m} = -a_{m} y_{m} + b_{m} r
$$

**Step 2 — Control law:**
$$
u = \theta_{1} r - \theta_{2} y_{p}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the plant equation:**
$$
\dot{y}_{p} = -(a_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{2}) y_{p} + b_{p} \theta_{1} r
$$

**Step 4 — Error dynamics.** Define $e = y_{p} - y_{m}$:
$$
\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}\!\left[\frac{b_{p}\theta_{1} - b_{m}}{b_{m}} r - \frac{a_{p} + b_{p}\theta_{2} - a_{m}}{b_{m}} y_{p}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Ideal parameters** (the values that make the bracketed term vanish):
$$
\theta_{1}^{*} = \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}, \qquad \theta_{2}^{*} = \frac{a_{m} - a_{p}}{b_{p}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compact error equation** with $\phi = \theta - \theta^{*}$:
$$
\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}\,\phi^{T}\omega, \qquad \omega = [\,r,\; -y_{p}\,]^{T}
$$

**Step 7 — Sensitivity derivatives** under the frozen-parameter assumption:
$$
\frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta_{1}} \approx \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{m}, \qquad \frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta_{2}} \approx -\frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{p}
$$

**Step 8 — MIT adaptation laws:**
$$
\dot{\theta}_{1} = -\gamma_{1}\, e\, \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{m} = -\gamma\, e\, y_{m}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_{2} = -\gamma_{2}\, e\, \left(-\frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\right) y_{p} = +\gamma\, e\, y_{p}
$$

(after absorbing the constant $b_{m}/b_{p}$ into a redefined gain $\gamma$, the standard KTU notational form). **Final boxed result:**
$$
\boxed{\;\dot{\theta}_{1} = -\gamma\, e\, y_{m}, \qquad \dot{\theta}_{2} = +\gamma\, e\, y_{p}\;}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Choose a Lyapunov candidate:**
$$
V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2} + \tfrac{b_{m}}{2\gamma}\, (\theta_{1} - \theta_{1}^{*})^{2} + \tfrac{b_{m}}{2\gamma}\, (\theta_{2} - \theta_{2}^{*})^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate and substitute the adaptation laws:**
$$
\dot{V} = e\,\dot{e} + \tfrac{b_{m}}{\gamma}\,(\theta_{1} - \theta_{1}^{*})\,\dot{\theta}_{1} + \tfrac{b_{m}}{\gamma}\,(\theta_{2} - \theta_{2}^{*})\,\dot{\theta}_{2}
$$

Substituting $\dot{e} = -a_{m} e + b_{m}\phi^{T}\omega$ and $\dot{\theta}_{i} = -\gamma e (\partial e/\partial \theta_{i})$ with $\partial e/\partial \theta_{1} \approx y_{m}$ and $\partial e/\partial \theta_{2} \approx -y_{p}$:

$$
\dot{V} = -a_{m} e^{2} + b_{m}\,e\,\phi^{T}\omega - b_{m}\,e\,\phi^{T}\omega = -a_{m} e^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** $\dot{V} = -a_{m} e^{2} \le 0$ guarantees $V$ is non-increasing and bounded below, so $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ (Barbashin-Krasovskii / LaSalle-Yoshizawa).

**Step 4 — Stability bound on $\gamma$:** The above cancellation is exact *only* when the frozen-parameter assumption holds, i.e. when $\gamma$ is *small enough* that the parameter drift during one plant time-constant is negligible. Quantitatively:
$$
\boxed{\;\gamma < \frac{2 a_{m}}{\Phi_{m}^{2}}\;}
$$
where $\Phi_{m} = \sup_{t} \vert \phi(t)\vert$ is the bound on the regressor.

**Step 5 — Effect of large $\gamma$:** If $\gamma$ exceeds this bound, the cross-terms in $\dot{V}$ no longer cancel, $\dot{V}$ can become positive, $V$ grows without bound, and the closed-loop system exhibits **growing oscillations**, **parameter drift**, or **bursting instability**. The plant output diverges from the reference-model output. **Final boxed comment:**
$$
\boxed{\;\text{Too large } \gamma \;\Rightarrow\; \text{oscillation, parameter drift, instability.}\;}
$$

---

### Question B (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the concept of the **MIT Rule** with the help of a suitable performance index. Derive the continuous-time MIT adaptation law.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A first-order plant has transfer function $G_{p}(s) = 1/(s+1)$. The reference model is $G_{m}(s) = 1/(s+2)$. The control law is $u = \theta r$, and the adaptation gain is $\gamma = 1$. Compute the sensitivity derivative $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta$. If at $t = 1$ s the reference input is $r = 1$ (step), compute the parameter update $\Delta\theta$ for a sample period $T_{s} = 0.1$ s, assuming $y_{p}(1) = 0.4$ and $y_{m}(1) = 0.5$.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Step 1 — Definition and performance index:**

> The MIT Rule is a heuristic, gradient-descent-based parameter adaptation law that updates an adjustable controller parameter $\theta$ in the direction that minimizes a chosen performance (cost) index.

The standard cost function used in the derivation is:
$$
J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}(\theta)
$$
where $e$ is the tracking error (typically $e = y_{p} - y_{m}$ in MRAC).

**Step 2 — Gradient of the cost:**
$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$
using the chain rule and the fact that $\partial(\tfrac{1}{2}e^{2})/\partial\theta = e\,\partial e/\partial \theta$.

**Step 3 — Steepest-descent update:**
$$
\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta}
$$

This is the **continuous-time MIT Rule**. The parameter $\gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain.

**Step 4 — Discrete-time version** (often asked as a follow-up):
$$
\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma\, e(k)\, \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical meaning (final exam sentence):** "The parameter $\theta$ is updated in proportion to the error $e$ and the sensitivity $\partial e / \partial \theta$. The negative sign ensures that $\theta$ moves to reduce $|e|$."

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Sensitivity derivative for the first-order plant:**

Plant: $G_{p}(s) = 1/(s+1)$. With $u = \theta r$, the plant output is $y_{p}(s) = G_{p}(s)\,\theta\,R(s) = \theta/(s+1) \cdot R(s)$. Hence:
$$
\frac{\partial Y_{p}(s)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{s+1}\, R(s) = Y_{p}(s) / \theta
$$

Equivalently in time-domain, using $G_{m}(s) = 1/(s+2)$ as the reference and the frozen-parameter approximation:
$$
\frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta} \approx \frac{b_{m}}{b_{p}}\, y_{m}(t)
$$
For our system $b_{p} = 1$ (numerator of $G_{p}$) and $b_{m} = 1$ (numerator of $G_{m}$), so:
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta} = y_{m}(t) \approx 0.5 \text{ at } t=1\text{ s}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Error at $t = 1$ s:**
$$
e(1) = y_{p}(1) - y_{m}(1) = 0.4 - 0.5 = -0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Parameter update over one sampling period:**
$$
\Delta\theta = \theta(k+1) - \theta(k) = -\gamma \, e(k)\, \frac{\partial y_{p}}{\partial \theta}
$$
$$
\Delta\theta = -(1)(-0.1)(0.5) = +0.05
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The parameter $\theta$ increased by **0.05** in this single step, because the error was negative (plant output below reference) and the sensitivity was positive, and the MIT Rule always drives the parameter to *reduce* the magnitude of $e$. **Final boxed answer:**
$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\theta = +0.05\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A *very common* mistake students make is to forget the negative sign in the MIT Rule, producing $\Delta\theta = -0.05$ (wrong direction). Other frequent errors: (i) confusing the sign of $e$ (use $e = y_{p} - y_{m}$, *not* $y_{m} - y_{p}$); (ii) using the *current* $y_{p}$ instead of $y_{m}$ in the sensitivity; (iii) omitting the factor of $b_{m}/b_{p}$ when the plant and model gains differ. **Always check signs in the adaptation law — it is worth 2 marks by itself in KTU valuations.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Origin:** MIT, 1958 (Whitaker, Osburn, Kezer) — for adaptive aircraft control.
- **Core law (continuous):** $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \partial e / \partial \theta$.
- **Core law (discrete):** $\theta(k+1) = \theta(k) - \gamma\, e(k)\, \partial e(k)/\partial \theta$.
- **Performance index:** $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$.
- **Sensitivity derivative** $\partial y_{p}/\partial \theta$ is computed with $\theta$ **frozen**.
- **For 1st-order MRAC** with $u = \theta_{1} r - \theta_{2} y_{p}$:
  - $\dot{\theta}_{1} = -\gamma\, e\, y_{m}$.
  - $\dot{\theta}_{2} = +\gamma\, e\, y_{p}$.
- **Ideal parameters:** $\theta_{1}^{*} = b_{m}/b_{p}$, $\theta_{2}^{*} = (a_{m} - a_{p})/b_{p}$.
- **Stability heuristic:** $\gamma < 2 a_{m} / \Phi_{m}^{2}$.
- **Normalized form** (for safety with large signals): $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma\, e\, \phi / (1 + \phi^{T}\phi)$.
- **Limitations:** No formal stability proof, sensitive to $\gamma$, needs persistent excitation, drift possible.
- **Connection to modern algorithms:** LMS adaptive filters and RNN back-prop are direct descendants of the MIT Rule.
- **Board-favourite contrasts:** MIT Rule vs. Lyapunov MRAC; MIT Rule vs. Gradient-descent optimization; MIT Rule vs. Self-Tuning Regulator (STR).
- **Examiner's mental checklist:** Always (i) state the law, (ii) derive the sensitivities, (iii) check stability, (iv) comment on limitations.

> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In 14-mark derivations, allocate **3 marks to defining the cost function**, **4 marks to deriving the adaptation law**, **4 marks to the sensitivity computation**, and **3 marks to the stability/limitation discussion**. This time-budget mirrors the actual KTU valuation key and ensures marks are not lost on incomplete answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Adaptation Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Adaptation Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptation Gain (γ or Γ):** A positive scalar (or positive-definite matrix) that scales the rate at which the controller parameters are updated in a Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) system or a Self-Tuning Regulator (STR). It is the single most critical *tuning knob* that balances the **conflict between convergence speed and closed-loop stability**.

In the context of the **MIT Rule**, the adaptation gain appears explicitly in the parameter update law:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\gamma \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$
