where $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^{2}(t)$ is the cost function (loss criterion), $e(t) = y_m(t) - y_p(t)$ is the output error between the reference model and the actual plant, and $\theta$ is the vector of adjustable controller parameters.

When $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$, the gradient becomes $\partial J / \partial \theta = e \cdot (\partial e / \partial \theta) = -e \cdot (\partial y_p / \partial \theta)$, giving the canonical MIT update:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\theta}(t) = +\gamma \, e(t) \, \phi(t)\;}$$

where $\phi(t) = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$ is the **sensitivity derivative** (signal that measures how the plant output changes with respect to a parameter change).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Learning Rate" of a Control Engineer

Imagine you are learning to ride a bicycle up a hill. The **steering correction** you apply each second is your *adaptation gain*:

- If your correction is **too small** ($\gamma$ very small) → you wobble slowly and never reach the top of the hill; the system takes hours to adapt.
- If your correction is **too large** ($\gamma$ very big) → you over-steer violently, crash into the bushes; the parameters diverge.
- A **well-chosen $\gamma$** → smooth, fast convergence to the top of the hill without crashing.

The adaptation gain is therefore literally the **"step-size"** of the online gradient-descent search in the parameter space.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Determining this gain analytically (not by trial-and-error) is a *frequently asked* module-4 question. The two classical routes are (i) the **MIT Rule** heuristic and (ii) the **Lyapunov / Stability Theory** route.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Origin of the Adaptation Gain — MIT Rule

In **1958**, researchers at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) proposed the first systematic way to adjust controller parameters in real time. The MIT Rule is a direct application of **steepest-descent** on a quadratic cost of the model-tracking error.

### Step-by-step Logic

1. Define the **loss criterion**: $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}(t)$.
2. The gradient direction that *minimises* $J$ is $-\partial J/\partial \theta$.
3. Move $\theta$ along this gradient with a step size $\gamma > 0$:
   $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \,\partial J/\partial \theta$.
4. Substitute the gradient: $\dot{\theta} = +\gamma \, e \, \phi$.
5. The scalar $\gamma$ is the **adaptation gain**.

### Why Does $\gamma$ Control Stability?

Linearising the closed-loop system about the ideal parameter $\theta^{\*}$ yields a linear time-varying ODE in the error dynamics. The eigenvalues of this ODE depend *linearly* on $\gamma$. A classical sufficient stability bound for a first-order plant is:

$$0 \;<\; \gamma \;<\; \frac{2 a_m}{b_p^{2}\, r_{ss}^{2}}$$

where $a_m$ is the model pole, $b_p$ is the plant high-frequency gain, and $r_{ss}$ is the steady-state reference magnitude.

## 2.2 Normalised MIT Rule — Killing the Divergence Problem

The unnormalised MIT rule suffers from a phenomenon called **parameter drift** or **bursting**: when $e \to 0$ but the reference $r$ keeps varying, the integrator $\dot{\theta} = \gamma e \phi$ keeps accumulating until $\theta$ blows up. The cure is the **Normalised MIT Rule**:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{\gamma \, e \, \phi}{\alpha + \phi^{T}\phi}, \quad \alpha > 0$$

Here, $\alpha$ is a small positive constant (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$). The denominator *automatically* shrinks the effective gain when $\phi$ is large, keeping the closed-loop bounded for **any** positive $\gamma$.

## 2.3 Determination of $\gamma$ via Lyapunov's Direct Method

A more rigorous (and KTU-favoured) route uses a **Lyapunov function candidate**:

$$V(e, \tilde{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}e^{2} + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\,\tilde{\theta}^{T}\tilde{\theta}$$

where $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^{\*}$. Taking the derivative along the trajectories and forcing $\dot{V} \le 0$ yields the **minimum allowable** $\gamma$ for guaranteed asymptotic stability.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Equation** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Range / Units** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \,\partial J/\partial \theta$ | MIT rule generic | — | All adaptive laws |
| 2 | $\dot{\theta} = \gamma \, e \, \phi$ | MIT rule explicit | $\gamma \in (10^{-3},\,10)$ | MRAC, scalar plant |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma \, e \, \phi}{\alpha + \phi^{T}\phi}$ | Normalised MIT rule | $\alpha \in [10^{-3},\,10^{-1}]$ | Robust MRAC |
| 4 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^{T}\tilde{\theta}$ | Lyapunov candidate | Scalar / matrix | Stability proof |
| 5 | $\gamma_{\max} < \dfrac{2 a_m}{b_p^{2}\,r_{ss}^{2}}$ | Stability bound, 1st order | Depends on $b_p$ | Quick design check |
| 6 | $\gamma_{RLS}$ (Recursive Least Squares) | Optimal gain matrix | $\Gamma(n) = \dfrac{P(n-1)\,\phi(n)}{\lambda + \phi^{T}P\phi}$ | STR / identification |
| 7 | $\dot{P} = \beta P - P \phi \phi^{T} P$, $\beta > 0$ | Covariance update (forgetting) | $\beta \in (0,\,1)$ | RLS with exponential forgetting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Adaptive gain selection is used in **autonomous-vehicle lane-keeping** (steering adaptation), **robotic manipulator** control under varying payloads, **aerospace flight-control reconfiguration** after actuator failure, and **wind-turbine pitch control** in changing wind regimes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Adaptation Gain from the MIT Rule (Pedagogical)

**Given:**
- Plant: $\dot{y}_p = -a_p\, y_p + b_p\, u$,  with $a_p > 0$, $b_p > 0$.
- Reference model: $\dot{y}_m = -a_m\, y_m + b_m\, r$.
- Controller: $u = \theta \, r$ (single-parameter adaptation).
- Cost: $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$,  $e = y_m - y_p$.

**Step 1 — Output error dynamics.**
Differentiate $e$:
$\dot{e} = \dot{y}_m - \dot{y}_p = -a_m y_m + b_m r - (-a_p y_p + b_p \theta r)$.

**Step 2 — Define parameter error** $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^{\*}$, where $\theta^{\*} = b_m / b_p$ is the ideal gain that makes the plant match the model.
Then $b_p \theta = b_m + b_p \tilde{\theta}$.

Substitute:
$\dot{e} = -a_m y_m + b_m r + a_p y_p - b_m r - b_p \tilde{\theta} r$
$= -a_m (y_m - y_p) - a_p e \cdot (\dots)$ — after simplification using $a_m = a_p$ for exact model match:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m\, e - b_p\, \tilde{\theta}\, r$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity derivative.**
$y_p = \dfrac{b_p}{s + a_p}\,\theta r$, so the sensitivity is:
$\phi = \dfrac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} = \dfrac{b_p}{s + a_p}\, r$.

**Step 4 — MIT Rule update.**
$\dot{\theta} = \gamma \, e \, \phi = \gamma\, e\, \dfrac{b_p}{s + a_p}\, r$.

**Step 5 — Define filtered sensitivity** $\zeta = \dfrac{b_p}{s + a_p}\, r$. Then:
$\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \gamma\, e\, \zeta$ (since $\theta^{\*}$ is constant).

**Step 6 — Closed-loop system** (in $(e, \tilde{\theta})$ space):
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{e} &= -a_m\, e \;-\; b_p\, \tilde{\theta}\, r \\
\dot{\tilde{\theta}} &= \gamma\, e\, \zeta
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic of conversion:** $\dot{e}$ contains the *plant mismatch* $-b_p \tilde{\theta} r$, and $\dot{\tilde{\theta}}$ is driven by the error $e$ scaled by the adaptation gain $\gamma$. The pair forms a coupled LTV system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stability Bound on $\gamma$ via Lyapunov

**Choose Lyapunov function:**
$$V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} e^{2} + \frac{b_p}{2\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}^{2}$$

> **Why this $V$?** The first term penalises tracking error; the second penalises parameter mismatch, with the factor $1/\gamma$ to make $\dot{V}$ come out cleanly.

**Differentiate along trajectories:**
$$\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{b_p}{\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}$$

Substitute the closed-loop equations (assuming $r$ is approximately constant over the short term so $\zeta \approx r$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= e \left(-a_m\, e - b_p\, \tilde{\theta}\, r\right) + \frac{b_p}{\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}\, \left(\gamma\, e\, r\right) \\
&= -a_m\, e^{2} \;-\; b_p\, e\, \tilde{\theta}\, r \;+\; b_p\, e\, \tilde{\theta}\, r \\
&= -a_m\, e^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\dot{V} = -a_m\, e^{2} \;\le\; 0 \quad (\text{since } a_m > 0)$$

Since $\dot{V}$ is **negative semi-definite** and $V$ is radially unbounded, by **Barbalat's Lemma**, $e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Furthermore, $\dot{V} \equiv 0$ only when $e \equiv 0$, so the equilibrium $(e=0,\, \tilde{\theta}=0)$ is **asymptotically stable** for **any** $\gamma > 0$ under the frozen-$r$ assumption.

> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice that in this simplified analysis, $\gamma$ has *disappeared* from $\dot{V}$. This is the magic of the Lyapunov choice — stability is independent of $\gamma$ in the *ideal* case, but in practice (with time-varying $r$, unmodelled dynamics, and disturbances), $\gamma$ must still be kept small.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Necessary Bound on $\gamma$ for the Unnormalised MIT Rule (Practical Plant)

**Setting:** Plant $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$ with controller $u = \theta r$ and $\theta$ adapted by unnormalised MIT rule.

For a *sinusoidal* reference $r(t) = R \sin(\omega t)$, the average dynamics yield the sufficient stability condition:

$$\gamma < \frac{a_p}{b_p^{2}\, R^{2}/4}$$

A common engineering rule-of-thumb (used in KTU numericals):

$$\boxed{\;\gamma_{\text{safe}} \;\approx\; \frac{a_p}{b_p^{2}\, R_{\max}^{2}}\;}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A plant with a *large* high-frequency gain $b_p$ requires a *small* adaptation gain to remain stable. A *larger* reference amplitude $R$ also forces a *smaller* $\gamma$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Adaptation Gain in the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Identifier

In a **Self-Tuning Regulator**, the adaptation gain is not a scalar but a **matrix** $\Gamma(n)$ that updates each sample:

$$\Gamma(n) = \frac{P(n-1)\,\phi(n)}{\lambda + \phi^{T}(n)\,P(n-1)\,\phi(n)}$$

where:
- $P(n)$ is the covariance matrix (inverse of the Fisher information),
- $\phi(n)$ is the regressor vector,
- $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ is the **forgetting factor** (typical: $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$).

The parameter update is:
$\hat{\theta}(n) = \hat{\theta}(n-1) + \Gamma(n)\, e(n)$.

> **Analytical insight:** RLS gives the *optimal* (minimum-variance) adaptation gain in a stochastic setting. It is the discrete-time analogue of choosing $\gamma$ to minimise the *a posteriori* prediction error.

---

## 3.5 Summary Table — Determination Methods

| **Method** | **Mathematical Basis** | **Output** | **Robustness** | **KTU Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trial-and-error | Simulation | Empirical $\gamma$ | Low | Low |
| MIT Rule heuristic | Gradient descent | Scalar $\gamma$ | Medium | **High** |
| Normalised MIT | Gradient + normalisation | Scalar $\gamma$, scalar $\alpha$ | High | **High** |
| Lyapunov direct method | Lyapunov stability | Lower-bound on $\gamma$ | Very High | **Very High** |
| RLS | Stochastic approximation | Matrix $\Gamma(n)$ | High (optimal) | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of MRAC with Adaptation Gain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r t] --> SUM1((+))
    SUM1 --> MODEL[Reference Model W m s]
    MODEL --> YM[Model Output y m]
    SUM1 --> CONTROLLER[Adjustable Controller theta]
    CONTROLLER --> U[Control Signal u t]
    U --> PLANT[Plant G p s]
    PLANT --> YP[Plant Output y p]
    YM --> SUM2(( - ))
    YP --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> E[Tracking Error e t]
    E --> MIT[MIT Adaptation Law]
    SENS[Sensitivity phi t] --> MIT
    GAMMA[Adaptation Gain GAMMA] --> MIT
    MIT --> THETA[Parameter Update theta dot]
    THETA --> CONTROLLER
    YP --> SENS

    classDef gainBox fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef errorBox fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlBox fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class GAMMA gainBox
    class E,SUM2 errorBox
    class CONTROLLER,MIT,THETA ctrlBox
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Determination Procedure for $\gamma$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Plant Gp s and Model Wm s] --> B[Choose Cost Function J equals 0.5 e squared]
    B --> C[Derive Sensitivity phi equals dy over d theta]
    C --> D{Select Determination Method}
    D -->|Method 1| E1[MIT Rule: gamma as scalar]
    D -->|Method 2| E2[Lyapunov: derive lower bound]
    D -->|Method 3| E3[Normalised: gamma plus alpha]
    D -->|Method 4| E4[RLS: gamma as matrix Gamma n]
    E1 --> F[Simulate closed loop]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G{Check Stability}
    G -->|Unstable| H[Reduce gamma by factor 2 to 10]
    G -->|Too slow| I[Increase gamma slightly]
    H --> F
    I --> F
    G -->|Satisfactory| J[Lock gamma, document value]

    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px

    class D,G decision
    class A,B,C,E1,E2,E3,E4,F,H,I process
    class J terminal
```

## 4.3 Stability-Region Map (Adaptation Gain vs Reference Amplitude)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STABLE[Stable Region]
        S1[gamma small]
        S2[R small]
    end
    subgraph UNSTABLE[Unstable Region]
        U1[gamma large]
        U2[R large]
    end
    BD[Boundary gamma times R squared equals a m over b p squared] --- STABLE
    BD --- UNSTABLE

    classDef stable fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef unstable fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef boundary fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px

    class S1,S2 stable
    class U1,U2 unstable
    class BD boundary
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the map:** Points *below and to the left* of the boundary (low $\gamma$, low $R$) are stable. Points *above and to the right* are unstable. The boundary itself is the *sufficient stability condition* derived in Section 3.3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the adaptation gain used in the MIT Rule for Model Reference Adaptive Control. Why is its choice critical?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The adaptation gain $\gamma$ is a positive constant that scales the rate of parameter update in the MIT Rule: $\dot{\theta} = \gamma \, e \, \phi$, where $e$ is the tracking error and $\phi$ is the sensitivity derivative. **(1 mark)**

Its choice is critical because:
- A **large $\gamma$** gives fast adaptation but may cause parameter divergence and closed-loop instability. **(1 mark)**
- A **small $\gamma$** ensures stability but results in sluggish convergence and poor tracking. **(1 mark)**
Hence, $\gamma$ must be carefully chosen as a compromise between convergence speed and stability.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Normalised MIT Rule. How does it improve robustness over the unnormalised version?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Normalised MIT Rule is:
$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{\gamma \, e \, \phi}{\alpha + \phi^{T}\phi}, \quad \alpha > 0$$ **(1 mark)**

Improvements:
- The denominator $\alpha + \phi^T\phi$ automatically *reduces* the effective gain when $\phi$ is large, preventing parameter drift and bursting. **(1 mark)**
- It guarantees boundedness of all signals for *any* $\gamma > 0$, making the system robust to disturbances and unmodelled dynamics. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Derive the parameter update law using the MIT Rule for a first-order plant $\dot{y}_p = -a_p y_p + b_p u$ with reference model $\dot{y}_m = -a_m y_m + b_m r$ and controller $u = \theta r$. Show clearly how the adaptation gain $\gamma$ appears. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2} + \tfrac{b_p}{2\gamma} \tilde{\theta}^{2}$, prove that the equilibrium is asymptotically stable for any $\gamma > 0$. Comment on the practical limitation of this result. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution (a) — Derivation of MIT Rule (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define the error and cost.
$e = y_m - y_p$,  $J(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Differentiate $J$ with respect to $\theta$:
$\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e \, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} = -e \, \dfrac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Compute the sensitivity derivative.
From the plant: $y_p = \dfrac{b_p}{s+a_p}\,\theta\, r$, hence $\phi \triangleq \dfrac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta} = \dfrac{b_p}{s+a_p}\, r$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Write the MIT update.
$\dot{\theta} = -\gamma \, \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = \gamma\, e\, \phi$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Substitute $\phi$:
$\dot{\theta} = \gamma\, e\, \dfrac{b_p}{s+a_p}\, r$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Define the filtered sensitivity $\zeta = \dfrac{b_p}{s+a_p}\, r$, so the final update is:
$\dot{\theta} = \gamma \, e \, \zeta$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Identify that $\gamma$ is the adaptation gain controlling the *magnitude* of the parameter increment per unit time. **(1 mark)**

---

**Solution (b) — Lyapunov Stability Proof (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Define parameter error $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^{*}$, where $\theta^{*} = b_m/b_p$. Hence $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \dot{\theta}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** From the earlier derivation (Sec. 3.1):
$\dot{e} = -a_m e - b_p \tilde{\theta}\, r$,  $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \gamma\, e\, \zeta$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Choose Lyapunov function:
$V(e, \tilde{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2} + \dfrac{b_p}{2\gamma}\, \tilde{\theta}^{2}$. **(1 mark)**
Clearly $V \ge 0$, $V = 0 \iff e=\tilde{\theta}=0$.

**Step 4:** Differentiate:
$\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \dfrac{b_p}{\gamma}\,\tilde{\theta}\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Substitute (assume $r$ is approximately constant, so $\zeta \approx r$):
$\dot{V} = e(-a_m e - b_p \tilde{\theta} r) + \dfrac{b_p}{\gamma}\,\tilde{\theta}(\gamma e r)$
$= -a_m e^{2} - b_p e \tilde{\theta} r + b_p e \tilde{\theta} r$
$= -a_m e^{2}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Since $a_m > 0$, $\dot{V} \le 0$. By Barbalat's Lemma, $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. The equilibrium $(0, 0)$ is asymptotically stable for **any** $\gamma > 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Practical limitation:** In real systems, $r$ is *not* constant (it varies with time), and there are unmodelled dynamics and disturbances. The frozen-$r$ assumption fails, so the actual stability bound on $\gamma$ is *finite*, not infinite. Engineers must still pick $\gamma$ carefully, often using the rule-of-thumb $\gamma < a_m/(b_p^2 R^2_{\max})$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the method of determining the adaptation gain using the Normalised MIT Rule. Discuss the role of the normalisation constant $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order plant has $a_p = 2$, $b_p = 1.5$. The reference amplitude is $R = 2$. Find the maximum allowable adaptation gain for stable operation using the practical bound $\gamma < a_p / (b_p^{2} R^{2})$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution (a) — Normalised MIT Rule (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The unnormalised MIT rule $\dot{\theta} = \gamma e \phi$ suffers from **parameter drift** when the reference $r$ keeps the integrator active even when $e$ is small. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** The Normalised MIT Rule introduces a *signal-level* normalisation in the denominator:
$\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma\, e\, \phi}{\alpha + \phi^{T}\phi}$, with $\alpha > 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Role of $\alpha$:**
- $\alpha$ sets a *floor* on the denominator, preventing division by zero when $\phi \to 0$. **(1 mark)**
- For very small $\phi$, the effective gain is $\gamma/\alpha$, a constant; for large $\phi$, the gain shrinks as $1/\phi^{T}\phi$, providing automatic gain-scheduling. **(1 mark)**
- Typical values: $\alpha \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-1}]$. Larger $\alpha$ → more conservative (slower but stable); smaller $\alpha$ → faster but riskier. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** With normalisation, the closed-loop system is **bounded for any $\gamma > 0$**, a strong robustness property. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** The trade-off: normalisation slows down convergence when $\phi$ is large, but prevents bursting. In practice, normalised MIT is preferred in industrial implementations. **(1 mark)**

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the given data. **(1 mark)**
$a_p = 2$, $b_p = 1.5$, $R = 2$.

**Step 2:** Recall the stability bound:
$\gamma_{\max} < \dfrac{a_p}{b_p^{2}\, R^{2}}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Substitute numerically. **(1 mark)**
$b_p^{2} = (1.5)^{2} = 2.25$
$R^{2} = (2)^{2} = 4$
$b_p^{2}\, R^{2} = 2.25 \times 4 = 9.0$

**Step 4:** Compute $\gamma_{\max}$. **(1 mark)**
$\gamma_{\max} < \dfrac{2}{9.0} \approx 0.2222$

**Step 5:** Choose a safe engineering value (typically 30-50% of the bound). **(1 mark)**
$\gamma_{\text{design}} \approx 0.1$ (i.e., 45% of the limit)

**Step 6:** Verify dimensionally. $\gamma$ has units of $[\text{time}]/[\text{error}\cdot\text{sensitivity}]$; since $a_p$ is $[\text{time}]^{-1}$ and $b_p^{2}R^{2}$ is dimensionless, $\gamma$ has units consistent with the update law. **(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Conclude: For stable adaptation, select $\gamma \le 0.2$. Going beyond $0.222$ risks instability. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the sign** of $\partial e/\partial \theta = -\partial y_p/\partial \theta$ → results in *negative* $\gamma$ by mistake. Always check: $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma(\partial J/\partial \theta)$ and $\partial J/\partial \theta = -e\,\phi$, so signs flip twice. Final form is **+** $\gamma e \phi$.
> 2. **Confusing adaptation gain $\gamma$ with controller gain $\theta$** — these are two completely different quantities. $\gamma$ tunes the *learning rate*, while $\theta$ is the *parameter being learned*.
> 3. **Writing $\gamma$ as a vector/matrix without specifying** — the scalar $\gamma$ applies to MIT rule; matrix $\Gamma(n)$ applies to RLS. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the wrong symbol is used.
> 4. **Skipping the assumption** "$r$ constant or slowly varying" in the Lyapunov proof → loses the link between $\zeta$ and $r$, and $\dot{V}$ no longer simplifies.
> 5. **Not normalising units** in numerical problems → answer in wrong units loses 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Determination of Adaptation Gain**

- **Definition:** $\gamma$ is the positive scalar that scales parameter updates in $\dot{\theta} = \gamma e \phi$. **[High Frequency]**
- **Two extremes:** $\gamma \to 0$ → no adaptation (open-loop); $\gamma \to \infty$ → parameter explosion. **[High Frequency]**
- **MIT Rule origin (1958):** Steepest descent on $J = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2}$. **[High Frequency]**
- **Normalised MIT Rule:** Adds $\alpha + \phi^{T}\phi$ in the denominator; $\alpha$ is typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$. **[High Frequency]**
- **Lyapunov candidate:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2\gamma} \tilde{\theta}^{T} \tilde{\theta}$ → $\dot{V} = -a_m e^{2} \le 0$. **[High Frequency]**
- **Stability bound (practical):** $\gamma_{\max} < a_p / (b_p^{2} R_{\max}^{2})$. **[Exam Must-Know]**
- **Barbalat's Lemma** is the standard tool to conclude $e \to 0$ from $\dot{V} \le 0$. **[Exam Must-Know]**
- **RLS adaptation gain:** $\Gamma(n) = P(n-1) \phi / (\lambda + \phi^{T} P \phi)$, optimal in a stochastic sense. **[Medium Frequency]**
- **Forgetting factor** $\lambda \in (0.95, 0.99)$ balances tracking vs memory in RLS. **[Medium Frequency]**
- **Persistent excitation** of the reference signal is required for *parameter convergence* (not just error convergence). **[Medium Frequency]**
- **Engineering design heuristic:** Choose $\gamma$ at 30-50% of the theoretical bound; validate by simulation. **[Exam Must-Know]**
- **Common mistake:** Confusing $\gamma$ (adaptation gain) with $\theta$ (parameter being adapted). **[Watch out]**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lyapunov Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lyapunov Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lyapunov Theory** is a mathematical framework developed by Russian mathematician **Aleksandr Mikhailovich Lyapunov** in **1892** for analyzing the stability of dynamical systems, particularly nonlinear and time-varying systems, without explicitly solving the system's differential equations. In the context of adaptive control, Lyapunov theory provides the foundational tool for proving the **stability**, **convergence**, and **boundedness** of adaptive control systems.

The theory establishes conditions under which the state of a system, when perturbed from its equilibrium point, will remain bounded, converge back, or remain stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Lyapunov Theory is the cornerstone of *Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)* stability proofs. Without Lyapunov's direct method, proving the stability of adaptive laws (like the MIT rule replacement) is mathematically impossible.

## 1.2 Fundamental Stability Concepts

| Concept | Definition | Mathematical Statement |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Equilibrium Point** | A state where the system can remain forever at rest | $\dot{x}_e = f(x_e, t) = 0$ |
| **Stability (Lyapunov)** | System stays close to equilibrium for small perturbations | For every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta(\epsilon, t_0) > 0$ |
| **Asymptotic Stability** | Stability + convergence to equilibrium | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert = 0$ |
| **Global Asymptotic Stability** | Asymptotic stability for ALL initial conditions | Holds for any $x(t_0)$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Exponential Stability** | Convergence bounded by an exponential decay | $\vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert \leq K \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert e^{-\alpha(t-t_0)}$ |
| **Instability** | System diverges from equilibrium | $\vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert \to \infty$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Ball-in-a-Bowl System**

Imagine placing a ball inside different shaped bowls on a flat table:

- **Stable System (Concave bowl):** The ball always returns to the bottom of the bowl when pushed. This represents **asymptotic stability**.
- **Lyapunov Stable (Flat bowl bottom):** The ball, when pushed, rolls around but stays within the bowl. This is **Lyapunov stable** but not asymptotically stable.
- **Unstable (Inverted bowl/peak):** The ball, when slightly disturbed, rolls off the table. This is **unstable**.

**Lyapunov's brilliant insight:** Instead of tracking the ball's exact motion (solving ODEs), just measure the *total energy* of the system at any instant. If energy continuously decreases and never increases, the ball must be settling into the bottom — proving stability!

The **Lyapunov function** $V(x)$ acts as a *generalized energy function* of the system. The proof of stability reduces to checking whether $V(x)$ behaves like physical energy: always positive (energy exists) and decreasing over time (energy dissipates).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Positivity Constant:** $c_1, c_2 > 0$ used in bounding $V(x)$
- **Decay Rate:** $\alpha > 0$ for exponential stability bounds
- **Class $\mathcal{K}$, $\mathcal{KL}$ Functions:** Used in modern nonlinear control
- **Equilibrium Point:** $x_e = 0$ (without loss of generality, by coordinate shift)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sign Definiteness of Scalar Functions

A scalar function $V(x): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is classified based on its behavior in a region $\Omega$ around the origin:

| Classification | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Positive Definite (PD)** | $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $\Omega$ | Pure "energy" function |
| **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD)** | $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$ in $\Omega$ | Energy that can be zero in motion |
| **Negative Definite (ND)** | $-V(x)$ is positive definite | Pure "energy sink" |
| **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD)** | $-V(x)$ is positive semi-definite | Energy dissipation |
| **Indefinite** | Takes both positive and negative values | Not a valid Lyapunov function |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Is $V(x) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$ positive definite?"* The answer is YES because $V(x) = 0$ only when $x_1 = x_2 = 0$.

## 2.2 Lyapunov's Direct (Second) Method — Core Logic

The **Direct Method** (also called Lyapunov's Second Method) states:

> **Theorem (Lyapunov's Direct Method for Stability):** If there exists a scalar function $V(x)$ that is **positive definite** and its time derivative $\dot{V}(x)$ along the system trajectories is **negative semi-definite**, then the equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **stable** in the sense of Lyapunov.

> **Theorem (Asymptotic Stability):** If $V(x)$ is **positive definite** and $\dot{V}(x)$ is **negative definite**, then the equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**.

> **Theorem (Global Asymptotic Stability - GAS):** If the above conditions hold for all $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\vert \vert x \vert \vert \to \infty$ (radially unbounded), then the equilibrium is **globally asymptotically stable**.

## 2.3 Lyapunov's Indirect (First) Method

The **Indirect Method** linearizes the nonlinear system around the equilibrium and uses eigenvalues of the linearized system $A = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0}$:

- If all eigenvalues of $A$ have **negative real parts** → **locally asymptotically stable**
- If at least one eigenvalue of $A$ has **positive real part** → **unstable**
- If eigenvalues are on the imaginary axis → **inconclusive** (must use direct method)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Application Context** |
|------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| State-space nonlinear system | $\dot{x} = f(x, t), \quad x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Standard form for Lyapunov analysis |
| Lyapunov function candidate | $V(x): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ | Generalized energy function |
| Lower bound (PD condition) | $V(x) \geq c_1 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2, \quad c_1 > 0$ | Ensures positivity |
| Upper bound (PD condition) | $V(x) \leq c_2 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2, \quad c_2 > 0$ | Boundedness |
| Decay rate inequality | $\dot{V}(x) \leq -c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2, \quad c_3 > 0$ | Energy dissipation |
| Exponential bound | $\vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{c_2}{c_1}} \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert e^{-\frac{c_3}{2c_2}(t-t_0)}$ | Exponential stability proof |
| LaSalle's Invariance Principle | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ NSD, no trajectory stays at $\dot{V}=0$ except $x=0$ | Weaker than $\dot{V}$ ND |
| Linearization matrix | $A = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{x=0}$ | Indirect method |
| Linearized system | $\dot{x} = Ax$ | Local stability analysis |
| MRAC Lyapunov equation | $A^T P + P A = -Q, \quad Q > 0$ | Core MRAC stability proof |
| Parameter error dynamics | $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = -\Gamma \phi e$ | Adaptive law from Lyapunov |
| Mass-Spring-Damper energy | $V = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 + \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ | Physical Lyapunov function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** In all Lyapunov stability proofs, you must verify THREE conditions in order: (1) $V(x)$ is positive definite, (2) $\dot{V}(x)$ is negative definite (or semi-definite), (3) state the conclusion. Skipping any one will cost marks.

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Lyapunov theory is the **mathematical backbone** of:

1. **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC):** Proving convergence of parameter estimates
2. **Self-Tuning Regulators (STR):** Ensuring bounded parameter drift
3. **Neural Network Control:** Stability of learning-based controllers
4. **Robotics:** Proving stability of bipedal walking, manipulator control
5. **Aerospace:** Flight control stability under uncertain conditions
6. **Power Systems:** Stability of interconnected grids
7. **Sliding Mode Control:** Designing sliding surfaces
8. **Backstepping Control:** Recursive Lyapunov design

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Stability Bound

**Problem Setup:** Given that $V(x)$ is positive definite with bounds:

$$c_1 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2 \leq V(x) \leq c_2 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$$

and $\dot{V}(x) \leq -c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$, derive the exponential stability bound.

### **Step 1: Start with the upper bound of V(x)**

From the positive definiteness condition, we have:

$$V(x) \leq c_2 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$$

### **Step 2: Use the lower bound to express $\vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$ in terms of V(x)**

Rearranging the lower bound:

$$\vert \vert x \vert \vert^2 \geq \frac{V(x)}{c_2}$$

### **Step 3: Substitute the bound into the derivative inequality**

The given decay rate is:

$$\dot{V}(x) \leq -c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$$

Substituting $\vert \vert x \vert \vert^2 \geq \frac{V(x)}{c_2}$:

$$\dot{V}(x) \leq -c_3 \cdot \frac{V(x)}{c_2} = -\frac{c_3}{c_2} V(x)$$

### **Step 4: Solve the resulting differential inequality**

We have the first-order linear ODE inequality:

$$\dot{V}(x) + \frac{c_3}{c_2} V(x) \leq 0$$

Using the integrating factor $e^{\frac{c_3}{c_2}t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[V(x) e^{\frac{c_3}{c_2}t}\right] \leq 0$$

Integrating from $t_0$ to $t$:

$$V(x(t)) e^{\frac{c_3}{c_2}t} - V(x(t_0)) e^{\frac{c_3}{c_2}t_0} \leq 0$$

### **Step 5: Solve for $V(x(t))$**

$$V(x(t)) \leq V(x(t_0)) e^{-\frac{c_3}{c_2}(t-t_0)}$$

### **Step 6: Convert to norm bound using both bounds**

Using $V(x(t)) \geq c_1 \vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert^2$ and $V(x(t_0)) \leq c_2 \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert^2$:

$$c_1 \vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert^2 \leq c_2 \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert^2 e^{-\frac{c_3}{c_2}(t-t_0)}$$

### **Step 7: Final exponential bound**

$$\boxed{\vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{c_2}{c_1}} \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert e^{-\frac{c_3}{2c_2}(t-t_0)}}$$

This proves **exponential stability** of the origin.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lyapunov Stability of a Simple 2D System

**System:** Consider $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_1 x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_2$. Determine stability using $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$.

### **Step 1: Verify positive definiteness of V(x)**

$$V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$

$V(0,0) = 0$ ✓ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$ ✓. So $V$ is **positive definite**.

### **Step 2: Compute the time derivative along trajectories**

$$\dot{V}(x) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2$$

Computing partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2$$

### **Step 3: Substitute the system dynamics**

$$\dot{V}(x) = 2x_1(-x_1 + x_1 x_2) + 2x_2(-x_2)$$

### **Step 4: Expand and simplify**

$$\dot{V}(x) = -2x_1^2 + 2x_1^2 x_2 - 2x_2^2$$

### **Step 5: Group terms**

$$\dot{V}(x) = -2(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + 2x_1^2 x_2 = -2V(x) + 2x_1^2 x_2$$

### **Step 6: Determine sign in a local region**

For stability, we need to check $\dot{V}$ in some neighborhood. In a region where $\vert x_2 \vert < 1$:

$$2x_1^2 x_2 \leq 2 \vert x_1^2 \vert \vert x_2 \vert < 2 x_1^2$$

This gives:

$$\dot{V}(x) < -2x_1^2 - 2x_2^2 + 2x_1^2 = -2x_2^2 \leq 0$$

### **Step 7: Apply LaSalle's Principle (since $\dot{V}$ is NSD, not ND)**

$\dot{V} = 0$ only when $x_2 = 0$. On $x_2 = 0$, the system becomes $\dot{x}_1 = -x_1$, $\dot{x}_2 = 0$, so the only invariant set is $x_1 = x_2 = 0$.

### **Step 8: Conclusion**

By **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

## 3.3 Derivation: MRAC Adaptive Law from Lyapunov

**System:** $\dot{x} = ax + bu$ where $a, b$ are unknown. Control law: $u = \theta x$ (state feedback). Define tracking error $e = x - x_m$.

### **Step 1: Formulate the error dynamics**

With $u = \theta^* x$ (ideal control), reference model: $\dot{x}_m = a_m x_m + b_m r$.

Error dynamics: $\dot{e} = a_m e + (\theta - \theta^*)\phi$ where $\phi = b x$ is the regressor.

### **Step 2: Choose Lyapunov function candidate**

$$V(e, \tilde{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^2$$

where $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^*$ is the parameter error and $\gamma > 0$ is the adaptation gain.

### **Step 3: Verify positive definiteness**

$V = 0$ only when $e = 0$ and $\tilde{\theta} = 0$ ✓. $V > 0$ otherwise ✓. So $V$ is **positive definite**.

### **Step 4: Compute $\dot{V}$**

$$\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\tilde{\theta}}$$

Assuming $\theta^*$ is constant: $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \dot{\theta}$

### **Step 5: Substitute error dynamics**

$$\dot{V} = e(a_m e + \tilde{\theta}\phi) + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\theta}$$

### **Step 6: Expand and group terms**

$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + e\phi\tilde{\theta} + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\theta}$$

$$\dot{V} = a_m e^2 + \tilde{\theta}\left(e\phi + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\theta}\right)$$

### **Step 7: Choose adaptive law to cancel the second term**

Set the term in parentheses to zero:

$$e\phi + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\theta} = 0 \implies \dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$$

### **Step 8: Substitute back to obtain $\dot{V}$**

$$\boxed{\dot{V} = a_m e^2 < 0 \quad \text{(since } a_m < 0\text{)}}$$

### **Step 9: Conclude**

Since $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} < 0$, the equilibrium $(e, \tilde{\theta}) = (0, 0)$ is **asymptotically stable**. This proves tracking error $e \to 0$ and parameter $\theta \to \theta^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Power of Lyapunov in Adaptive Control:** Notice how we *derived* the adaptive law $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ directly from the stability requirement. This is called **Lyapunov redesign** — the most rigorous method for deriving adaptive laws, replacing the heuristic MIT rule.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Stability Concept** | **Test Method** | **Conclusion** | **Required Conditions** |
|------------------------|------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Lyapunov Stability | Direct Method | Stable | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ NSD |
| Asymptotic Stability | Direct Method | Asymptotically stable | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ ND |
| Global Asymptotic Stability | Direct Method + Radial Unboundedness | Globally asymptotically stable | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ ND, $V \to \infty$ as $\vert \vert x \vert \vert \to \infty$ |
| Exponential Stability | Direct Method | Exponentially stable | $c_1 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2 \leq V \leq c_2 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$, $\dot{V} \leq -c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$ |
| Linear (Indirect) | Eigenvalue test | Locally asymptotically stable | All eigenvalues of $A$ have $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ |
| Instability (Chetaev's) | Direct Method | Unstable | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ PD in some region |
| Boundedness | Direct Method | Bounded | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ NSD with bounded disturbance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lyapunov Stability Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nonlinear System dot x equals f of x,t] --> B{Equilibrium Point at Origin}
    B --> C[Choose Lyapunov Function V of x]
    C --> D{Is V Positive Definite?}
    D -->|No| E[V is not a valid Lyapunov function]
    D -->|Yes| F[Compute dot V along trajectories]
    F --> G{What is sign of dot V?}
    G -->|dot V less than or equal to 0 NSD| H[Lyapunov Stable]
    G -->|dot V less than 0 ND| I[Asymptotically Stable]
    G -->|V radially unbounded| J[Globally Asymptotically Stable]
    G -->|dot V positive PD| K[Unstable by Chetaev Theorem]
    H --> L[Conclusion: Stable but may not converge]
    I --> M[Conclusion: Converges to equilibrium]
    J --> N[Conclusion: Converges from any initial condition]
    K --> O[Conclusion: System diverges]
```

## 4.2 MRAC Adaptive Law Derivation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input r] --> B[Reference Model]
    B --> C[Desired Output xm]
    A --> D[Plant with Unknown Parameters]
    D --> E[Actual Output x]
    C --> F[Error e equals x minus xm]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Lyapunov Stability Analysis]
    G --> H[Adaptive Law dot theta equals minus gamma e phi]
    H --> I[Controller u equals theta times x]
    I --> D
    G --> J[Stability Proof]
    J --> K[e goes to 0 as t goes to infinity]
    J --> L[theta converges to theta star]
```

## 4.3 Sign Definiteness Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scalar Function V of x] --> B{V at 0 equals 0?}
    B -->|No| C[Not a valid candidate]
    B -->|Yes| D{V of x sign for x not equal to 0}
    D -->|V greater than 0| E[Positive Definite PD]
    D -->|V greater than or equal to 0| F[Positive Semi-Definite PSD]
    D -->|V less than 0| G[Negative Definite ND]
    D -->|V less than or equal to 0| H[Negative Semi-Definite NSD]
    D -->|V takes both signs| I[Indefinite]
    E --> J[Use in Lyapunov with dot V less than 0]
    F --> K[Use with LaSalle Invariance Principle]
    G --> L[Use in Lyapunov with V greater than 0]
    H --> M[Use in Lyapunov with V greater than 0]
    I --> N[Not a valid Lyapunov function]
```

## 4.4 Direct vs Indirect Method Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stability Analysis of Nonlinear System] --> B{Choose Approach}
    B -->|Direct Method| C[Construct V of x]
    C --> D[Check V PD]
    D --> E[Check dot V ND or NSD]
    E --> F[Conclude Stability]
    B -->|Indirect Method| G[Linearize around equilibrium]
    G --> H[Compute Jacobian A]
    H --> I[Find eigenvalues of A]
    I --> J{All Re less than 0?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Locally Asymptotically Stable]
    J -->|No, some greater than 0| L[Unstable]
    J -->|Imaginary axis| M[Inconclusive Use Direct Method]
```

## 4.5 Physical Energy-to-Lyapunov Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical System] --> B[Mass Spring Damper]
    B --> C[Total Energy E equals KE plus PE]
    C --> D[V of x equals one half m dot x squared plus one half k x squared]
    D --> E{V always greater than or equal to 0 PD}
    D --> F[dot V equals minus c dot x squared NSD]
    E --> G[Lyapunov Function]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Convergence to Equilibrium]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**State and explain Lyapunov's stability theorem for asymptotic stability of nonlinear systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Lyapunov's Theorem for Asymptotic Stability:**

If there exists a scalar function $V(x)$ defined on a region $\Omega$ containing the origin such that:

1. $V(x)$ is **positive definite**, i.e., $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $\Omega$
2. $\dot{V}(x)$ is **negative definite**, i.e., $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $\Omega$

Then the equilibrium point $x = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** in $\Omega$.

**Explanation:** The function $V(x)$ acts as a generalized energy function. Condition (1) ensures the system has positive "energy" everywhere except at equilibrium. Condition (2) ensures the energy continuously decreases along trajectories, forcing the state to converge to the equilibrium. If additionally $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\vert \vert x \vert \vert \to \infty$, then the stability is **global**.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Define positive definiteness of a scalar function. Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A scalar function $V(x): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is said to be **positive definite** in a region $\Omega$ containing the origin if:

1. $V(0) = 0$
2. $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in $\Omega$

**Example:** $V(x) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$ is positive definite because:
- $V(0,0) = 0$
- $V(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 > 0$ for any $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$

**Counter-example:** $V(x) = x_1^2$ is only **positive semi-definite** because $V(0, 5) = 0$ with $x \neq 0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Lyapunov's direct method** for stability analysis of nonlinear systems. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for asymptotic stability. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2$, use $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ to prove asymptotic stability. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution (a):**

**Lyapunov's Direct Method:**

The direct method (also called Lyapunov's second method) analyzes stability **without solving the differential equations**. It uses a scalar energy-like function $V(x)$ whose behavior along trajectories determines stability.

**Procedure:**

**Step 1: Define the system and equilibrium.** Consider $\dot{x} = f(x, t)$ with equilibrium at $x = 0$.

**Step 2: Construct a candidate Lyapunov function $V(x)$.** This is the most challenging step — there is no systematic method. Common choices include quadratic forms $V = x^T P x$ where $P$ is positive definite.

**Step 3: Check positive definiteness of $V(x)$.** Verify $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$.

**Step 4: Compute the time derivative along trajectories.** $\dot{V}(x) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} \dot{x} = \nabla V \cdot f(x, t)$

**Step 5: Check the sign of $\dot{V}(x)$ and conclude.**

**Theorems:**

| Theorem | Conditions | Conclusion |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Stability | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ NSD | Stable in Lyapunov sense |
| Asymptotic Stability | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ ND | Asymptotically stable |
| Global Asymptotic Stability | $V$ PD, $\dot{V}$ ND, $V \to \infty$ as $\vert \vert x \vert \vert \to \infty$ | Globally asymptotically stable |
| Exponential Stability | $c_1 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2 \leq V \leq c_2 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$, $\dot{V} \leq -c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$ | Exponentially stable |

**Physical Interpretation:** $V$ represents stored system energy. If energy is positive everywhere (PD) and continuously dissipated (ND derivative), the system must settle at minimum energy — the equilibrium point.

---

#### **Solution (b):**

**Given System:**
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2, \quad \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2$$

**Candidate Lyapunov Function:**
$$V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$

**Step 1: Verify positive definiteness.**

$V(0,0) = 0$ ✓. $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 > 0$ for $(x_1, x_2) \neq (0,0)$ ✓.

Therefore, $V$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 2: Compute partial derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1, \quad \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2$$

**Step 3: Compute $\dot{V}$ using chain rule.**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_1}\dot{x}_1 + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x_2}\dot{x}_2$$

**Step 4: Substitute system dynamics.**

$$\dot{V} = 2x_1 \cdot (x_2) + 2x_2 \cdot (-x_1 - x_2)$$

**Step 5: Expand.**

$$\dot{V} = 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 2x_2^2$$

**Step 6: Simplify.**

$$\dot{V} = -2x_2^2$$

**Step 7: Check sign definiteness.**

$\dot{V} = -2x_2^2 \leq 0$ for all $(x_1, x_2)$. This is **negative semi-definite** (not strictly negative definite because $\dot{V} = 0$ when $x_2 = 0$ even if $x_1 \neq 0$).

**Step 8: Apply LaSalle's Invariance Principle.**

Check where $\dot{V} = 0$: on the set $x_2 = 0$. On this set, the dynamics become $\dot{x}_1 = 0$, $\dot{x}_2 = -x_1$. The only invariant set (where trajectories can stay forever) requires $x_1 = 0$ as well.

**Step 9: Conclusion.**

By **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how **Lyapunov theory is used to derive the adaptive law** in Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC). (7 Marks)

**(b)** Consider the system $\dot{x} = -x^3$. Prove global asymptotic stability using $V(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2$. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution (a):**

**MRAC Adaptive Law via Lyapunov Theory:**

**System Setup:** Consider a first-order plant with unknown parameter:
$$\dot{x} = ax + bu, \quad y = x$$
where $a$ and $b$ are unknown constants.

**Reference Model:** $\dot{x}_m = -a_m x_m + b_m r$, with $a_m > 0$, $b_m > 0$.

**Control Law:** $u = \theta x$ (state feedback with adjustable gain $\theta$).

**Step 1: Define the tracking error.**

$$e = x - x_m$$

**Step 2: Derive the error dynamics.**

$$\dot{e} = \dot{x} - \dot{x}_m = ax + b\theta x - (-a_m x_m + b_m r)$$

Adding and subtracting $-a_m x$:
$$\dot{e} = -a_m e + (a + a_m)x + b\theta x - b_m r$$

Assuming we choose $\theta^*$ such that $a + a_m + b\theta^* = 0$ and $b_m = b$:

$$\dot{e} = -a_m e + b\tilde{\theta}x$$

where $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^*$ is the parameter error and $\phi = bx$ is the regressor.

**Step 3: Choose Lyapunov function candidate.**

$$V(e, \tilde{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^2$$

where $\gamma > 0$ is the **adaptation gain**.

**Step 4: Verify positive definiteness.**

$V = 0$ only when $e = 0$ and $\tilde{\theta} = 0$ ✓. Otherwise $V > 0$ ✓.

**Step 5: Compute $\dot{V}$.**

$$\dot{V} = e\dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\tilde{\theta}}$$

Since $\theta^*$ is constant, $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \dot{\theta}$.

**Step 6: Substitute error dynamics.**

$$\dot{V} = e(-a_m e + b\tilde{\theta}x) + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\theta}$$

$$\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 + b\tilde{\theta}xe + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\theta}$$

**Step 7: Group terms with $\tilde{\theta}$.**

$$\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 + \tilde{\theta}\left(bxe + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\theta}\right)$$

**Step 8: Design adaptive law to cancel the second term.**

Set the term in parentheses to zero:

$$bxe + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\theta} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\theta} = -\gamma bxe = -\gamma e \phi}$$

This is the **adaptive law** derived from Lyapunov theory.

**Step 9: Substitute back to get $\dot{V}$.**

$$\dot{V} = -a_m e^2 < 0 \quad (\text{since } a_m > 0)$$

**Step 10: Conclude.**

Since $V > 0$ and $\dot{V} < 0$, by **Lyapunov's theorem**, the equilibrium $(e, \tilde{\theta}) = (0, 0)$ is **asymptotically stable**. This proves:
- Tracking error $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$
- Parameter $\theta(t) \to \theta^*$ (parameter convergence)

---

#### **Solution (b):**

**Given System:** $\dot{x} = -x^3$

**Candidate Lyapunov Function:**
$$V(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2$$

**Step 1: Verify positive definiteness.**

$V(0) = 0$ ✓. $V(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2 > 0$ for $x \neq 0$ ✓.

Therefore, $V$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 2: Verify radial unboundedness.**

As $\vert x \vert \to \infty$, $V(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2 \to \infty$ ✓.

**Step 3: Compute $\dot{V}$.**

$$\dot{V} = x\dot{x}$$

**Step 4: Substitute system dynamics.**

$$\dot{V} = x(-x^3) = -x^4$$

**Step 5: Check sign definiteness.**

$\dot{V} = -x^4 < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ ✓. $\dot{V} = 0$ only at $x = 0$.

Therefore, $\dot{V}$ is **negative definite**.

**Step 6: Check boundedness condition.**

$\dot{V} = -x^4 = -2(x^2)^2 \leq -2(0)\cdot x^2$ — not quite of the form $-c_3 \vert \vert x \vert \vert^2$. However, $\dot{V} \leq 0$ and $\dot{V} = 0$ only at origin, so by **Lyapunov's theorem**, the origin is asymptotically stable.

**Step 7: Global Asymptotic Stability.**

Since $V$ is radially unbounded and $\dot{V}$ is negative definite globally, the origin is **globally asymptotically stable**.

**Step 8: Exponential Stability Check.**

For exponential stability, we need $\dot{V} \leq -c V$ for some $c > 0$. Check:

$$\dot{V} = -x^4 = -2x^2 \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} = -2V \cdot x^2$$

This is not of the form $-cV$ with constant $c$, so we cannot directly prove exponential stability from $V = \frac{1}{2}x^2$. However, asymptotic stability is guaranteed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> **1. Forgetting to verify positive definiteness:** Many students jump directly to computing $\dot{V}$ without checking if $V$ is PD. This alone can cost **2-3 marks**.
> 
> **2. Confusing NSD with ND:** $\dot{V} = -2x_2^2$ is NSD (not ND) because it equals zero when $x_2 = 0$ even with $x_1 \neq 0$. Use **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**, not direct ND conclusion.
> 
> **3. Algebraic mistakes in $\dot{V}$:** Sign errors in chain rule application are very common. Always write out each substitution step explicitly.
> 
> **4. Forgetting to state the conclusion:** Every Lyapunov problem MUST end with an explicit stability conclusion (Stable / Asymptotically Stable / Globally Asymptotically Stable / Unstable). Without this, marks are deducted.
> 
> **5. Confusing $\tilde{\theta}$ with $\dot{\theta}$:** In MRAC derivations, the Lyapunov function uses $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \theta^*$, and you must use $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \dot{\theta}$ since $\theta^*$ is constant. Markers check this carefully.
> 
> **6. Not stating conditions for global vs local stability:** If $V$ is not radially unbounded, you can only claim **local** asymptotic stability. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist**

✅ **Lyapunov's Direct Method:** Construct $V(x)$, check PD, check $\dot{V}$ sign, conclude stability.

✅ **Lyapunov's Indirect Method:** Linearize via Jacobian $A$, check eigenvalues of $A$.

✅ **Sign Definiteness Rules:**
- PD: $V(0)=0$, $V(x)>0$ for $x \neq 0$
- ND: $-V(x)$ is PD, or $\dot{V}(x)<0$ for $x \neq 0$
- NSD: $V(x) \geq 0$ but can be zero on nontrivial sets
- For Lyapunov stability, $\dot{V}$ can be NSD
- For asymptotic stability, $\dot{V}$ must be ND
- For NSD case, use LaSalle's Invariance Principle

✅ **Standard Lyapunov Functions:**
- Quadratic: $V = x^T P x$, $P$ symmetric positive definite
- Physical energy: $V = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 + \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$
- For MRAC: $V = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^2$

✅ **MRAC Adaptive Law:** $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e \phi$ (derived from Lyapunov, NOT from MIT rule).

✅ **Key Theorems to Remember:**
- Krasovskii's theorem
- LaSalle's invariance principle
- Barbalat's lemma (for parameter convergence)
- Chetaev's instability theorem

✅ **Exponential Stability Bound:** $\vert \vert x(t) \vert \vert \leq \sqrt{\frac{c_2}{c_1}} \vert \vert x(t_0) \vert \vert e^{-\frac{c_3}{2c_2}(t-t_0)}$

✅ **Connection to Adaptive Control:** Lyapunov theory is the only rigorous way to prove stability of adaptive systems with nonlinear parameter updates.

✅ **Common Test Question Patterns:**
- "Verify asymptotic stability of [given system] using $V = x_1^2 + x_2^2$"
- "Derive the adaptive law using Lyapunov stability for [MRAC system]"
- "State the conditions for global asymptotic stability"
- "Apply LaSalle's invariance principle to conclude stability"

✅ **Examiner's Mental Checklist:** (1) Is $V$ PD? (2) Is $\dot{V}$ ND or NSD? (3) Is $V$ radially unbounded (for global)? (4) Explicit conclusion stated?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of MRAS Using Lyapunov Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of MRAS Using Lyapunov Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS)** is a fundamental architecture in adaptive control theory where the performance specifications are encapsulated in the form of a *reference model*. The objective of the adaptive mechanism is to make the output of the adjustable (plant) model asymptotically track the output of the reference model by continuously updating the controller parameters, despite parametric uncertainties in the plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MRAS — KTU Syllabus Definition**
> An MRAS consists of four essential blocks: (i) a **Plant** containing unknown/slowly time-varying parameters, (ii) a **Reference Model** that specifies the desired closed-loop response, (iii) an **Adjustable Model / Controller** containing adjustable parameters, and (iv) an **Adaptation Mechanism** that generates the parameter update laws.

**Lyapunov Theory–Based Design** is a deterministic approach to deriving the adaptive laws. It begins by selecting a *Lyapunov function candidate* $V(e, \tilde{\theta})$ of the tracking error $e$ and parameter error $\tilde{\theta}$. The adaptive law is then chosen so that $\dot{V} \le 0$, ensuring the system is **stable in the sense of Lyapunov**. This guarantees bounded tracking error and bounded parameter estimates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lyapunov?**
> Unlike the MIT rule (gradient-based), the Lyapunov design provides a *mathematical guarantee of global stability* — a property heavily rewarded in KTU board examinations and essential for safety-critical adaptive systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an apprentice calligrapher who is asked to perfectly imitate the strokes of a master calligrapher's handwriting.

- The **master calligrapher** = *Reference Model* (defines the perfect output).
- The **apprentice** = *Adjustable Plant / Model* (whose hand-movement parameters are unknown and being learned).
- The **difference between their writings** = *Tracking Error* $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$.
- The **mentor (Lyapunov theory)** = *Adaptation Mechanism*, who constantly adjusts the apprentice's grip, pressure, and speed based on the deviation from the master's strokes.

The mentor uses a mathematical rule (Lyapunov condition $\dot{V} \le 0$) that *guarantees* the apprentice will never go completely off-track. The apprentice's errors will monotonically shrink — eventually becoming zero or bounded.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols in KTU Board Notation

| Symbol | Physical / Mathematical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $y_m(t)$ | Output of the **reference model** |
| $y_p(t)$ | Output of the **actual plant (process)** |
| $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$ | **Tracking error** (output error) |
| $\theta(t)$ | **True (unknown) plant parameter vector** |
| $\hat{\theta}(t)$ | **Estimated parameter vector** produced by adaptive law |
| $\tilde{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t) - \theta(t)$ | **Parameter estimation error** |
| $V(\cdot)$ | **Lyapunov function candidate** (positive definite scalar) |
| $\gamma > 0$ | **Adaptation gain** (positive scalar tuning parameter) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of MRAS — Four Functional Blocks

1. **Reference Model:** A fixed, stable, linear time-invariant system describing the ideal input-output behaviour.
2. **Plant:** The actual system, with unknown or slowly time-varying parameters $\theta$.
3. **Adjustable Mechanism (Controller / Adjustable Model):** Contains the adjustable parameters $\hat{\theta}$ to be updated.
4. **Adaptation Law Generator:** Computes $\dot{\hat{\theta}}$ based on the state/output error $e$ using the Lyapunov stability condition.

### 2.2 Why Lyapunov? The Underlying Logic

The standard procedure follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Define the Tracking Error:** $e = y_p - y_m$. The goal is to drive $e \rightarrow 0$.
- **Step 2 — Derive the Error Dynamics:** Subtract the reference model equation from the plant equation; the error equation will contain $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ as an additive term.
- **Step 3 — Construct a Lyapunov Function Candidate:** Choose a positive-definite function of the augmented state $\{e, \tilde{\theta}\}$, typically a quadratic form $V = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^T P e + \tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta}\right)$.
- **Step 4 — Differentiate:** Compute $\dot{V}$ along the system trajectories.
- **Step 5 — Apply Lyapunov's Stability Theorem:** Choose $\dot{\hat{\theta}}$ such that $\dot{V} = -e^T Q e \le 0$, with $Q \succ 0$. This *yields the adaptive law*.
- **Step 6 — Invoke Invariance / Barbalat's Lemma:** To prove $e(t) \rightarrow 0$ asymptotically.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lyapunov-based MRAS schemes are deployed in:

- **Aerospace flight control** (aircraft autopilots under mass and aerodynamic uncertainties).
- **Robotic manipulators** with unknown payload inertia.
- **Electric drives** (sensorless speed estimation of induction motors using MRAC).
- **Process control** where plant dynamics drift due to ageing, fouling, or feedstock variation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **highest-yield formulas** for KTU Board Exams on MRAS-Lyapunov design. Memorize the structure of the error equation and the adaptive law form.

| # | Expression | Meaning / Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x}_m = A_m x_m + B_m r$ | Reference model dynamics ($A_m$ Hurwitz) |
| 2 | $\dot{x}_p = A_p x_p + B_p r$ | Plant dynamics (parameters $A_p, B_p$ unknown) |
| 3 | $e = x_p - x_m$ | State tracking error vector |
| 4 | $\dot{e} = A_m e + (A_p - A_m)x_p + (B_p - B_m) r$ | Open error dynamics |
| 5 | $V(e,\tilde{\theta}) = e^T P e + \text{tr}(\tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1} \tilde{\theta})$ | Lyapunov function candidate |
| 6 | $\dot{V} = -e^T Q e + 2 \text{tr}\left(\tilde{\theta}^T \Gamma^{-1}\dot{\tilde{\theta}} + \tilde{\theta}^T \Phi^T P e\right)$ | Time derivative of $V$ |
| 7 | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma \Phi^T P e$ | **Lyapunov adaptive law (gradient-like)** |
| 8 | $\dot{V} = -e^T Q e \le 0$ | Negative semi-definiteness ⇒ stable |
| 9 | $A_m^T P + P A_m = -Q$ | Lyapunov equation (for symmetric $P \succ 0$) |
| 10 | $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ | Parameter error definition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** In KTU valuations, students frequently confuse $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ with $\theta - \hat{\theta}$. This single sign error reverses the adaptive law and loses **2 marks** instantly.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a Lyapunov function of the augmented state?** Because the error alone does not capture parameter convergence. We must ensure *both* $e \rightarrow 0$ *and* $\hat{\theta}$ remains bounded.
- **Why $V$ quadratic in $\tilde{\theta}$?** Quadratic forms are the simplest *positive-definite* candidates and yield linear adaptive laws upon differentiation.
- **Why $\dot{V} = -e^T Q e$?** Negative semi-definiteness guarantees Lyapunov stability; *Barbalat's Lemma* further ensures $e \rightarrow 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> We now derive the **canonical first-order MRAS using Lyapunov theory**, which is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module-4 questions.

### 3.1 System Setup (First-Order Plant and Reference Model)

Consider a first-order plant with unknown gain $K_p$ and known time constant $T$:

$$
\dot{y}_p = -\frac{1}{T} y_p + \frac{K_p}{T} u(t)
$$

The reference model is a stable first-order system:

$$
\dot{y}_m = -\frac{1}{T_m} y_m + \frac{K_m}{T_m} r(t)
$$

The control input is parameterized as:

$$
u(t) = \hat{\theta}(t)\, r(t)
$$

where $\hat{\theta}(t)$ is the **adjustable feed-forward gain** to be updated.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Dynamics

**Step 1 — Define the tracking error:**

$$
e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the error:**

$$
\dot{e} = \dot{y}_p - \dot{y}_m
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the plant and reference model equations:**

$$
\dot{e} = -\frac{1}{T} y_p + \frac{K_p}{T} u(t) - \left(-\frac{1}{T_m} y_m + \frac{K_m}{T_m} r(t)\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u(t) = \hat{\theta}\, r(t)$:**

$$
\dot{e} = -\frac{1}{T} y_p + \frac{K_p \hat{\theta}}{T} r(t) + \frac{1}{T_m} y_m - \frac{K_m}{T_m} r(t)
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce parameter error** $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta^*$ where $\theta^* = \dfrac{K_m T}{K_p T_m}$ is the ideal gain that makes the plant match the reference model exactly.

After algebraic manipulation (grouping terms of $y_p$, $y_m$, $r$), the error dynamics simplify to:

$$
\dot{e} = -a\, e + b\, r(t)\, \tilde{\theta}(t)
$$

where $a = \dfrac{1}{T}$ (positive, since $T>0$) and $b = \dfrac{K_p}{T}$ (positive). **This is the canonical error equation** for MRAS design.

### 3.3 Selection of Lyapunov Function Candidate

We construct a positive-definite scalar function of the augmented state $(e, \tilde{\theta})$:

$$
V(e, \tilde{\theta}) = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma} \tilde{\theta}^2
$$

where $\gamma > 0$ is the **adaptation gain**. Clearly:

$$
V(0,0) = 0, \quad V(e,\tilde{\theta}) > 0 \;\; \forall \;(e,\tilde{\theta}) \neq (0,0)
$$

### 3.4 Time-Derivative of the Lyapunov Function

$$
\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma} \tilde{\theta} \dot{\tilde{\theta}}
$$

Substitute the error dynamics $\dot{e} = -a e + b r \tilde{\theta}$:

$$
\dot{V} = e\left(-a e + b r \tilde{\theta}\right) + \frac{1}{\gamma} \tilde{\theta} \dot{\tilde{\theta}}
$$

**Expand the product:**

$$
\dot{V} = -a e^2 + b r e \tilde{\theta} + \frac{1}{\gamma} \tilde{\theta} \dot{\tilde{\theta}}
$$

**Factor out $\tilde{\theta}$ from the last two terms:**

$$
\dot{V} = -a e^2 + \tilde{\theta}\left( b r e + \frac{1}{\gamma} \dot{\tilde{\theta}}\right)
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of the Adaptive Law

**Impose the stability condition** $\dot{V} \le 0$:

For the term inside the parentheses to vanish identically:

$$
b r e + \frac{1}{\gamma} \dot{\tilde{\theta}} = 0
$$

**Solve for $\dot{\tilde{\theta}}$:**

$$
\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = -\gamma \, b \, r(t) \, e(t)
$$

Since $\theta^*$ is a constant (or slowly varying), $\dot{\tilde{\theta}} = \dot{\hat{\theta}}$. Thus:

$$
\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\gamma \, b \, r(t) \, e(t)
$$

This is the **Lyapunov adaptive law** for the gain adaptation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substituting back**, we have $\dot{V} = -a e^2 \le 0$, which proves **Lyapunov stability**. By **Barbalat's Lemma**, since $e$ and $\dot{e}$ are uniformly continuous and $\dot{V}$ is uniformly negative-definite, we conclude $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

### 3.6 Summary — Final Canonical Results

| Quantity | Final Form |
|---|---|
| Tracking Error | $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$ |
| Error Dynamics | $\dot{e} = -a e + b r(t) \tilde{\theta}$ |
| Lyapunov Function | $V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma} \tilde{\theta}^2$ |
| Adaptive Law | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma b r(t) e(t)$ |
| Stability | $\dot{V} = -a e^2 \le 0$ ⇒ $e \to 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MRAS Block Diagram — Reference Model and Adjustable Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full MRAS architecture under Lyapunov-based design, including the four functional blocks and the adaptive-law feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input r of t] --> RM[Reference Model]
    R --> AP[Adjustable Plant]
    RM --> YM[Reference Output y sub m of t]
    AP --> YP[Plant Output y sub p of t]
    YM --> SUM[Comparator Node]
    YP --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Tracking Error e of t]
    ERR --> AM[Adaptation Mechanism using Lyapunov Law]
    AM --> THETA[Adjustable Parameter theta hat of t]
    THETA --> AP
    ERR -.feedback.-> AP
```

### 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Internal Flow of the Adaptation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Error Signal e of t] --> M1[Multiplier with gamma b r of t]
    R[Input r of t] --> M1
    M1 --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> THETA[theta hat of t]
    THETA --> CTRL[Adjustable Controller u equals theta hat times r]
    CTRL --> PLANT[Plant]
    PLANT --> YP[Plant Output y sub p]
    YP --> SUB[Subtractor]
    REFM[Reference Model] --> SUB
    SUB --> A
```

### 4.3 Lyapunov Stability Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with plant and reference model] --> S2[Define error e equals y sub p minus y sub m]
    S2 --> S3[Derive error dynamics dot e equals minus a e plus b r theta tilde]
    S3 --> S4[Choose Lyapunov function V equals half e squared plus half theta tilde squared over gamma]
    S4 --> S5[Compute dot V equals minus a e squared plus theta tilde times bracket b r e plus dot theta tilde over gamma]
    S5 --> S6[Set bracket to zero to enforce dot V less or equal zero]
    S6 --> S7[Adaptive Law dot theta hat equals minus gamma b r e]
    S7 --> S8[Stability Conclusion dot V equals minus a e squared less or equal zero]
    S8 --> S9[By Barbalats Lemma e of t tends to zero as t tends to infinity]
```

### 4.4 Information Flow Explanation

- The **reference input** $r(t)$ excites **both** the reference model and the adjustable plant in parallel.
- The **comparator** produces the tracking error $e(t) = y_p - y_m$.
- The **adaptation mechanism** computes $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma b r e$ and integrates to obtain $\hat{\theta}(t)$.
- $\hat{\theta}(t)$ modulates the control input $u(t) = \hat{\theta}(t) r(t)$ driving the plant.
- The **closed inner loop** ensures $e(t) \to 0$, i.e., $y_p(t) \to y_m(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS). List its four basic components.**

> **Model Answer:**
> An MRAS is an adaptive control architecture in which the desired performance is specified by a *reference model*, and an *adaptation mechanism* automatically adjusts the controller parameters so that the plant output tracks the reference model output.
>
> **Four Basic Components:**
> 1. **Plant** — with unknown/slowly time-varying parameters.
> 2. **Reference Model** — stable system specifying desired response.
> 3. **Adjustable Mechanism** (controller or adjustable model) — contains tunable parameters $\hat{\theta}$.
> 4. **Adaptation Law Generator** — computes $\dot{\hat{\theta}}$ using a stability criterion.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Lyapunov stability theorem. Why is it preferred over the MIT rule for MRAS design?**

> **Model Answer:**
> **Lyapunov Stability Theorem:** If a scalar function $V(\mathbf{x})$ exists such that $V(\mathbf{0}) = 0$, $V(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, and $\dot{V}(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ along system trajectories, then the equilibrium is *stable*; if $\dot{V} < 0$ strictly, then *asymptotically stable*.
>
> **Why preferred over MIT rule?**
> 1. Provides a **mathematical guarantee of global stability**, whereas MIT rule may lead to instability for large adaptation gains.
> 2. Eliminates trial-and-error tuning of adaptation gain based on heuristics.
> 3. Naturally handles multivariable and nonlinear systems.
> 4. The adaptive law emerges as a direct consequence of the stability condition, not the reverse.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the adaptive law for a first-order MRAS using Lyapunov stability theory, given the plant

$$
\dot{y}_p = -a_p\, y_p + b_p\, u, \qquad u = \hat{\theta}\, r
$$

and reference model

$$
\dot{y}_m = -a_m\, y_m + b_m\, r
$$

Show clearly the construction of the Lyapunov function and the resulting adaptive law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the structure of MRAS. Discuss the role of the adaptation mechanism and prove that $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) — Derivation of Adaptive Law:**

**Step 1 — Define tracking error:** $e = y_p - y_m$.

**Step 2 — Derive error dynamics** by subtracting reference model from plant equation:

$$
\dot{e} = -a_p y_p + b_p \hat{\theta}\, r + a_m y_m - b_m r
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce ideal gain** $\theta^* = \dfrac{b_m}{b_p}$ such that under perfect tuning, $y_p$ would equal $y_m$. Then using $\hat{\theta} = \theta^* + \tilde{\theta}$ and matching terms:

$$
\dot{e} = -a_p e + b_p r\, \tilde{\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose Lyapunov candidate:**

$$
V = \frac{1}{2} e^2 + \frac{1}{2 \gamma} \tilde{\theta}^2, \quad \gamma > 0
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate:**

$$
\dot{V} = e \dot{e} + \frac{1}{\gamma} \tilde{\theta}\, \dot{\tilde{\theta}} = e(-a_p e + b_p r \tilde{\theta}) + \frac{1}{\gamma}\tilde{\theta}\dot{\tilde{\theta}}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = -a_p e^2 + \tilde{\theta}\left(b_p r e + \frac{1}{\gamma}\dot{\tilde{\theta}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Choose adaptive law** to cancel the bracket:

$$
\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \, b_p\, r(t)\, e(t)
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

$$
\dot{V} = -a_p e^2 \le 0
$$

which proves Lyapunov stability. By Barbalat's Lemma, $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) — Block Diagram Explanation and Convergence Proof:**

**Block Diagram Structure** — Draw the standard MRAS diagram showing the parallel paths (reference model and adjustable plant), the comparator producing $e$, and the adaptation mechanism feeding back to the controller (refer to SECTION 4 diagram).

**Role of the Adaptation Mechanism:**
- Continuously monitors the error $e(t)$.
- Computes parameter update $\dot{\hat{\theta}}$ using the Lyapunov-derived law $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma b_p r e$.
- Adjusts controller gain $\hat{\theta}$ such that the plant response matches the reference model.

**Proof that $e(t) \to 0$:**
Since $V$ is bounded below (by zero) and non-increasing ($\dot{V} \le 0$), it converges to a finite limit $V_\infty$. Hence $e \in \mathcal{L}_2$ (square-integrable). Moreover, $\dot{e} = -a_p e + b_p r \tilde{\theta}$ is bounded (assuming bounded $r$ and bounded $\hat{\theta}$ — proven by $\hat{\theta}$ boundedness from $V$ finite). Therefore $\dot{V} = -a_p e^2$ is uniformly continuous. By **Barbalat's Lemma**, $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B** `(Internal Choice)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** For a second-order plant described by

$$
\ddot{y}_p + a_1 \dot{y}_p + a_0 y_p = b_0\, u, \qquad u = \hat{K}\, r
$$

formulate the MRAS with a second-order reference model and derive the adaptive law using Lyapunov theory for the parameter $b_0$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Lyapunov design method with the MIT rule for MRAS. State the merits and limitations of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) — Adaptive Law for Second-Order Plant:**

**Step 1 — Reference model:**

$$
\ddot{y}_m + a_{m1} \dot{y}_m + a_{m0} y_m = b_{m0}\, r
$$

**Step 2 — Tracking error:** $e = y_p - y_m$.

**Step 3 — Error dynamics** (subtract):

$$
\ddot{e} + a_{m1} \dot{e} + a_{m0} e = \tilde{b}\, r
$$

where $\tilde{b} = \hat{b}_0 - b_0$ is the parameter error and the system is assumed matched.

**Step 4 — Write in state-space form** with $\mathbf{x} = [e, \dot{e}]^T$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A_m \mathbf{x} + B\, r\, \tilde{b}
$$

where

$$
A_m = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_{m0} & -a_{m1} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve Lyapunov equation** $A_m^T P + P A_m = -Q$ with $Q = I$, yielding $P = P^T \succ 0$.

**Step 6 — Lyapunov function:**

$$
V(\mathbf{x}, \tilde{b}) = \mathbf{x}^T P \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{\gamma} \tilde{b}^2
$$

**Step 7 — Differentiate:**

$$
\dot{V} = \mathbf{x}^T(A_m^T P + P A_m)\mathbf{x} + 2 \mathbf{x}^T P B r \tilde{b} + \frac{2}{\gamma} \tilde{b} \dot{\tilde{b}}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = -\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} + 2 \tilde{b}\left(\mathbf{x}^T P B r + \frac{1}{\gamma} \dot{\tilde{b}}\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Adaptive law:**

$$
\dot{\hat{b}}_0 = -\gamma \cdot (\mathbf{x}^T P B) \cdot r(t) = -\gamma\, r(t) \, (p_{21} e + p_{22} \dot{e})
$$

where $p_{ij}$ are entries of $P$. Then $\dot{V} = -\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} \le 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) — Comparison of Lyapunov Method vs MIT Rule:**

| Criterion | MIT Rule | Lyapunov Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Gradient descent on a cost function $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e^2$ | Lyapunov stability condition $\dot{V} \le 0$ |
| **Stability guarantee** | Not guaranteed; may diverge for large $\gamma$ | **Globally stable** by construction |
| **Derivation** | Heuristic, requires trial-and-error | Rigorous, mathematically derived |
| **Adaptation law form** | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma e \frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \Phi^T P e$ (problem-specific) |
| **Multivariable extension** | Difficult | Natural via matrix Lyapunov equation |
| **Tuning sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to $\gamma$ | Less sensitive |
| **Computational load** | Lower | Higher (requires solving Lyapunov equation) |
| **Suitability for safety-critical** | Poor | Excellent |

**(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign error in $\tilde{\theta}$:** Writing $\tilde{\theta} = \theta - \hat{\theta}$ instead of $\hat{\theta} - \theta$ inverts the adaptive law. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Missing positive-definiteness proof:** The examiner must see that $V > 0$ for non-zero state. Omitting this loses **1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting to invoke Barbalat's Lemma:** Just showing $\dot{V} \le 0$ proves only stability, *not* convergence. To claim $e \to 0$, Barbalat must be stated. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing $V$ and $\dot{V}$:** Writing $\dot{V} = 0$ instead of $\dot{V} \le 0$ is a common slip. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not mentioning $A_m$ Hurwitz:** Without $A_m$ being Hurwitz, $P$ cannot be solved. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 / MRAS-Lyapunov Design**

- **MRAS = 4 blocks:** Plant + Reference Model + Adjustable Mechanism + Adaptation Mechanism.
- **Goal:** Drive $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t) \to 0$ despite unknown plant parameters.
- **Lyapunov Candidate:** Always quadratic in $(e, \tilde{\theta})$, e.g., $V = \frac{1}{2}e^2 + \frac{1}{2\gamma}\tilde{\theta}^2$.
- **Adaptive Law:** Derived by setting the $\tilde{\theta}$-dependent term in $\dot{V}$ to zero.
- **Stability:** $\dot{V} = -a e^2 \le 0$ ⇒ Lyapunov stable.
- **Convergence:** Requires **Barbalat's Lemma** ($e \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$).
- **Lyapunov Equation:** $A_m^T P + P A_m = -Q$, $Q \succ 0 \Rightarrow P \succ 0$ if $A_m$ Hurwitz.
- **Adaptation gain $\gamma$:** Positive scalar; higher $\gamma$ ⇒ faster adaptation but more oscillations.
- **Sign convention:** $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta$ (estimator minus true); $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma \Phi^T P e$.
- **Lyapunov $\gg$ MIT rule** for stability-critical applications (KTU expects this comparison).
- **Real-world use:** Aerospace autopilots, robotic manipulators, sensorless motor drives, process control.
- **Equation to memorize:**
$$
\boxed{\dot{e} = -a e + b r \tilde{\theta}, \quad \dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\gamma b r e, \quad \dot{V} = -a e^2 \le 0}
$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BIBO Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** BIBO Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
