**BIBO Stability** stands for **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stability**. It is the most fundamental and widely used practical stability criterion in linear, time-invariant (LTI) control systems, and forms the cornerstone for stability analysis in adaptive control theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A dynamical system is said to be **BIBO stable** if, for every bounded input signal $u(t)$, the corresponding output signal $y(t)$ remains bounded for all time $t \geq 0$. Equivalently, there must exist some finite constant $M < \infty$ such that $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M$ for all $t \geq 0$ implies $\vert y(t) \vert \leq K < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$, where $K$ is a finite constant that may depend on $M$ and the system parameters.

### 1.2 Mathematical Restatement

For a causal, relaxed, LTI continuous-time system with impulse response $h(t)$, the output is given by the convolution integral:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau) \, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

If $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$, then:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \cdot \vert u(\tau) \vert \, d\tau \leq M \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

Hence, $y(t)$ remains bounded **if and only if** the impulse response is **absolutely integrable**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Bucket with a Hole" System:**
> Imagine a water bucket where water flows in (input) and leaks out through a tiny hole at the bottom (natural system response).
> - If someone pours water at a controlled, finite rate (bounded input), and the bucket does not overflow (bounded output), the system is **BIBO stable**.
> - If even a small finite pour causes the bucket to overflow because the drain cannot keep up, the system is **BIBO unstable**.
> The size of the hole represents the **system's natural damping**. BIBO stability is essentially asking: *"Will my system always stay within physical limits whenever the input stays within reasonable limits?"*

In adaptive control, where system parameters are constantly tuned, we must guarantee BIBO stability not just at one operating point, but for **all possible operating conditions and parameter updates**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Boundedness Norm**: $\Vert u(t) \Vert_{\infty} = \sup_{t \geq 0} \vert u(t) \vert < \infty$
- **Absolute Integrability (CT)**: $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$
- **Absolute Summability (DT)**: $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$
- **Stability Margin**: Distance of the rightmost pole from the imaginary axis (CT) or unit circle (DT)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define Bounded Signals:**
A scalar signal $u(t)$ is **bounded** if there exists a finite real number $M_u$ such that $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M_u$ for all $t \in [0, \infty)$.

**Step 2 — State the BIBO Criterion:**
A relaxed LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if the zero-state response is bounded for every bounded input.

**Step 3 — Connect to Impulse Response (CT case):**
BIBO stability $\iff$ $h(t)$ is absolutely integrable on $[0, \infty)$.

**Step 4 — Connect to Poles (CT case):**
BIBO stability $\iff$ all poles of $H(s)$ lie strictly in the open left-half of the complex plane, i.e., $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for every pole $p_i$.

**Step 5 — Connect to Poles (DT case):**
BIBO stability $\iff$ all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle, i.e., $\vert p_i \vert < 1$.

**Step 6 — Why "Why" and "How":**
- **Why impulse response?** Because the zero-state response of any LTI system to an arbitrary input is the convolution of $h(t)$ with $u(t)$. If $h(t)$ decays fast enough (absolutely integrable), then no bounded input can drive the convolution integral to infinity.
- **How do poles relate?** Each real pole contributes an exponential $e^{p_i t}$ to $h(t)$. The integral $\int \vert e^{p_i t} \vert dt$ converges only when $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$. Complex conjugate poles contribute $e^{\sigma t} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ form, again requiring $\sigma < 0$.

### 2.2 BIBO Stability Theorem (Continuous-Time LTI)

**Theorem:** For a causal LTI continuous-time system with transfer function $H(s)$ and impulse response $h(t)$, the following are equivalent:

1. The system is BIBO stable.
2. $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ (Absolute Integrability).
3. Every pole of $H(s)$ has a strictly negative real part.
4. The transfer function $H(s)$ is **proper** and **Hurwitz** (no poles on or right of the $j\omega$-axis).

### 2.3 BIBO Stability Theorem (Discrete-Time LTI)

**Theorem:** For a causal LTI discrete-time system with transfer function $H(z)$ and impulse response $h(k)$, the following are equivalent:

1. The system is BIBO stable.
2. $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ (Absolute Summability).
3. Every pole of $H(z)$ lies strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Domain | BIBO Stability Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|--------|--------------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | CT — Time | Impulse response absolutely integrable | $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | $h(t)$ decays to zero fast enough |
| 2 | CT — Frequency | All poles in open left-half $s$-plane | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0 \; \forall i$ | All natural modes decay exponentially |
| 3 | DT — Time | Impulse response absolutely summable | $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ | $h(k)$ decays to zero in discrete steps |
| 4 | DT — Frequency | All poles strictly inside unit circle | $\vert p_i \vert < 1 \; \forall i$ | All natural modes decay geometrically |
| 5 | CT — Bounded Output | $\sup_{t} \vert y(t) \vert \leq M \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$ | Bounded convolution | Output bound scales linearly with input bound |
| 6 | CT — Routh-Hurwitz | First column of Routh array has no sign changes | $\text{RHP poles count} = 0$ | Algebraic pole-location test |
| 7 | DT — Jury Test | All Jury stability conditions satisfied | All roots inside unit circle | Discrete pole-location test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **BIBO stability** is an **external (input-output)** property — it concerns only what happens at the terminals.
> - **Internal stability** (Lyapunov / asymptotic stability of state) is a **stricter** property for state-space systems.
> - For **observable and controllable** LTI systems, BIBO stability $\iff$ internal asymptotic stability. Without these conditions, BIBO stability does **not** imply internal stability.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Adaptive Control Design**: Guarantees the closed-loop system never produces unbounded outputs even during parameter adaptation transients.
- **Aircraft Autopilot Certification**: BIBO stability must be demonstrated for all commanded bounded pilot inputs.
- **Robotic Manipulators**: Ensures bounded torque commands yield bounded joint motions.
- **Process Control**: Prevents runaway temperatures/pressures in chemical plants under bounded set-point changes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BIBO Stability from Convolution (Continuous-Time)

**Starting Point:** Zero-state response of a causal LTI system:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau) \, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Apply the triangle inequality for integrals:**

Taking absolute value of both sides:

$$\vert y(t) \vert = \left\vert \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau) \, u(\tau) \, d\tau \right\vert$$

**Step 2 — Use the property $\vert \int f \vert \leq \int \vert f \vert$:**

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \cdot \vert u(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

*(Logic: The integral of a function's absolute value upper-bounds the absolute value of the integral.)*

**Step 3 — Substitute the bound on $u(\tau)$:** Let $\vert u(\tau) \vert \leq M$ for all $\tau \geq 0$:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

*(Logic: Pulling out the maximum bound of the input reduces the integrand.)*

**Step 4 — Change of variable** $\sigma = t - \tau$, so $d\sigma = -d\tau$. When $\tau = 0$, $\sigma = t$; when $\tau = t$, $\sigma = 0$:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(\sigma) \vert \, d\sigma$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that the integral is monotonically non-decreasing in $t$, so:**

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\sigma) \vert \, d\sigma$$

*(Logic: Since $h(\sigma)$ is integrated over a longer interval, the bound only grows or stays the same, never shrinks. The supremum over all $t$ is achieved as $t \to \infty$.)*

**Step 6 — Final BIBO condition:**

If and only if $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\sigma) \vert \, d\sigma = K < \infty$, then $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \cdot K < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$.

$$\boxed{\; \text{BIBO Stable} \iff \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty \;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Pole-Location Criterion from Impulse Response

**Step 1 — Partial Fraction Expansion of $H(s)$:**

$$H(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{A_i}{s - p_i}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse Laplace Transform (Impulse Response):**

$$h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \, e^{p_i t} \;, \quad t \geq 0$$

*(Logic: Each pole contributes a natural mode with strength $A_i$ and rate $p_i$.)*

**Step 3 — Apply Absolute Integrability Test:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i e^{p_i t} \right\vert dt \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert A_i \vert \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{p_i t} \vert \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inner integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{p_i t} \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{\text{Re}(p_i) t} \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Series Convergence Criterion:**

This integral converges **if and only if** $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$, yielding the value $-\dfrac{1}{\text{Re}(p_i)}$.

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{\sigma t} \, dt = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{-\sigma}, & \sigma < 0 \\ \text{Diverges}, & \sigma \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (CT Pole-Location Theorem):**

$$\boxed{\; \text{BIBO Stable} \iff \text{Re}(p_i) < 0 \text{ for all poles } p_i \;}$$

*(Logic: Every pole must lie strictly in the left half of the $s$-plane. A single pole at $\text{Re}(p_i) \geq 0$ would cause exponential growth or sustained oscillation, making $h(t)$ non-absolutely-integrable.)*

### 3.3 Worked Example — Verifying BIBO Stability

**Problem:** Determine whether the system $H(s) = \dfrac{2}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$ is BIBO stable.

**Step 1 — Factor the denominator:**

$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

So the poles are $p_1 = -1$ and $p_2 = -2$.

**Step 2 — Partial Fraction Expansion:**

$$H(s) = \frac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$ and $B$** using cover-up method:

$$A = \left. \frac{2}{s+2} \right\vert_{s=-1} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

$$B = \left. \frac{2}{s+1} \right\vert_{s=-2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace:**

$$h(t) = 2e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the absolute integrability test:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert 2e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t} \vert \, dt$$

For $t \geq 0$, $2e^{-t} > 2e^{-2t}$ (since $e^{-t} > e^{-2t}$), so the integrand is positive:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} (2e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t}) \, dt = \left[ -2e^{-t} + e^{-2t} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

$$= (0 + 0) - (-2 + 1) = 0 - (-1) = 1 < \infty$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\; \text{The system is BIBO STABLE because both poles } (-1, -2) \text{ have } \text{Re}(p_i) < 0 \;}$$

### 3.4 Edge Case — Pure Imaginary Poles

Consider $H(s) = \dfrac{\omega_n^2}{s^2 + \omega_n^2}$, poles at $p_{1,2} = \pm j\omega_n$.

Impulse response: $h(t) = \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t)$, which is bounded but not absolutely integrable:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert \omega_n \sin(\omega_n t) \vert \, dt = \omega_n \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert \sin(\omega_n t) \vert \, dt = \infty$$

A bounded sinusoidal input at resonance would produce an **unbounded** output. Hence, marginally stable (poles on $j\omega$-axis) systems are **not BIBO stable**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BIBO Stability Concept Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Bounded Input Signal u t] --> sysBlockA[LTI System H s]
    sysBlockA --> outputA[Output Signal y t]
    inputB[Condition sup u t less than M] --> supCheckA{Is Output Bounded}
    outputA --> supCheckA
    sysBlockA --> poleA[System Poles p i]
    poleA --> poleTestA{All Re p i less than 0}
    poleTestA -->|YES| stableA[BIBO STABLE]
    poleTestA -->|NO| unstableA[BIBO UNSTABLE]
    supCheckA -->|YES| stableA
    supCheckA -->|NO| unstableA
    stableA --> convA[Impulse Response h t absolutely integrable]
    unstableA --> convB[Impulse Response diverges or oscillates without decay]
```

### 4.2 Stability Decision Flowchart (Continuous-Time)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given H s] --> orderA[Find Order and Poles]
    orderA --> polesA[Identify all poles p1 p2 ... pn]
    polesA --> realCheckA{Any pole on j omega axis or right half plane}
    realCheckA -->|YES| notStableA[NOT BIBO STABLE]
    realCheckA -->|NO| allLeftA[All poles strictly in LHP]
    allLeftA --> isStableA[BIBO STABLE]
    isStableA --> verifyA[Verify using Routh Hurwitz]
    notStableA --> remedyA[Apply Compensator or Redesign]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BIBO Stability Analysis in Adaptive Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refA[Reference Signal r t] --> compA[Comparator]
    distA[Disturbance d t] --> compA
    compA --> errA[Error Signal e t]
    errA --> ctrlA[Adaptive Controller K t adaptive]
    ctrlA --> plantA[Unknown Plant G s]
    plantA --> outA[Output y t]
    plantA --> fbA[Feedback Path]
    fbA --> compA
    ctrlA --> adaptLawA[Parameter Update Law theta dot]
    adaptLawA --> stabMonitorA[BIBO Stability Analyzer]
    stabMonitorA --> polesA[Pole Location Tracker]
    polesA --> stabCheckA{Bounded Output for Bounded Input}
    stabCheckA -->|YES| passA[System Passes BIBO Test]
    stabCheckA -->|NO| failA[Trigger Redesign or Projection]
```

### 4.4 Pole-Location Geometry Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sPlaneA[s Plane Complex] --> lhpA[LHP Left Half - Re less than 0]
    sPlaneA --> jwA[Imaginary Axis j omega]
    sPlaneA --> rhpA[RHP Right Half - Re greater than 0]
    lhpA --> regionA[STABLE REGION]
    jwA --> regionB[MARGINALLY UNSTABLE]
    rhpA --> regionC[UNSTABLE REGION]
    regionA --> conclusionA[h t decays exponentially]
    regionB --> conclusionB[h t oscillates with constant amplitude]
    regionC --> conclusionC[h t grows unbounded]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Architecture Matters in Adaptive Control:**
> In a standard adaptive control loop, the controller gains are continuously updated. The plant may have time-varying parameters, and the adaptation law must guarantee that **even during transients**, the closed-loop poles remain in the stable region. The BIBO Stability Analyzer block (in the diagram above) tracks poles in real-time and ensures no pole crosses into the RHP — this is the practical embodiment of BIBO stability analysis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define BIBO stability. State and prove the necessary and sufficient condition for BIBO stability of a continuous-time LTI system in terms of its impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** A system is BIBO stable if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

**Necessary and Sufficient Condition (2 Marks):**

For a causal, relaxed, LTI continuous-time system with impulse response $h(t)$, the system is BIBO stable **if and only if**:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

**Proof Sketch:** From convolution $y(t) = \int_0^t h(t-\tau) u(\tau) d\tau$, applying triangle inequality and bounded input $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M$, we get $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_0^\infty \vert h(\tau) \vert d\tau$. The output is bounded iff the integral is finite.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State the pole-location criterion for BIBO stability of a discrete-time LTI system. Why is a marginally stable system not BIBO stable?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Pole-Location Criterion (2 Marks):**
A discrete-time LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_i$.

**Why marginally stable is not BIBO stable (1 Mark):**
A marginally stable system has poles on the unit circle (e.g., $\vert p_i \vert = 1$). The corresponding impulse response terms like $\cos(\omega k)$ are bounded but not absolutely summable. A bounded resonant input can drive the output to unbounded values, violating BIBO stability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake**: Students often confuse *internal stability* with *BIBO stability*. Always specify which is being asked.
> 2. **Pole-Location Errors**: Forgetting that poles must be **strictly** inside the LHP/unit circle, not on the boundary.
> 3. **Skipping Conditions**: Failing to mention "causal" and "relaxed" conditions for the impulse response criterion.
> 4. **No Justification**: Simply stating "poles are in LHP, so stable" without showing the partial fraction expansion and absolute integrability calculation.
> 5. **Notation Mix-up**: Using $s$ for discrete-time and $z$ for continuous-time — always match the domain.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define BIBO stability. Derive the necessary and sufficient condition for BIBO stability of a continuous-time LTI system using the impulse response approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{s+4}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, determine whether the system is BIBO stable. Use both the impulse response method and the pole-location method. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A continuous-time LTI system is said to be BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stable if for any bounded input $u(t)$, i.e., $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$, the corresponding zero-state output $y(t)$ satisfies $\vert y(t) \vert \leq K < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$, where $K$ is a finite constant.

**Derivation (6 Marks):**

**Step 1:** For a causal LTI system, the zero-state response is given by convolution:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau) \, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Taking absolute value:

$$\vert y(t) \vert = \left\vert \int_{0}^{t} h(t-\tau) u(\tau) d\tau \right\vert$$

**Step 3:** Applying triangle inequality:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \cdot \vert u(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\vert u(\tau) \vert \leq M$:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(t-\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Change of variable $\lambda = t - \tau$:

$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_{0}^{t} \vert h(\lambda) \vert \, d\lambda \leq M \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(\lambda) \vert \, d\lambda$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion — Sufficiency and Necessity:

$$\text{If } \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty \text{, then } \vert y(t) \vert \leq MK < \infty$$

Conversely, if the integral diverges, we can construct a bounded input that drives $y(t)$ to infinity, violating BIBO stability.

$$\boxed{\; \text{BIBO stable} \iff \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty \;}$$

#### **Part (b) — Stability Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Factor the denominator:**

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = (s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$$

Verification: $(s+1)(s+2)(s+3) = (s^2 + 3s + 2)(s+3) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 3s^2 + 9s + 2s + 6 = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6$ ✓

**Pole-Location Method (3 Marks):**

Poles are: $p_1 = -1$, $p_2 = -2$, $p_3 = -3$.

Since $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all $i$, all poles lie in the **strict left-half plane**.

$$\therefore \text{The system is BIBO STABLE.}$$

**Impulse Response Method (4 Marks):**

**Step 2: Partial Fraction Expansion:**

$$H(s) = \frac{s+4}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3}$$

**Step 3: Solve using cover-up method:**

$$A = \left. \frac{s+4}{(s+2)(s+3)} \right\vert_{s=-1} = \frac{3}{(1)(2)} = \frac{3}{2}$$

$$B = \left. \frac{s+4}{(s+1)(s+3)} \right\vert_{s=-2} = \frac{2}{(-1)(1)} = -2$$

$$C = \left. \frac{s+4}{(s+1)(s+2)} \right\vert_{s=-3} = \frac{1}{(-2)(-1)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$h(t) = \frac{3}{2} e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 5: Absolute Integrability Test:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left\vert \frac{3}{2}e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-3t} \right\vert dt$$

For $t \geq 0$, all exponential terms are positive and the dominant term is $e^{-t}$. Since each exponential has a negative exponent, the absolute value can be dropped inside the integral:

$$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{3}{2}e^{-t} - 2e^{-2t} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-3t} \right) dt$$

Wait — the terms $-2e^{-2t}$ could dominate. Let us check: at $t = 0$, $h(0) = 1.5 - 2 + 0.5 = 0$. As $t$ increases, $e^{-t}$ dominates over $e^{-2t}$ and $e^{-3t}$, so $h(t) > 0$ for large $t$. The integral of a positive function with all exponentially decaying terms is finite.

Computing explicitly:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{3}{2}e^{-t} dt = \frac{3}{2}, \quad \int_{0}^{\infty} 2e^{-2t} dt = 1, \quad \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2}e^{-3t} dt = \frac{1}{6}$$

Total = $\dfrac{3}{2} - 1 + \dfrac{1}{6} = \dfrac{9 - 6 + 1}{6} = \dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{2}{3} < \infty$

**Step 6: Conclusion:**

Since $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert \, dt = \dfrac{2}{3} < \infty$, the impulse response is absolutely integrable.

$$\boxed{\; \text{Both methods confirm: The system is BIBO STABLE.} \;}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the BIBO stability criteria in continuous-time and discrete-time domains. Discuss why a marginally stable system fails the BIBO test, with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback control system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is BIBO stable using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Comparison and Marginal Stability (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| Impulse response test | $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ | $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ |
| Pole-location test | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all $p_i$ | $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for all $p_i$ |
| Stable region | Open left-half $s$-plane | Interior of unit circle in $z$-plane |
| Natural mode form | $e^{p_i t}$ | $p_i^k$ |
| Stability test method | Routh-Hurwitz | Jury test / Schur-Cohn |
| Boundary behavior | Poles on $j\omega$-axis → marginally stable | Poles on unit circle → marginally stable |

**Marginal Stability Failure (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why marginally stable systems are NOT BIBO stable:**
> A marginally stable system has poles on the stability boundary (imaginary axis for CT, unit circle for DT). Its impulse response contains **sustained oscillation terms** that neither decay nor grow. Such $h(t)$ is bounded but **not absolutely integrable**.

**Example:** Consider $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + 1}$, with poles at $p = \pm j$.

Impulse response: $h(t) = \sin(t)$

Absolute integrability:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert \sin(t) \vert \, dt = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \int_{n\pi}^{(n+1)\pi} \vert \sin(t) \vert \, dt = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2 = \infty$$

**Resonant input test:** For $u(t) = \sin(t)$ (bounded, $\vert u \vert \leq 1$):

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \sin(t-\tau) \sin(\tau) \, d\tau = \frac{1}{2}\left[t - \sin(t)\cos(t)\right]$$

As $t \to \infty$, $y(t) \to \infty$. The output becomes unbounded.

$$\therefore \text{Marginally stable} \not\Rightarrow \text{BIBO stable}$$

#### **Part (b) — Range of K using Routh-Hurwitz (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

With unity feedback, $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}}{1 + \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}} = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5) + K}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic Polynomial:**

$$s(s+2)(s+5) + K = s(s^2 + 7s + 10) + K = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K$$

**Step 3: Routh Array:**

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 10 \\
s^2 & 7 & K \\
s^1 & \dfrac{7 \cdot 10 - 1 \cdot K}{7} & 0 \\
s^0 & K & 0
\end{array}$$

Computing the $s^1$ row:

$$b_1 = \frac{7 \cdot 10 - 1 \cdot K}{7} = \frac{70 - K}{7}$$

**Step 4: Routh-Hurwitz Stability Conditions:**

For all poles in LHP, the first column must have all positive entries:

- Row $s^3$: $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- Row $s^2$: $7 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- Row $s^1$: $\dfrac{70 - K}{7} > 0 \implies K < 70$
- Row $s^0$: $K > 0$

**Step 5: Range of K:**

$$\boxed{\; 0 < K < 70 \;}$$

For any $K$ in this range, the closed-loop system is BIBO stable. If $K = 70$, a pole lies on the $j\omega$-axis (marginally stable, not BIBO stable). If $K > 70$, the system becomes BIBO unstable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — BIBO Stability**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ BIBO stable: bounded input $\Rightarrow$ bounded output
- ☐ Applies to **causal, relaxed, LTI** systems
- ☐ Equivalent to **absolute integrability** of impulse response (CT)
- ☐ Equivalent to **absolute summability** of impulse response (DT)

**Pole-Location Rules:**
- ☐ **Continuous-time**: All poles in open LHP ($\text{Re}(p) < 0$)
- ☐ **Discrete-time**: All poles strictly inside unit circle ($\vert p \vert < 1$)
- ☐ **Poles on the boundary** (marginally stable) $\Rightarrow$ **NOT BIBO stable**
- ☐ **RHP / outside-unit-circle poles** $\Rightarrow$ **BIBO unstable**

**Stability Tests:**
- ☐ **CT**: Routh-Hurwitz criterion
- ☐ **DT**: Jury test or Schur-Cohn criterion
- ☐ **Frequency domain**: Bode plot phase/gain margins, Nyquist criterion

**Key Theorems:**
- ☐ Convolution inequality: $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M \int_0^\infty \vert h(\tau) \vert d\tau$
- ☐ For observable + controllable LTI systems: BIBO $\iff$ asymptotic internal stability
- ☐ Marginal stability fails BIBO test (resonance example)

**Common Pitfalls:**
- ☐ Don't confuse BIBO stability with Lyapunov stability
- ☐ "Strictly" inside the stable region — no boundary inclusion
- ☐ Check for pole-zero cancellations — they may hide unstable modes
- ☐ In adaptive control, BIBO must hold **uniformly** across parameter trajectories

**Adaptive Control Connection:**
- ☐ MRAC (Model Reference Adaptive Control) uses BIBO + Lyapunov arguments
- ☐ Parameter projection / $\sigma$-modification ensure BIBO stability despite bounded disturbances
- ☐ Persistence of excitation needed for parameter convergence but not directly for BIBO

**High-Yield Exam Formulas:**
- ☐ $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ (CT condition)
- ☐ $\sum_{k=0}^\infty \vert h(k) \vert < \infty$ (DT condition)
- ☐ $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ (CT pole test)
- ☐ $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ (DT pole test)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Output Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Output Feedback** in adaptive control refers to the class of adaptive control schemes in which the controller generates the control input $u(t)$ using **only the measured output** $y_p(t)$ of the plant, without access to the full state vector $x(t)$. In this setting, the plant is generally described by an I/O differential equation or a transfer function with *unknown* parameters, and a tracking or regulation goal must be achieved using measurements restricted to the output.

For the KTU Module-4 syllabus (PERUT868), the topic is developed through the lens of **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) with output feedback**, where the control law is constructed using filtered input–output signals (regressors) rather than the actual internal state $x$.

Mathematically, the plant is taken as a SISO linear time-invariant system of *known relative degree* $n^*$, written as

$$y_p(t) = W_p(s)\,[\,u(t)\,], \qquad W_p(s) = K_p \frac{Z_p(s)}{R_p(s)}$$

with unknown gain $K_p$ and unknown numerator/denominator polynomials. The control law is restricted to the form

$$u(t) = \theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)$$

where $\omega(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ is a vector of *measurable* (filterable) input/output signals, and $\theta(t)$ is the adaptive parameter vector. The adaptation law updates $\theta$ so that the closed-loop output tracks a chosen reference-model output $y_m$ given by $y_m = W_m(s)[r]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, PERUT868 Module 4):** The treatment of Output Feedback centres on three pillars —
> 1. The **augmented error** formulation of Monopoli (for relative degree $n^* \geq 1$),
> 2. The **SPR / passivity** condition on the error model,
> 3. The **Lyapunov-type adaptive law** that guarantees boundedness and tracking.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car in a dense fog. You cannot see the road-signs (the *states*), but you can still use the **speedometer** (the *output* $y_p$) to keep the car at a steady reference speed. The brain continuously compares the actual speed to the target speed, estimates (adapts) the pedal pressure, and ignores the fact that it cannot see beyond the bonnet. The speedometer reading is the *only* signal available for control, and the adaptation engine uses past input–output data to guess the car's behaviour.

In the same way, an output-feedback adaptive controller:

- Treats $y_p$ as the *only* trustworthy measurement.
- Constructs internal *filtered* versions of $u$ and $y_p$ that mimic the inaccessible states.
- Adjusts $\theta$ until $y_p$ behaves exactly like the *reference model* $y_m$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just measure the states?** In practice, states are either too expensive to measure (e.g., concentrations in a chemical reactor, structural displacements of a flexible beam) or simply inaccessible. Output feedback bridges the gap between the elegant state-feedback theory and the *real* industrial world.

## 1.3 Notation and Core Symbols (KTU Board-Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| $W_p(s)$ | Plant transfer function (unknown) |
| $K_p$ | Plant high-frequency gain (sign known) |
| $n^*$ | Plant relative degree |
| $W_m(s)$ | Reference-model transfer function (designer's choice) |
| $\theta(t)$ | Adaptive parameter vector |
| $\omega(t)$ | Regressor / filtered input–output vector |
| $e(t)$ | Output tracking error $y_p - y_m$ |
| $\varepsilon_1(t)$ | Augmented error |
| $\Lambda(s)$ | SPR filter polynomial |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Output Feedback is Harder than State Feedback

In **state-feedback MRAC**, the certainty-equivalence principle allows the designer to place closed-loop poles arbitrarily and the SPR condition is automatically satisfied because the error model is *relative degree one*. With **output feedback**, two fundamental difficulties appear:

1. The state $x$ is unknown, so the regressor vector $\omega$ must be built using *filtered* versions of the input $u$ and the output $y_p$.
2. The resulting error model $e = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$ is, in general, **not strictly positive real** unless $n^* = 1$. For $n^* \geq 2$ the SPR property is lost, and naive gradient laws fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution (Monopoli, 1974):** Augment the scalar error $e$ with an auxiliary term so that the *augmented* error $\varepsilon_1$ is generated by an SPR transfer function, and adapt on $\varepsilon_1$ instead of $e$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Output-Feedback MRAC

**Step 1 — Plant assumption (KTU standard form).**
The plant is SISO, of order $n$, minimum-phase, with known relative degree $n^* = n - m \geq 1$ (where $m = \deg Z_p$). The sign of $K_p$ is known.

**Step 2 — Choice of reference model.**
Select $W_m(s)$ *strictly positive real* and *stable*, with the same relative degree $n^*$, e.g.

$$W_m(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$

for a second-order system.

**Step 3 — Filtered regressors.**
Let $F(s) = \dfrac{\lambda(s)}{\Lambda(s)}$ be a stable filter of relative degree $\geq n^*$. Define the $(2n)$-dimensional regressor

$$\omega(t) = \left[\,\frac{\lambda(s)}{\Lambda(s)}[\,u\,],\,\frac{\lambda(s)}{\Lambda(s)}[\,y_p\,]\,\right]^{\top}$$

**Step 4 — Control law (certainty equivalence).**

$$u(t) = \theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)$$

**Step 5 — Error equation.**
Define $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$. Using the matching-equation condition, one obtains

$$e = W_m(s)\,\bigl[\,\rho^*\,u - \theta^{\top}\omega\,\bigr] = W_m(s)\,\bigl[\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega\,\bigr]$$

where $\rho^* = K_p / K_m > 0$ and $\tilde\theta = \theta - \theta^*$ is the parameter error.

**Step 6 — Augmented error (Monopoli).**
The signal $\varepsilon_1$ is constructed as

$$\varepsilon_1(t) = e(t) + \theta^{\top}(t)\,W_m(s)\,[\omega(t)] - \theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)$$

so that

$$\varepsilon_1 = W_m(s)\,\bigl[\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega\,\bigr]$$

**Step 7 — Adaptive law.**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1(t)\,\omega(t), \qquad \Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$$

(with a leakage / $\sigma$-modification term added in robust variants).

**Step 8 — Stability.**
Because $W_m(s)$ is SPR, the error ODE $\dot{e} = A_m e + b_m\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega$ admits a Lyapunov function

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,e^{\top}P e + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\tilde\theta, \qquad P A_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q, \; P b_m = c_m$$

that yields $\dot V \le 0$, guaranteeing **boundedness of all signals and asymptotic tracking** ($e(t) \to 0$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | $W_p(s) = K_p\,Z_p(s)/R_p(s)$ | Plant TF, $\deg R_p = n$, $\deg Z_p = m$, $n^* = n-m$ |
| 2 | $W_m(s)$ SPR, stable, $\deg R_m - \deg Z_m = n^*$ | Reference-model TF |
| 3 | $\omega = [F(s)u,\; F(s)y_p]^{\top}$ | Filtered regressor, $F = \lambda/\Lambda$, $\Lambda$ monic Hurwitz |
| 4 | $u = \theta^{\top}\omega$ | Control law |
| 5 | $e = y_p - y_m$ | Output tracking error |
| 6 | $\rho^* = K_p / K_m$ | Plant-to-model gain ratio (sign known) |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_1 = e + \theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega] - \theta^{\top}\omega$ | **Monopoli augmented error** |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_1 = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$ | Error system in parameter error |
| 9 | $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega,\; \Gamma > 0$ | **MRAC adaptive law** |
| 10 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\tilde\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}e^{\top}P e$ | Lyapunov function |
| 11 | $P A_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q$, $P b_m = c_m$ | KYP / Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov conditions |
| 12 | $\dot V = -\tfrac{1}{2}e^{\top}Q e \le 0$ | Stability derivative (SPR case) |
| 13 | $\sigma$-mod: $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega - \sigma\Gamma\theta$ | Robust adaptive law (adds leakage) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-exam tip:** Memorise equations 7, 8, 9 and 11 — they appear in **almost every** Module-4 numerical or derivation question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Output-Feedback MRAC |
| --- | --- |
| Aerospace | Re-entry vehicle attitude control (only IMU measurements) |
| Robotics | Joint-position tracking when velocity sensors fail |
| Process Control | Distillation column temperature control |
| Automotive | Active suspension (only accelerometer output) |
| Biomedical | Insulin-pump dosing from blood-glucose sensor only |
| Power Systems | Frequency regulation using only bus-frequency measurements |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output-Feedback Error Equation

### Starting point

Plant and reference model are

$$y_p = W_p(s)\,u, \qquad y_m = W_m(s)\,r$$

with $W_p(s) = K_p\,\dfrac{Z_p(s)}{R_p(s)}$, $\;W_m(s) = K_m\,\dfrac{Z_m(s)}{R_m(s)}$.

The control law is $u = \theta^{\top}\omega$, and the regressor $\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ is generated by the stable filter $F(s) = \lambda(s)/\Lambda(s)$ of relative degree $\geq n^*$ as

$$\omega_1 = F(s)\,u, \qquad \omega_2 = F(s)\,y_p$$

so $\omega = [\omega_1^{\top}, \omega_2^{\top}]^{\top}$.

### Step 1 — Matching Equation

There exists a unique constant parameter vector $\theta^* \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$ such that, when the true plant is excited by the regressor $\omega$, the closed-loop transfer function matches the reference model:

$$W_m(s) = \frac{\theta^{*\top}\,\omega}{r}\bigg/_{\text{steady-state}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad W_p(s)\,\bigl[\theta^{*\top}\omega\bigr] = W_m(s)[r]$$

The existence of $\theta^*$ is guaranteed by the *Diophantine equation* over the Bezout ring of stable polynomials.

### Step 2 — Form the tracking error

$$e = y_p - y_m = W_p(s)\,u - W_m(s)[r]$$

Using the matching condition $W_m(s)[\theta^{*\top}\omega] = W_p(s)[\theta^{*\top}\omega]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= W_p(s)\bigl[\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr] - W_m(s)[r] \\
  &= W_p(s)\bigl[\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr] - W_m(s)\bigl[\theta^{*\top}\omega\bigr] + W_m(s)\bigl[\theta^{*\top}\omega\bigr] - W_m(s)[r] \\
  &= \bigl(W_p(s) - W_m(s)\bigr)\bigl[\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr] + W_m(s)\bigl[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\tilde\theta = \theta - \theta^*$ is the *parameter error vector*. Choosing the structure of $\omega$ so that the first term vanishes (this is exactly the role of the Diophantine solution), we obtain the **fundamental error equation**

$$\boxed{\;e(t) = W_m(s)\,\bigl[\tilde\theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)\bigr]\;}$$

This is the key starting point of all output-feedback MRAC analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Augmented Error $\varepsilon_1$

Define the *auxiliary* signal

$$\hat e(t) = \theta^{\top}(t)\,\bigl[\,W_m(s)\,\omega(t)\,\bigr] - \theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)$$

Note: $W_m(s)$ is implemented in real time using the *known* reference-model state-space realization; the filter is therefore *causal and implementable*.

Now form

$$\varepsilon_1 \triangleq e + \hat e$$

Substituting $e = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$ and noting that $W_m(s)[\theta^{\top}\omega] = W_m(s)[\theta^{*\top}\omega] + W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega] = W_p(s)[\theta^{\top}\omega] + W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= W_m(s)\bigl[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr] + \theta^{\top}\bigl[W_m(s)\omega\bigr] - \theta^{\top}\omega \\
            &= W_m(s)\bigl[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega\bigr] + \theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega] - \theta^{\top}\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

But $W_m(s)$ is **SPR**, so by the KYP lemma there exists a state-space realisation $(A_m, b_m, c_m^{\top})$ with $c_m^{\top}(sI - A_m)^{-1} b_m = W_m(s)$ and the transfer function from $\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega$ to $\varepsilon_1$ is *exactly* $W_m(s)$. Therefore

$$\boxed{\;\varepsilon_1(t) = W_m(s)\,\bigl[\tilde\theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)\bigr]\;}$$

**Crucial observation:** $\varepsilon_1$ is generated by the same SPR transfer function as $e$, but unlike $e$ it is **computable in real time** because both $e$ and $\theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega]$ are measurable. This is the central insight of Monopoli (1974).

## 3.3 Stability Proof (Lyapunov / Passivity)

The augmented error is governed by the state-space form of the SPR model:

$$\dot x_m = A_m x_m + b_m\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega, \qquad \varepsilon_1 = c_m^{\top} x_m$$

Define the positive-definite Lyapunov candidate

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,x_m^{\top}P x_m + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\tilde\theta, \qquad P = P^{\top} > 0$$

Differentiate along the trajectories:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot V &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,x_m^{\top}\!\bigl(A_m^{\top}P + PA_m\bigr)x_m + x_m^{\top}Pb_m\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega + \tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot{\tilde\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the KYP identities $PA_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q = -Q^{\top} < 0$ and $Pb_m = c_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot V &= -\tfrac{1}{2}\,x_m^{\top}Q x_m + \varepsilon_1\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega + \tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot{\tilde\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose the adaptive law

$$\dot{\tilde\theta} = \dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega$$

Substituting, the second and third terms cancel exactly:

$$
\dot V = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,x_m^{\top}Q x_m \le 0
$$

### Conclusions

1. $\tilde\theta, x_m, \theta \in \mathcal{L}_\infty$ — **all signals bounded**.
2. $\int_0^{\infty} \varepsilon_1^2(\tau)\,d\tau < \infty$ — **square-integrable error**.
3. By Barbalat's Lemma, $\lim_{t\to\infty} \varepsilon_1(t) = 0$, and hence $\lim_{t\to\infty} e(t) = 0$ — **asymptotic tracking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SPR is essential:** The KYP lemma guarantees the existence of $P > 0$ solving the pair of equations in step 11 of the formula sheet *iff* $W_m(s)$ is SPR. If we had chosen a non-SPR $W_m(s)$, the candidate Lyapunov function could not be sign-definite and stability could not be established.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A first-order plant has $W_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a}$ with $K_p > 0$, $a$ unknown. The reference model is $W_m(s) = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$ (SPR). Choose the adaptive law so that $y_p \to y_m$.

**Solution path:**

1. Order $n = 1$, so $\omega = [\omega_1, \omega_2]^{\top}$ with $\omega_1 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}[u]$, $\omega_2 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}[y_p]$.
2. Control law: $u = \theta_1\,\omega_1 + \theta_2\,\omega_2$.
3. Augmented error: $\varepsilon_1 = e + \theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega] - \theta^{\top}\omega$.
4. Adaptive law: $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega$ with $\Gamma = \text{diag}\{\gamma_1,\gamma_2\}$, $\gamma_i > 0$.
5. Stability follows from $W_m(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ being SPR (state-space form $A_m = -1$, $b_m = 1$, $c_m = 1$, $P = 1$, $Q = 2$).
6. **Conclusion:** $\lim_{t\to\infty} e(t) = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Output-Feedback MRAC (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference r"] --> nodeRm["Reference Model W_m"]
    nodeRm -- y_m --> nodeSum["Summing Junction e = y_p - y_m"]
    nodeP["Plant W_p s"] -- y_p --> nodeSum
    nodeP -- u --> nodeF1["Filter F s for u"]
    nodeP -- y_p --> nodeF2["Filter F s for y_p"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeOmega["Regressor omega in R 2n"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeOmega
    nodeOmega --> nodeCtrl["Adaptive Law u = theta trans omega"]
    nodeCtrl -- u --> nodeP
    nodeSum --> nodeAug["Augmented Error e1 = e + theta trans W_m omega - theta trans omega"]
    nodeCtrl --> nodeAug
    nodeRm --> nodeAug
    nodeAug -- e1 --> nodeAdapt["Adaptation dot theta = - Gamma e1 omega"]
    nodeAdapt -- theta --> nodeCtrl
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow / Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Plant"
        a1["u measured input"] --> a2["Internal dynamics unknown state x"]
        a2 --> a3["y_p measured output"]
    end
    subgraph "Reconstruction Layer"
        b1["F s for u"] --> b3["Filtered regressor omega"]
        b2["F s for y_p"] --> b3
    end
    subgraph "Control Layer"
        b3 --> c1["Control Law u = theta trans omega"]
        c1 --> a1
    end
    subgraph "Tracking Layer"
        a3 --> d1["Subtract y_m"]
        d2["Reference r"] --> d3["Reference Model W_m"]
        d3 --> d1
        d1 --> d4["Augmented Error e1"]
        c1 --> d4
        d4 --> d5["Adaptation dot theta = - Gamma e1 omega"]
        d5 --> c1
    end
```

## 4.3 Stability Argument Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1["W_m is SPR"] --> n2["KYP Lemma gives P greater 0"]
    n2 --> n3["Define V = 1/2 x_m trans P x_m + 1/2 theta tilde trans Gamma inv theta tilde"]
    n3 --> n4["dot V = - 1/2 x_m trans Q x_m less or equal 0"]
    n4 --> n5["All signals bounded"]
    n4 --> n6["e1 in L2"]
    n5 --> n7["Barbalat Lemma"]
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8["Tracking e to 0 as t to infinity"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In all three figures, follow the bold blue arrow *reference $r$ → $y_m$* on one side and the green arrow *plant $y_p$ → $e$ → $\varepsilon_1$ → $\dot\theta$* on the other. The cross-connection $u = \theta^{\top}\omega$ closes the loop, exactly as the Lyapunov argument requires.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**State the Monopoli augmented error and explain its role in output-feedback MRAC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Monopoli augmented error is defined as

$$\varepsilon_1(t) = e(t) + \theta^{\top}(t)\,W_m(s)\,[\omega(t)] - \theta^{\top}(t)\,\omega(t)$$

where $e = y_p - y_m$ is the tracking error, $W_m(s)$ is the SPR reference model, and $\omega$ is the filtered regressor. **Role (1 mark):** it converts the *non-implementable* true error $e$ (which depends on $\theta^*$) into a signal that is *computable in real time* using measurable quantities. **SPR property (1 mark):** the auxiliary construction makes the error model $W_m(s)$ strictly positive real, so that the **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) lemma** (1 mark) supplies a Lyapunov function certifying boundedness and asymptotic tracking.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the SPR property of the reference model crucial in output-feedback MRAC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The SPR property guarantees the existence of a positive-definite $P = P^{\top} > 0$ satisfying the KYP identities

$$P A_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q < 0, \qquad P b_m = c_m$$

(2 marks). These identities are the *only* way to choose a Lyapunov function $V$ whose derivative $\dot V = -\tfrac12 x^{\top}Q x \le 0$ gives a rigorous stability proof for the adaptive law $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\varepsilon_1\omega$ (1 mark). If $W_m(s)$ is not SPR, the proof collapses and tracking cannot be assured.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, derive the output-feedback MRAC control law and the **augmented error equation** for the plant $W_p(s) = K_p\,Z_p(s)/R_p(s)$. Clearly state all assumptions. *\[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted\]*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using a Lyapunov function, prove that the parameter adaptation law

$$\dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega, \quad \Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$$

guarantees boundedness of all signals and asymptotic tracking $\lim_{t\to\infty} e(t) = 0$. *\[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper\]*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

1. **Assumptions (1 mark):** $W_p$ is SISO, minimum-phase, $\deg R_p = n$, $\deg Z_p = m$, $n^* = n-m$ known, sign of $K_p$ known, $W_m$ SPR with same $n^*$.
2. **Filtered regressor (1 mark):** Choose $F(s) = \lambda(s)/\Lambda(s)$, $\Lambda$ monic Hurwitz, $\deg(\Lambda) - \deg(\lambda) \ge n^*$. Set $\omega = [F(s)u,\; F(s)y_p]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
3. **Control law (1 mark):** $u = \theta^{\top}\omega$, $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$.
4. **Matching condition (1 mark):** There exists $\theta^*$ such that $W_p(s)[\theta^{*\top}\omega] = W_m(s)[r]$.
5. **Tracking error (1 mark):** $e = y_p - y_m = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$.
6. **Augmented error (1 mark):** $\varepsilon_1 = e + \theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega] - \theta^{\top}\omega$, hence $\varepsilon_1 = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$.
7. **Block diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.1 above.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

1. **State-space realisation of $W_m$ (1 mark):** $\dot x_m = A_m x_m + b_m(\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega)$, $\varepsilon_1 = c_m^{\top}x_m$.
2. **KYP lemma (2 marks):** Because $W_m$ is SPR, $\exists\,P = P^{\top} > 0$ with $PA_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q$ and $Pb_m = c_m$.
3. **Lyapunov function (1 mark):** $V = \tfrac12 x_m^{\top}P x_m + \tfrac12 \tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\tilde\theta \ge 0$.
4. **Derivative (1 mark):** $\dot V = \tfrac12 x_m^{\top}(A_m^{\top}P + PA_m)x_m + x_m^{\top}Pb_m\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega + \tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot{\tilde\theta} = -\tfrac12 x_m^{\top}Q x_m + \varepsilon_1\,\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega + \tilde\theta^{\top}\Gamma^{-1}\dot\theta$.
5. **Adaptive law substitution (1 mark):** With $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\varepsilon_1\omega$, the last two terms cancel, leaving $\dot V = -\tfrac12 x_m^{\top}Q x_m \le 0$.
6. **Stability conclusions (1 mark):** $x_m, \tilde\theta \in \mathcal{L}_\infty$; $\varepsilon_1 \in \mathcal{L}_2 \cap \mathcal{L}_\infty$; by Barbalat's Lemma, $\varepsilon_1 \to 0$ and hence $e \to 0$ asymptotically.

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of filtered regressors in output-feedback MRAC. Why is the choice $F(s) = \lambda(s)/\Lambda(s)$ with $\deg(\Lambda)-\deg(\lambda) \ge n^*$ necessary? *\[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted\]*

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the plant $W_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s + a}$ with $K_p > 0$ and $a$ unknown, design an output-feedback MRAC to track the reference model $W_m(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$. Write the regressor, the control law, the augmented error, and the adaptation law. Verify that $W_m(s)$ is SPR. *\[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper\]*

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

1. **Regressors in state-feedback vs output-feedback (2 marks):** In state feedback we use $x$; in output feedback we use $\omega = [F(s)u,\; F(s)y_p]^{\top}$ so that *every* signal in the regressor is measurable.
2. **Filter conditions (2 marks):** $F(s)$ must be (i) proper, (ii) stable, (iii) strictly proper, and (iv) of relative degree $\ge n^*$, so that $F(s)W_m^{-1}(s)$ is proper and the Bezout / Diophantine equation has a solution.
3. **Effect on closed loop (2 marks):** With this choice the matching equation has a solution $\theta^*$, and the SPR property of $W_m$ is preserved in the augmented error system.
4. **Engineering meaning (1 mark):** $F$ is essentially a low-pass pre-filter that mimics the unmeasured state — it converts a hidden-state control problem into a measurable-regressor adaptive problem.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

1. **Regressors (1 mark):** $n = 1$, $n^* = 1$. Choose $F(s) = 1/(s+1)$, so $\omega = [\omega_1, \omega_2]^{\top}$ with $\omega_1 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}[u]$, $\omega_2 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}[y_p]$.
2. **Control law (1 mark):** $u = \theta_1\omega_1 + \theta_2\omega_2$.
3. **Augmented error (2 marks):** $\varepsilon_1 = e + \theta_1\,\dfrac{1}{s+1}[\omega_1] + \theta_2\,\dfrac{1}{s+1}[\omega_2] - \theta_1\omega_1 - \theta_2\omega_2$.
4. **Adaptation law (1 mark):** $\dot\theta_1 = -\gamma_1\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega_1$, $\dot\theta_2 = -\gamma_2\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega_2$, with $\gamma_1,\gamma_2 > 0$.
5. **SPR verification (2 marks):** $W_m(s) = 1/(s+1)$ has state-space form $A_m = -1$, $b_m = 1$, $c_m = 1$. Choosing $P = 1$ gives $PA_m + A_m^{\top}P = -2 < 0$ and $Pb_m = 1 = c_m$. All KYP conditions hold, so $W_m$ is strictly positive real.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $K_p$:** Many students omit $\text{sign}(K_p)$ in the adaptive law. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for not stating the known-sign assumption.
> 2. **Using the *true* error $e$ in the adaptive law instead of the *augmented* error $\varepsilon_1$:** This is the single most common mistake in Module 4; expect a **3-mark** deduction.
> 3. **Choosing a non-SPR $W_m(s)$:** If you pick $W_m(s) = 1/s$ or $1/s^2$, the KYP lemma fails and stability cannot be proved — the answer is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping the state-space realisation:** Examiners give partial credit only when the pair $(A_m, b_m, c_m^{\top})$ is explicitly written.
> 5. **Confusing $\theta$ with $\theta^*$:** Always denote the *ideal* parameter as $\theta^*$ and the *adapted* parameter as $\theta(t)$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Output Feedback MRAC** = adaptive control that uses only $y_p$ (not the full state $x$).
- **Regressors** are *filtered* input–output vectors: $\omega = [F(s)u,\; F(s)y_p]^{\top}$, with $\deg(\Lambda) - \deg(\lambda) \ge n^*$.
- **Control law:** $u = \theta^{\top}\omega$.
- **Fundamental error equation:** $e = W_m(s)[\tilde\theta^{\top}\omega]$.
- **Augmented (Monopoli) error:**
  $$\varepsilon_1 = e + \theta^{\top}W_m(s)[\omega] - \theta^{\top}\omega$$
- **Adaptive law:** $\dot\theta = -\Gamma\,\varepsilon_1\,\omega$, with $\Gamma = \Gamma^{\top} > 0$ and $\sigma$-modification for robustness.
- **SPR condition on $W_m(s)$** is mandatory for the KYP lemma and the Lyapunov proof.
- **KYP identities:** $PA_m + A_m^{\top}P = -Q < 0$ and $Pb_m = c_m$ guarantee the existence of the Lyapunov function.
- **Stability result:** $V \ge 0$, $\dot V = -\tfrac12 x_m^{\top}Q x_m \le 0$ ⇒ all signals bounded and $e \to 0$ by Barbalat's lemma.
- **Plant assumptions:** minimum-phase, known $n^*$, known sign of $K_p$.
- **Practical add-ons:** $\sigma$-modification, projection, $e_1$-modification to handle bounded disturbances and unmodelled dynamics.
- **Variants to remember:** MRAC with *parallel feedforward compensator* (for non-minimum-phase plants), *command governor*, *Nussbaum-type* high-gain output feedback for relative-degree 1 systems.
- **Key references on the topic** (do **not** attribute to specific editions in the answer book): Åström & Wittenmark (*Adaptive Control*); Ioannou & Sun (*Robust Adaptive Control*); Narendra & Annaswamy (*Stable Adaptive Systems*); Monopoli's 1974 augmented-error paper.
- **Most-likely board question patterns:** (i) derive the augmented error and adaptive law, (ii) prove stability using KYP, (iii) design a controller for a given first/second-order plant, (iv) state and apply the Barbalat argument.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relations Between MRAS and STR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT868 - ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relations Between MRAS and STR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions

### Model Reference Adaptive System (MRAS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MRAS** is an adaptive control scheme in which the performance specifications are expressed through a **reference model** that describes the desired closed-loop input-output behaviour. The controller parameters are adjusted so that the actual plant output tracks the output of this fixed reference model, with the adaptation driven by the **generalized output error** $e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$.

Mathematically, the structure is governed by:

$$y_m(t) = W_m(s)\, r(t)$$

$$y_p(t) = W_p(s,\hat{\theta}(t))\, r(t)$$

$$e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t) \;\longrightarrow\; \hat{\theta}(t) \text{ (adaptation law)}$$

### Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STR** is an adaptive controller that performs two on-line tasks simultaneously: (i) a **recursive parameter estimator** identifies an explicit parametric model of the plant, and (ii) a **control-design block** uses the estimated parameters and the desired closed-loop specifications to recompute the controller gains. The implicit reference behaviour is embedded inside the design step.

Mathematically:

$$\hat{\theta}(t) \;\xrightarrow{\text{Design block}}\; \text{Controller } C(s,\hat{\theta})$$

$$u(t) = C(s,\hat{\theta})\, e_c(t)$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of **MRAS** like a **student copying a master calligrapher**: the student keeps comparing his handwriting (`$y_p$`) against the master's sample (`$y_m$`), and continuously corrects his grip (parameters $\hat{\theta}$) to reduce the visible difference. He never asks "what ink should I use?"—he just keeps reducing the error he sees.

Think of **STR** like a **GPS-enabled autonomous car**: it constantly re-measures the road (estimates the plant model), then recomputes the steering angle that will deliver the user-requested destination (desired closed-loop poles). It does **not** compare its trajectory to a reference car; it builds the reference implicitly from a destination description.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Both schemes are two-loop adaptive architectures, but **MRAS drives adaptation from an output error**, while **STR drives adaptation from a prediction error (parameter estimator)** and produces a controller through a separate design step.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Metrics & Standard Notations

- **$W_m(s)$** — reference-model transfer function (fixed, stable, minimum phase). Dimensionless.
- **$W_p(s)$** — actual plant transfer function with parameter vector $\theta$.
- **$\hat{\theta}(t)$** — adjustable parameter vector at time $t$. Dimensionless (normalised).
- **$e(t)$** — adaptation (output) error, units same as $y(t)$.
- **Adaptation gain** $\gamma$ — dimensionless scalar weighting the rate of parameter update.
- **Sampling period** $T_s$ — in seconds, governs the discrete-time implementation of STR.
- **Persistent Excitation (PE) condition** — qualitative requirement on the regressor for convergence.
- **Certainty Equivalence (CE)** — design principle underlying STR.
- **MIT Rule** — original gradient-descent adaptation law used in early MRAS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition

### MRAS — Three Functional Blocks

1. **Reference Model $W_m(s)$**
   - Pre-defined stable plant emulating the desired behaviour.
   - Defines the *implicit performance benchmark*.
2. **Plant + Adjustable Controller (Inner Loop)**
   - Composed transfer function $W_p(s,\hat{\theta})$ must match $W_m(s)$.
   - Operates on the actual plant with current parameter guess.
3. **Adaptation Mechanism (Outer Loop)**
   - Maps the generalized error $e(t)$ to a parameter update rule.
   - Classical forms: MIT rule, Lyapunov-based MRAC, Gradient / $e$-modification.

### STR — Two Functional Blocks

1. **Recursive Parameter Estimator**
   - Most commonly: **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)**, **Extended Least Squares (ELS)**, or **Recursive Maximum Likelihood (RML)**.
   - Produces $\hat{\theta}(k)$ each sampling instant $kT_s$.
2. **Controller Re-Design (Certainty-Equivalent Block)**
   - At each $kT_s$, the design equation treats $\hat{\theta}$ as if it were the true $\theta$.
   - Generates controller parameters via pole-placement, minimum-variance, or LQG design.

> [!NOTE]
> The STR *embeds* the reference model into the controller design equation, whereas the MRAS *externalises* it as a separately implemented block.

## 2.2 Why and How the Two Approaches Coexist

Both MRAS and STR were developed in parallel to address the same fundamental problem: **control of plants with unknown or slowly time-varying parameters**. Their historical development is summarised below:

| Aspect | MRAS (1958–1965) | STR (1973) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | MIT Instrumentation Lab (Whitaker, Osburn, Kezer) | Lund Institute of Technology (Åström & Wittenmark) |
| Performance spec. | Explicit reference model | Implicit via pole-placement or MV law |
| Adaptation driver | Output error $e = y_p - y_m$ | Prediction / parameter error |
| Inner loop | Closed-loop with adjustable $C(s,\hat{\theta})$ | Closed-loop with re-tuned $C(s,\hat{\theta})$ |
| Outer loop | Direct parameter adjustment from $e$ | Two-step: estimate, then design |

The unification of these approaches was carried out in a rigorous manner by **Egardt (1979)** and **Landau (1979)**, who showed that under appropriate assumptions the two schemes become **mathematically equivalent**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all equations necessary for solving KTU exam problems on the relation between MRAS and STR. Master these expressions.

| Concept | Equation | Description / KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| MRAS reference model | $A_m(s)\, y_m = B_m(s)\, r$ | Polynomial form of desired closed-loop response |
| Plant parametric model | $A(s,\theta)\, y = B(s,\theta)\, u$ | STR inner estimator target |
| MIT rule (continuous MRAS) | $\dfrac{d\hat{\theta}}{dt} = -\gamma\, e\, \dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$ | Classical gradient MRAS update |
| Lyapunov-based MRAC | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma\, e\, \zeta$ with $\Gamma > 0$ | Stable MRAS update |
| RLS estimator (STR) | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,\varepsilon(k)$ | Recursive parameter update |
| RLS gain | $K(k) = P(k-1)\,\phi(k)\,[\lambda + \phi^T P(k-1)\phi(k)]^{-1}$ | Covariance update |
| Prediction error | $\varepsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)$ | Drives STR estimator |
| Certainty Equivalence design | $C(s,\hat{\theta}) \;\Rightarrow\; A(s,\hat{\theta})\,y + B(s,\hat{\theta})\,u = A_m(s)\,y_m$ | STR pole-placement condition |
| Egardt equivalence | MRAS with RLS + pole-placement $\equiv$ STR with implicit $A_m$ | The unifying theorem |
| Landau unified law | $\dot{\hat{\theta}} = -\Gamma\, \Phi\, e_p$ where $\Phi$ is regressor, $e_p$ prediction error | Common framework |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace flight control**: MRAS is used when the desired handling qualities are codified in a fixed reference model (e.g., flying qualities specifications).
- **Process control (paper, chemical, steel mills)**: STR dominates because parameters drift slowly and recursive identification is computationally attractive.
- **Robotics**: Joint-level adaptive position controllers often employ MRAS with a reference trajectory generator.
- **Automotive**: Engine idle-speed and adaptive cruise controllers frequently use STR architectures for their robustness to load variation.
- **Power systems**: Damping controllers for low-frequency oscillations adopt MRAS when the desired damping ratio is expressed as a reference transfer function.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setting Up the Equivalence Framework

Consider a SISO discrete-time plant described by the ARX model used in STR:

$$A(q^{-1})\, y(k) = B(q^{-1})\, u(k-1) + \xi(k)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are polynomials in the backward-shift operator $q^{-1}$, and $\xi(k)$ is a stochastic disturbance.

The corresponding MRAS reference model for a desired closed-loop behaviour is:

$$A_m(q^{-1})\, y_m(k) = B_m(q^{-1})\, r(k-1)$$

The regulator is to be designed so that the closed-loop transfer function matches $W_m(q^{-1}) = B_m/A_m$.

## 3.2 STR Re-Design Equation (Certainty-Equivalent Pole Placement)

Suppose the chosen control law has the form of a *general linear regulator*:

$$R(q^{-1})\, u(k) = T(q^{-1})\, r(k) - S(q^{-1})\, y(k)$$

Substituting this control into the plant equation and equating the resulting closed-loop denominator to $A_m(q^{-1})$ gives the **Diophantine equation** central to STR:

$$A(q^{-1})\,R(q^{-1}) + B(q^{-1})\,S(q^{-1}) = A_m(q^{-1})\, P(q^{-1})$$

where $P(q^{-1})$ is an observer polynomial. Under certainty equivalence, the unknown coefficients of $A$ and $B$ are replaced by their on-line estimates $\hat{A}$, $\hat{B}$, and the regulator polynomials $R$, $S$ are solved each sample from:

$$\hat{A}(q^{-1})\,R(q^{-1}) + \hat{B}(q^{-1})\,S(q^{-1}) = A_m(q^{-1})\, P(q^{-1})$$

This equation is the **algebraic carrier of the implicit reference model** of the STR.

## 3.3 MRAS Adaptation Law from the Same Cost

Define the output error:

$$e(k) = y(k) - y_m(k)$$

A quadratic cost function commonly minimised in MRAS-STR unification is:

$$J(\hat{\theta}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \mathbb{E}[\,e(k)^2\,]$$

Applying the steepest-descent update to this cost with respect to the adjustable parameter vector $\hat{\theta}$ yields:

$$\hat{\theta}(k+1) = \hat{\theta}(k) - \gamma(k)\, \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{\theta}}$$

## 3.4 Showing the Equivalence (Egardt's Result)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Egardt's theorem (1979):** Under (i) the plant is minimum-phase, (ii) the regressor $\phi(k)$ is persistently exciting, and (iii) the adaptation gain $\gamma(k) \to 0$ (vanishing gain), an MRAS employing a normalised MIT-type rule with a reference-model polynomial $A_m$ is **asymptotically equivalent** to an STR performing recursive least-squares estimation combined with pole-placement against the same $A_m$.

We derive the structural identity in steps.

**Step 1.** Express the MRAS adjustment error in terms of parameter deviation.

Let $\theta^\star$ be the *true* plant parameter vector and define $\tilde{\theta} = \hat{\theta} - \theta^\star$. Linearising the closed-loop plant around $\theta^\star$ gives:

$$e(k) = -\phi^T(k)\, \tilde{\theta}(k) + \text{higher-order terms}$$

where $\phi(k)$ is the regressor vector $[-y(k-1), \ldots, -y(k-n_a), u(k-1), \ldots, u(k-n_b)]^T$.

**Step 2.** Apply the MIT rule to the linearised error.

$$\hat{\theta}(k+1) = \hat{\theta}(k) - \gamma\, e(k)\, \frac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}}\bigg|_{\hat{\theta}=\theta^\star}$$

Using the linearised form $e \approx -\phi^T \tilde{\theta}$ and noting $\dfrac{\partial e}{\partial \hat{\theta}} = -\phi$ (with appropriate sign convention):

$$\tilde{\theta}(k+1) = \tilde{\theta}(k) - \gamma\, \phi(k)\, \phi^T(k)\, \tilde{\theta}(k)$$

**Step 3.** Compare with RLS parameter update.

The RLS algorithm for the ARX model can be written as:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,\varepsilon(k)$$

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k)\,P(k-1)\,\phi(k)}$$

with prediction error $\varepsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^T(k)\,\hat{\theta}(k-1)$.

**Step 4.** Identify the correspondence.

Using the **Egardt simplification**, when the covariance $P(k)$ is approximately proportional to the identity $\gamma(k)\, I$ with a *time-varying* $\gamma(k) = \beta / \bigl[\phi^T(k)\,\phi(k) + \beta\bigr]$, the RLS update reduces to:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + \gamma(k)\, \phi(k)\,\varepsilon(k)$$

Comparing with Step 2, since $\varepsilon(k) = -\phi^T(k)\,\tilde{\theta}(k-1)$:

$$\tilde{\theta}(k) = \tilde{\theta}(k-1) - \gamma(k)\, \phi(k)\,\phi^T(k)\,\tilde{\theta}(k-1)$$

This is **identical in form** to the MRAS MIT update. The instantaneous gain $\gamma(k) = \beta/[\phi^T\phi + \beta]$ matches the **normalised MIT rule**:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) - \frac{\gamma_0\, e(k)\, \zeta(k)}{1 + \gamma_0\, \zeta^T(k)\,\zeta(k)}$$

where $\zeta(k)$ is the sensitivity function $\partial y_p/\partial \hat{\theta}$.

**Step 5.** Final algebraic statement.

Therefore, defining the *normalised error* $\bar{e}(k) = e(k)/[1 + \phi^T(k)\,\phi(k)]$, the MRAS update becomes:

$$\hat{\theta}(k+1) = \hat{\theta}(k) - \gamma_0\, \bar{e}(k)\, \phi(k)$$

which coincides term-by-term with the recursive least-squares STR update under normalised gain.

## 3.5 Landau's Unified Adaptive Law

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Landau (1979)** demonstrated that both MRAS and STR can be derived from a single *posteriori* adaptive law operating on an extended regressor that includes both output error and prediction error.

The unified update has the form:

$$\dot{\hat{\theta}}(t) = -\Gamma\, \Phi(t)\, \eta(t)$$

where $\eta(t)$ is the composite adaptation error:

$$\eta(t) = \alpha\, e(t) + (1-\alpha)\, \varepsilon(t), \quad \alpha \in [0,1]$$

- $\alpha = 1$ recovers **classical MRAS** (driven purely by output error).
- $\alpha = 0$ recovers **STR with re-design** (driven purely by prediction error).
- $\alpha \in (0,1)$ yields a **mixed scheme** with combined convergence properties.

This single equation is the strongest formal evidence that MRAS and STR are *two endpoints of a continuous spectrum* of adaptive controllers rather than fundamentally distinct methodologies.

## 3.6 Convergence Conditions

For the equivalence to hold globally (not just locally) the following **sufficient conditions** are required:

1. The plant is minimum-phase (no unstable zeros-cancellations).
2. The reference model $W_m(s)$ is strictly positive real (SPR) or the error filtering $L(s)$ is chosen to make the augmented plant SPR.
3. The regressor $\phi(k)$ is persistently exciting (PE) of order $\geq \dim(\theta)$.
4. The adaptation gain $\gamma$ satisfies the **persistency condition** $\sum_k \gamma(k) = \infty$ and $\sum_k \gamma^2(k) < \infty$.
5. Initial parameter estimate $\hat{\theta}(0)$ lies in a known convex region of attraction.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unified Architecture Showing Both MRAS and STR

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how the two adaptive schemes share a common backbone and how the Egardt–Landau bridge connects them through the *adaptive law* and the *re-design equation*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r] --> RM[Reference Model W_m]
    RM --> YM[y_m]
    RM -. implicit .-> DSGN[Design Equation A_m]

    R --> P[Plant W_p]
    P --> YP[y_p]
    YP --> E[Error e = y_p - y_m]
    YM --> E
    E --> ADAPT[Adaptation Mechanism]

    P --> EST[Recursive Parameter Estimator RLS]
    EST --> THETA[Parameter Estimate theta_hat]
    THETA --> DSGN
    DSGN --> CTRL[Controller C theta_hat]
    CTRL --> P

    ADAPT -. Egardt-Landau bridge .- EST
    THETA -. Certainty equivalence .- CTRL

    classDef refbox fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef plantbox fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef adaptbox fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef bridgebox fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000

    class R,RM,YM refbox
    class P,YP,E,DSGN,CTRL plantbox
    class ADAPT,EST,THETA adaptbox
```

## 4.2 MRAS Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Command r] --> RM[Reference Model W_m]
    RM --> YM[y_m]
    R --> CL[Closed-loop inner plant W_p with adjustable C]
    CL --> YP[y_p]
    YP --> E[Subtractor]
    YM --> E
    E --> AM[Adaptation Mechanism MIT or Lyapunov]
    AM --> THETA[theta_hat]
    THETA --> CL

    classDef mras fill:#e8f8f5,stroke:#117864,color:#000
    class R,RM,YM,CL,YP,E,AM,THETA mras
```

## 4.3 STR Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference r] --> CL[Closed-loop with re-tuned controller C theta_hat]
    CL --> Y[y]
    Y --> EST[Recursive Estimator RLS / ELS]
    EST --> THETA[theta_hat]
    THETA --> DSGN[Design Equation pole placement / MV]
    DSGN --> CTRL[Controller Polynomials R and S]
    CTRL --> CL

    classDef str fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    class R,CL,Y,EST,THETA,DSGN,CTRL str
```

## 4.4 Mapping Between MRAS Components and STR Components

| MRAS element | STR counterpart | Connection through Egardt-Landau theorem |
|---|---|---|
| Reference model $W_m$ | Design equation with desired $A_m$ | Same polynomial $A_m$ appears in both |
| Inner-loop adjustable $C$ | Re-designed $R, S$ polynomials | Both solved via Diophantine equation |
| Output error $e = y_p - y_m$ | Prediction error $\varepsilon = y - \phi^T \hat{\theta}$ | Linked by the linearised relation $e \approx -\phi^T \tilde{\theta}$ |
| MIT / Lyapunov rule | Normalised RLS update | Equivalent under normalised gain |
| $\hat{\theta}$ dynamics | $\hat{\theta}$ dynamics | Identical recursive equation |
